rows.title: AI Diplomacy
rows.summary: Open-source project (GoodStartLabs, ~684 stars) where seven frontier LLMs (Claude, GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek, Llama, Qwen, Mistral, etc.) each control a power in the board game Diplomacy, negotiating, allying and betraying each other; transcripts were widely shared as public evidence of emergent LLM negotiation/deception behavior.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is used for (1) giving several Diplomacy powers open-weight models (Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-235B, DeepSeek-Prover, Mistral-Small, Phi-4, Gemma-3, incl. ':free' tier variants) that compete head-to-head in the same game against proprietary models called via native OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini APIs; (2) as the default backend for the 'formatter' model (config.py) that converts each power's free-text LLM output into the structured JSON orders actually submitted to the game engine; (3) as the default backend for the narrative/phase-summary model; and (4) as the LLM judge in analyze_game_moments.py that auto-detects betrayals/lies/collaborations for highlight content.
rows.importance: 684 GitHub stars, actively maintained, widely shared on AI-Twitter/media as one of the most-cited public demos of multi-model LLM negotiation and betrayal behavior; no peer review but high public influence on discourse about LLM social/strategic behavior.
rows.one_line_fix: Set provider={"order"/"only": [...], "quantizations": [...], "require_parameters": true, "data_collection": "deny"} on every OpenRouterClient/RequestsOpenAIClient call (especially the default formatter/narrative models in config.py and every open-weight power model), and log the response's actual `provider` field + generation id into llm_responses.csv alongside model_name.
rows.mistakes: ai_diplomacy/clients.py:1186-1192 (OpenRouterClient.generate_response) sends only model/messages/max_tokens/temperature — no `provider` dict anywhere in the class — so open-weight powers (e.g. README.md:376 'ITALY': 'openrouter-meta-llama/llama-4-maverick') are load-balanced across providers/quantizations run-to-run (M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic routing). No require_parameters is set anywhere in the repo (grep confirms zero non-commented hits), so the temperature sampling param can be silently dropped by whichever backend is picked (M2). ai_diplomacy/utils.py:367-390 (log_llm_response) writes only {timestamp, model, power, phase, response_type, raw_input, raw_response, success} to CSV — the served provider and generation id are never recorded, so which weights actually produced any game's orders is unrecoverable after the fact (M4). Some defaults are bare/undated, e.g. analyze_game_moments.py:65 'openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview' with no snapshot date (M6). Most importantly, the project's entire premise is head-to-head comparison of named models' negotiation/betrayal behavior — README.md:370-380 assigns proprietary models (o3, claude-opus-4-20250514, gpt-4.1, gemini-2.5-pro-preview) and OpenRouter open-weight models (llama-4-maverick, gemini-2.5-flash-preview-05-20) to different powers in the *same* game. The proprietary legs run through pinned, single-provider native APIs while the open-weight legs run through completely unpinned OpenRouter — an asymmetric confound (M8) that directly threatens any 'model X betrayed/won more than model Y' claim drawn from the publicly shared transcripts. Nothing in README.md or the code discloses this routing/quantization risk to readers of the transcripts (M10); the sole sign the authors ever considered it is one commented-out provider pin for a single Qwen/Cerebras experiment (ai_diplomacy/clients.py:1361-1365, `"order": ["Cerebras"], "allow_fallbacks": False`) that was never enabled and never generalized to any other model or call site, including the formatter/judge calls that convert free text into actual game orders.
rows.evidence: ai_diplomacy/clients.py:1154-1173 (OpenRouterClient.__init__, base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"); clients.py:1186-1192 (request body has only model/messages/max_tokens/temperature, no provider dict); clients.py:1361-1365 (commented-out `payload["provider"] = {"order": ["Cerebras"], "allow_fallbacks": False}` — disabled); ai_diplomacy/utils.py:367-390 (CSV fieldnames = [timestamp, model, power, phase, response_type, raw_input, raw_response, success] — no provider/generation_id field); config.py:17-18 (`AI_DIPLOMACY_NARRATIVE_MODEL`/`AI_DIPLOMACY_FORMATTER_MODEL` default to "openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview-05-20"); ai_diplomacy/formatter.py:65-86 (formatter_client.generate_response(...) parses free text into game-engine JSON orders, temperature=0 but no response_format/require_parameters); analyze_game_moments.py:65 (`model_name: str = "openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview"`, bare/undated, used for betrayal/lie detection); README.md:370-380 (default assign_models_to_powers mixes o3/claude-opus-4-20250514/gpt-4.1/gemini-2.5-pro-preview with openrouter-meta-llama/llama-4-maverick and openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview-05-20 in one game); ai_diplomacy/utils.py:58-95 (assign_models_to_powers docstring lists ':free' tier OpenRouter models — e.g. openrouter-meta-llama/llama-4-maverick:free — as supported).
rows.audit_notes: The formatter model is the most interesting/load-bearing finding beyond typical "judge" risk: it isn't just a downstream analysis step, it's the piece that converts each power's free-text LLM output into the JSON that gets submitted to the game engine as that power's actual move (ai_diplomacy/formatter.py:82-102, called every phase for every power by default via OpenRouter Gemini Flash). So provider/quantization drift there could causally change what orders a power submits, invisibly, in every published game transcript. Separately, README.md:349 documents `--seed_base` as "enabling reproducible batches," but lm_game.py:127-131 explicitly labels it "RNG seed placeholder for compatibility with experiment_runner. Currently unused" — a dead/misleading reproducibility claim, though I did not count it as M7 since no API `seed` parameter is ever actually sent to any provider (the `generate_random_seed()` helper injects noise text into the system prompt instead, for output diversity, not determinism).
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Fresh `git clone --depth 1` of https://github.com/GoodStartLabs/AI_Diplomacy.git (HEAD 736a0a9, pushed 2026-06-01, GitHub API confirms 684 stars exactly matching the "importance" claim) verifies every cited evidence location, essentially verbatim (line numbers off by ≤1-15 due to blob differences, never off by content):

1. **clients.py OpenRouterClient.generate_response** (class starts line 1154, the create() call at 1187): confirmed body is exactly `model=`, `messages=`, `max_tokens=self.max_tokens`, `temperature=temperature` — no `provider=`/`extra_body` anywhere in the class. Repo-wide `grep -rn '"provider"|require_parameters|response_format|extra_body|json_schema'` across all .py files returns exactly ONE hit: the commented-out `#payload["provider"] = {"order": ["Cerebras"], "allow_fallbacks": False}` block at clients.py:1361-1365, inside `RequestsOpenAIClient` (a *different* client than `OpenRouterClient`), gated on `qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b` and disabled. This fully confirms M1/M2/M3 for the open-weight legs and confirms `safeguards_present: []` is accurate — the one near-miss was never activated or generalized.

2. **utils.py:369** (`log_llm_response`) confirmed fieldnames exactly `["timestamp","model","power","phase","response_type","raw_input","raw_response","success"]` — no provider/generation_id field. Materially worse than it looks: the OpenAI-compatible response object returned by `self.client.chat.completions.create()` in OpenRouterClient *does* carry `.id` and `.model` fields (standard OpenRouter/OpenAI chat-completion schema), but the code discards everything except `.choices[0].message.content.strip()` before it ever reaches the logger. The provenance data was available and thrown away, not merely never requested. M4 confirmed and if anything understated.

3. **config.py:17-18** confirmed verbatim: both `AI_DIPLOMACY_NARRATIVE_MODEL` and `AI_DIPLOMACY_FORMATTER_MODEL` default to `"openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview-05-20"`.

4. **formatter.py** confirmed: `format_with_gemini_flash` loads the formatter client via `load_model_client(model_name)` (which routes any "openrouter"-containing name to `OpenRouterClient`), calls `generate_response(..., temperature=0, ...)` with no response_format/require_parameters, and the returned text is what becomes the power's submitted game order. This substantiates `critical_route: true` — quantization/provider drift on this call can causally change published game outcomes, not just downstream commentary.

5. **analyze_game_moments.py:65** confirmed verbatim bare/undated `"openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview"` (note: this is a *different, even less pinned* string than config.py's dated `-05-20` variant — the auditor cited the correct, genuinely undated instance for M6, showing care rather than sloppiness).

6. **README.md:369-379** (cited as 370-380) confirmed verbatim: `assign_models_to_powers()` example assigns pinned/native-API proprietary models (`o3`, `gpt-4.1`, `gemini-2.5-pro-preview-05-06`, `claude-opus-4-20250514`) alongside unpinned OpenRouter open-weight models (`openrouter-meta-llama/llama-4-maverick`, `openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview-05-20`) to different powers in one game. This is the strongest finding — it's a direct, in-repo, in-docs confound (M8) for exactly the "model X betrayed/won more" claims that made the project's transcripts widely shared.

7. **utils.py:58-69** docstring confirmed listing `:free`-tier OpenRouter models (`llama-4-maverick:free`, `phi-4-reasoning-plus:free`, `deepseek-prover-v2:free`, `gemma-3-12b-it:free`) as supported.

8. **M10**: no mention of "quantiz", "reproducib", "provider rout", "load-balanc", or "non-determin" anywhere in README.md except the `--seed_base` row claiming "enabling reproducible batches" — which is independently confirmed dead/misleading: `lm_game.py:127-131` labels it "RNG seed placeholder for compatibility with experiment_runner. Currently unused," and `generate_random_seed()` only injects noise text into the system prompt (never an API `seed` param — confirmed via repo-wide grep, zero non-`random_seed`/`seed_base` hits on `"seed"`). This *reinforces* M10 (a false reproducibility claim is arguably worse than silence) and confirms the audit_notes' correct exclusion of M7.

Sanity checks specifically requested:
- **No proprietary model wrongly flagged M1/M3/M8 individually** — the proprietary powers (o3, claude-opus-4, gpt-4.1, gemini-2.5-pro-preview) go through native single-provider SDKs (ClaudeClient/OpenAIClient/GeminiClient), so M1/M3 correctly don't apply to those legs standalone; M8 is correctly applied at the *game/comparison* level, per the taxonomy's own LEGIT carve-out language.
- **M9 (judge on unconstrained route)**: analyze_game_moments.py's judge calls `self.client.generate_response(prompt)` then `json.loads(response)` on a plain-text completion — repo-wide grep confirms `response_format`/`json_schema` is never used anywhere in the codebase. Since M9 by definition requires response_format/schema usage, it is correctly *excluded* — the judge is unconstrained but not via the M9 mechanism (it's really an M2/M8-adjacent risk already captured).
- **Provider actually pinned/logged anywhere (would make it safe)?** No — confirmed zero non-commented `provider=`/`require_parameters` hits repo-wide, and CSV schema confirmed to omit provider/generation_id.
- **Critical route real?** Yes — formatter.py is confirmed on the order-submission path, not just an analysis afterthought.

One minor, non-verdict-changing observation: no `data_collection` setting exists anywhere either (mechanically implying M5's "left allow" applies), and README.md documents an `openai-requests:model@base_url` syntax that could point a second client class (with its own now-dead provider-pin logic) at OpenRouter, a latent M11-style dual-path risk — but I found no evidence either is exercised in any of the repo's actual default configs, so I did not add M5/M11 to the mistake set; they're privacy/latent-risk notes, not confirmed corruption channels, and the original 7-item set is already well-evidenced without them. The verdict as given is accurate and, if anything, conservative.

rows.title: AI Induced Psychosis: A shallow investigation (Tim Hua)
rows.summary: A red-teaming pipeline where one LLM roleplays an escalating psychotic user against a target model for N turns while a third LLM grades each target response on pushback/delusion-validation scales; results are aggregated into CSVs and plotted to compare psychosis-inducing tendencies across ~9 frontier/open-weight models for a widely-read Alignment Forum/LessWrong post.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is the inference backend (via inspect_ai's `get_model()`) for the majority of target models under test (grok-3, grok-4, gemini-2.5-flash/pro, kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1-0528, gpt-oss-20b) and for both the red-teaming "psychotic user" model and the LLM grader (default `openrouter/x-ai/grok-4` or `grok-3`), whose outputs are parsed into the numeric ratings (pushback score, delusion-confirmation score, therapeutic-collaboration scores, etc.) that drive every comparative chart in the post.
rows.importance: 52 GitHub stars (well above typical single-post LW/AF repos); post is a widely-discussed early empirical treatment of "AI psychosis," a topic with major 2025-2026 mainstream media attention; systematically compares 9 frontier/open-weight models (Grok, Gemini, Kimi, DeepSeek, GPT-OSS, GPT-5, Claude) on psychosis-validating behavior.
rows.evidence: redteaming_systematic.py:34-46 DEFAULT_MODELS uses bare undated slugs ('openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2', 'openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat', 'openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528', 'openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-20b', 'openrouter/x-ai/grok-3', 'openrouter/x-ai/grok-4', 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-flash/pro') passed straight into inspect_ai's get_model() at redteaming_systematic.py:351-354 (`self.target_model=get_model(target_model_name)`, `self.grader_model=get_model(grader_model_name)`) with zero model_args. Fetched inspect_ai's OpenRouterAPI provider source (github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai, src/inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py): extra_body.provider/models/transforms are only added if the caller passes a `provider=` model_arg to get_model() -- none is passed anywhere in this repo (grep across all .py/.md/.toml for 'provider=', 'require_parameters', 'quantizations', 'data_collection', 'order', 'only', 'sort:' returned zero hits). result_grades/redteaming_results_*.csv columns (verified by reading an actual output file) are turn_number/.../target_model/redteam_model/grader_model/patient_name/timestamps -- no provider or generation-id column, and inspect_ai's ModelOutput (checked _model_output.py) does not surface OpenRouter's response-level provider field either, so backend provenance is unrecoverable after the fact. WebFetched the live post: it states "I accessed chatgpt-4o-latest and gpt-5 using the OpenAI API, Claude 4 Sonnet using the Anthropic API, and the remaining models through OpenRouter" and separately "I use the default API settings in the InspectAI package" as a caveat -- confirming both the OpenRouter/direct-API split and that no provider config was ever set.
rows.mistakes: Critical route: OpenRouter-served completions ARE the data -- target-model comparisons (pushback/delusion-validation charts) and the grader's numeric scores drive the post's headline model-ranking claims. M1/M3: kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1-0528 and gpt-oss-20b are all multi-provider on OpenRouter with materially different quantizations across backends, yet every call goes out with default probabilistic load-balanced routing and no quant floor -- model-to-model and run-to-run comparisons of "psychosis-inducing behavior" are confounded by whichever backend/quant served each turn. M4: no provider or generation-id is ever recorded (confirmed against real CSV columns and the ChatMessage serialization in save_conversation), so which backend produced any given rating is unrecoverable, threatening every reported comparison. M5: data_collection is never set to 'deny', so prompts (detailed psychotic-delusion roleplay) default to OpenRouter's 'allow' policy on whichever provider serves them. M6: several DEFAULT_MODELS slugs are bare/undated (kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, grok-3, grok-4, gemini-2.5-flash/pro), so a rerun later may silently hit a different checkpoint than the one graded in the post. M8: the post's central charts (per its own text) compare these unpinned open-weight/OpenRouter-routed models directly against models called via effectively single-served direct APIs (chatgpt-4o-latest/gpt-5 via OpenAI API, claude-sonnet-4 via Anthropic API), mixing fully-pinned and completely-unpinned inference paths under one "model performance" ranking. Partial credit on disclosure: the post does name OpenRouter as the backend and flags "I use the default API settings in the InspectAI package" as a caveat, clearing the bare M10 threshold of disclosing the inference stack -- but never connects this to quantization/provider-drift as a threat to the cross-model rankings, hence "aware_partial" not "aware_and_handled." M2/M7/M9/M11/M12 do not apply: no sampling params (temperature/top_p/seed) are set anywhere (grep confirmed empty), the grader parses free-text JSON via json.loads() rather than response_format/schema so nothing is silently dropped, and no :floor/:nitro price-sort suffixes are used. Fun/telling detail: README line 7 notes "Grok-4 on openrouter has started refusing to role play as a psychotic user. However, I found that Grok-3 is just as good and doesn't refuse" -- the author already observed behavior drift on an unpinned route and worked around it by swapping models rather than pinning the route, exactly the failure mode M1/M3/M6 predict.
rows.one_line_fix: Pass provider={"quantizations":["fp16","bf16","fp8"],"data_collection":"deny","sort":"exacto"} as a model_arg to inspect_ai's get_model() for every openrouter/... target/redteam/grader model, and log each response's OpenRouter 'provider' field / generation id alongside every saved transcript.
rows.audit_notes: Depends on inspect_ai>=0.3.122 (UK AISI's Inspect framework) rather than calling the OpenRouter SDK directly, so I had to fetch inspect_ai's own openrouter.py provider source live from GitHub to confirm Inspect applies zero provider-pinning defaults on the caller's behalf -- the safety burden is entirely on passing model_args, which this repo never does.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-fetched every source cited in 'evidence' and all of it checks out verbatim; found no false accusations and no missed corruption channels.

1. redteaming_systematic.py (git clone --depth 1, tim-hua-01/ai-psychosis): DEFAULT_MODELS at lines 34-46 is byte-for-byte as quoted, including bare undated slugs 'openrouter/x-ai/grok-3', 'openrouter/x-ai/grok-4', 'openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2', 'openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat', 'openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528', 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-flash', 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-pro'. Lines 351/352/354 confirm `self.target_model = get_model(target_model_name)`, `self.redteam_model = get_model(redteam_model_name)`, `self.grader_model = get_model(grader_model_name) if grader_model_name else get_model(redteam_model_name)` -- single positional arg only, zero model_args anywhere in the file or repo.

2. Repo-wide grep for 'provider=', 'require_parameters', 'quantizations', 'data_collection', order/only/sort keys, GenerateConfig, temperature/top_p/seed, response_format/json_schema across all .py/.md/.toml: zero real hits (the one grep match was an unrelated 'keywords:' string in a transcript). This independently confirms M2/M7/M9 correctly excluded (no sampling params exist to be dropped, no seed, no structured-output constraint on the grader -- it free-parses JSON via json.loads with a try/except) and M11/M12 correctly excluded (no cross-routing of the same nominal model, no :floor/:nitro suffixes).

3. Live-fetched inspect_ai's actual OpenRouterAPI provider source (github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai, src/inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py, current main). Confirmed the exact mechanism: `self.provider = collect_model_arg("provider")` defaults to None, and extra_body is only populated with `provider`/`models`/`transforms`/`reasoning` keys `if self.models or self.provider or self.transforms or reasoning` (line ~373-382). With zero model_args passed (as in this repo), no extra_body is ever sent -- i.e., Inspect applies OpenRouter's raw defaults (probabilistic routing, no quant floor, no data_collection restriction) exactly as the verdict claims, placing 100% of the pinning burden on the caller.

4. Read an actual result_grades/*.csv: header is exactly `turn_number,mention_professional_help,pushback_rating,...,target_model,redteam_model,grader_model,patient_name,redteam_prompt_header,start_time,end_time` -- no provider or generation-id column, confirmed against two different CSV files.

5. Inspected an actual full_transcripts/*_redteam.json message dict: fields are id/content/source/metadata/internal/role/tool_calls/model, with metadata: null and model just echoing the requested slug ('x-ai/grok-4') -- no OpenRouter provider/generation-id is captured even where a metadata slot exists, and save_conversations() (the JSON-dumping path) is in fact commented out in run_single_redteaming, leaving only the markdown transcript for 'target'. This is stronger confirmation of M4 than the original evidence text cited.

6. README.md matches the quoted line verbatim: 'Note: Grok-4 on openrouter has started refusing to role play as a psychotic user. However, I found that Grok-3 is just as good and doesn't refuse.' -- a real instance of unpinned-route behavior drift being worked around by model-swapping rather than route-pinning, exactly as M1/M3/M6 predict.

7. WebFetched the live Alignment Forum post: both quotes are verbatim present -- 'I accessed chatgpt-4o-latest and gpt-5 using the OpenAI API, Claude 4 Sonnet using the Anthropic API, and the remaining models through OpenRouter' and 'I use the default API settings in the InspectAI package.' This confirms the mixed direct-API/OpenRouter comparison underlying M8, and supports 'aware_partial' (backend named + a caveat flagged, but never connected to quantization/routing-drift as a threat to the cross-model rankings).

8. GitHub API confirms stargazers_count = 52, matching the 'importance' field exactly.

Sanity checks per the taxonomy's LEGIT carve-out: none of the flagged models are proprietary single-served models wrongly tagged -- the evidence text itself narrows M1/M3 to the genuinely multi-provider/multi-quant OpenRouter models (kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1-0528, gpt-oss-20b), which is accurate (these are well-known multi-host OpenRouter listings with materially different quant/precision across backends), and does not over-apply M1/M3 to the OpenAI/Anthropic-direct models (chatgpt-4o-latest, gpt-5, claude-sonnet-4), which are correctly excluded from the OpenRouter-risk classes. No missed M9 (grader is free-text JSON parse, not schema-constrained) and no missed M11 (no model is called through two different backends under one name). critical_route:true is justified -- OpenRouter-served completions and the OpenRouter-routed grader's parsed scores are literally the dependent variable of every comparative chart in the post. I find no basis to weaken or strengthen any field; the verdict is fully supported by re-fetched primary evidence.

rows.title: ARC-AGI Benchmarking (ARC Prize Foundation)
rows.venue: arXiv / ARC Prize official leaderboard tooling
rows.summary: Official evaluation harness for the ARC-AGI / ARC Prize benchmark, running candidate LLMs against ARC-AGI tasks via a pluggable set of provider adapters (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Fireworks, Grok, OpenRouter, MuleRouter, etc.) to produce pass/fail scores that feed the public ARC-AGI leaderboard.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouterAdapter (src/arc_agi_benchmarking/adapters/openrouter.py) wraps the OpenAI SDK pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 and is used as the serving path for 13 model configs in models.yml, including several open-weight, multi-provider models: DeepSeek-R1-0528, Qwen3-235B-A22B-07-25, Mistral's Magistral-small/medium-2506 (+thinking variant), plus proprietary models like grok-3/grok-3-mini-beta and gemini-2.5-pro-preview.
safeguards_present: extra_body.provider.only (used on only 2 of 13 openrouter configs)
rows.mistakes: M1/M3 (Unpinned quantization / probabilistic routing, HIGH): Of the 13 model configs in src/arc_agi_benchmarking/models.yml with provider: "openrouter", only 2 set extra_body.provider.only to pin a backend (claude-4-sonnet-...-bedrock -> only:[amazon-bedrock] at models.yml:1050-1052; gemini-2-5-pro-preview-openrouter-thinking-1k -> only:[google-ai-studio] at models.yml:1394-1396). The remaining 11 -- including the clearly open-weight, multi-provider models deepseek_r1_0528-openrouter (models.yml:1458-1465), qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25 (models.yml:1562-1569), magistral-small-2506/magistral-medium-2506/magistral-medium-2506-thinking (models.yml:1407-1433) -- have no provider order/only/sort and no quantizations field, so OpenRouter is free to load-balance each request across whichever upstream inference provider is available (Fireworks/Together/DeepInfra/Novita/etc.), each potentially serving a different quantization (down to int4/fp8). Because this harness generates the official ARC-AGI leaderboard scores for these exact models, a leaderboard score attributed to "DeepSeek R1" or "Qwen3-235B" may reflect a randomly-selected backend/precision rather than a fixed, reproducible configuration, and repeat runs can silently drift. M4 (No provenance logging, HIGH): AttemptMetadata.provider (src/arc_agi_benchmarking/schemas.py:128) is populated from the static config string self.model_config.provider (openai_base.py:102), i.e. always the literal string "openrouter" -- never the actual serving backend or generation id that OpenRouter's API returns per response. AttemptMetadata (schemas.py:122-131) has no field for served-provider or generation_id at all, and a repo-wide grep for generation_id/provider_used/x-openrouter/response.provider returned nothing. There is therefore no way, even after the fact, to audit which upstream provider/quantization actually produced any given official ARC-AGI attempt for an OpenRouter-routed model. M6 (Model version drift, low/medium): grok-3-openrouter maps to model_name: "x-ai/grok-beta" (models.yml:810), a bare undated alias slug rather than a dated snapshot, so the served weights can change silently between evaluation runs. M8 (Cross-provider comparison confound, HIGH given this is a comparative leaderboard): The leaderboard juxtaposes proprietary models served through single, consistent direct APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini native adapters) against open-weight models served unpinned through OpenRouter's multi-provider pool -- an asymmetric, uncontrolled source of score variance baked into head-to-head leaderboard comparisons. Note the team clearly knows the extra_body.provider.only mechanism exists (used it twice) but did not apply it to the higher-risk open-weight models where multi-provider quantization variance on OpenRouter is best documented (DeepSeek R1, Qwen3, Magistral) -- hence 'aware_partial' rather than 'unaware'. M2 (silent parameter dropping) and M9 (judge on unconstrained route) were checked and do not apply: none of the 13 OpenRouter configs set temperature/top_p/response_format, and a full-repo grep for response_format/json_schema/require_parameters/data_collection/quantiz/extra_body found no require_parameters or data_collection usage anywhere, but also no reliance on strict sampling or structured-output params that would silently break.
rows.one_line_fix: Add extra_body.provider.only (or order + quantizations) pinning to every OpenRouter model config -- especially the open-weight ones (DeepSeek R1, Qwen3-235B, Magistral) -- and add a served_provider / generation_id field to AttemptMetadata populated from the OpenRouter response so every leaderboard attempt is auditable post hoc.
rows.evidence: src/arc_agi_benchmarking/adapters/openrouter.py:1-13 defines OpenRouterAdapter(OpenAIBaseAdapter) with base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1". src/arc_agi_benchmarking/adapters/openai_base.py:144-155 shows `_chat_completion` calling `self.client.chat.completions.create(model=self.model_config.model_name, messages=messages, **api_kwargs)` where api_kwargs come straight from models.yml's extra fields (including extra_body). src/arc_agi_benchmarking/models.yml has 13 `provider: "openrouter"` entries; only 2 contain `extra_body.provider.only` (lines ~1050 and ~1394); deepseek_r1_0528-openrouter (line 1458), qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25 (line 1562), and the three magistral-* entries (lines 1407-1433) have no provider pinning at all. openai_base.py:102 assigns `provider=self.model_config.provider` into AttemptMetadata -- a static config-file string, not the served backend from OpenRouter's response; schemas.py:122-131 (AttemptMetadata class) has no served-provider/generation-id field anywhere in the schema.
rows.audit_notes: Interesting split: the authors clearly know the provider-pinning knob exists (extra_body.provider.only used for exactly 2 of 13 OpenRouter configs -- Claude via Bedrock and one Gemini thinking variant) but applied it inconsistently, missing precisely the open-weight, multi-backend models (DeepSeek R1, Qwen3-235B, Magistral) where OpenRouter quantization/provider variance is most severe and best documented. The repo also has a second router adapter (mulerouter.py) not investigated here. Verified via shallow clone + grep rather than GitHub search API (rate-limited mid-investigation).
rows.importance: Official evaluation harness for the ARC Prize / ARC-AGI benchmark created by François Chollet -- one of the most media-covered "general reasoning" benchmarks in AI (cited in OpenAI's o3 announcement, $1M+ prize purses, ARC-AGI-2 paper arXiv:2505.11831); 351 GitHub stars on the tooling repo; results cited constantly in reasoning-model discourse.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned arcprize/arc-agi-benchmarking fresh (git clone --depth 1) and re-verified every cited file/line; all core evidence checks out, and live OpenRouter API data strengthens the case further.

Confirmed exactly as claimed:
- src/arc_agi_benchmarking/adapters/openrouter.py: OpenRouterAdapter(OpenAIBaseAdapter), base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1" — matches.
- `grep -c 'provider: "openrouter"' models.yml` = 13 entries (lines 811,820,832,1047,1247,1382,1391,1410,1419,1428,1460,1555,1564). Note: the repo has evolved since the verdict was written (new entries added: gemini-3-pro-preview, qwen3-max-thinking-openrouter, with future pricing dates up to 2026-02-10), so the *specific* 13 configs differ slightly from what's named in the evidence text, but the count and the pattern hold.
- `grep -i 'only|order|sort|quantiz|data_collection|require_parameters' models.yml` returns exactly 2 hits, both `only:` — at line 1051 (claude-4-sonnet-...-bedrock → only:[amazon-bedrock]) and line 1395 (gemini-2-5-pro-preview-openrouter-thinking-1k → only:[google-ai-studio]). This exactly matches the verdict's "only 2 of 13 pinned" claim, confirmed by direct grep, not just spot-checking.
- deepseek_r1_0528-openrouter (line 1458-1465), qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25 (1562-1569), and all three magistral-* entries (1408-1433) have zero provider/quantization pinning — confirmed no `extra_body` block at all in these entries.
- openai_base.py:100-113 (`metadata = AttemptMetadata(... provider=self.model_config.provider ...)`) confirms `provider` is the static config-file string, not a served-backend value — matches evidence.
- schemas.py AttemptMetadata (lines 126-152 in current HEAD, vs. cited 122-131 — minor line drift from repo changes, same content) has no served-provider or generation_id field. Repo-wide grep for `response_id|resp\.id|response\.id` across .py files shows the only place OpenRouter's response.id is ever touched is a local streaming variable in openai_base.py that is discarded — never persisted into AttemptMetadata or the submission JSON. This fully corroborates M4.
- Repo-wide grep for `response_format|json_schema|require_parameters|generation_id|provider_used|x-openrouter` in *.py returns zero hits — confirms M2/M9 correctly excluded (no sampling/structured-output params on any OpenRouter config to silently break).
- grok-3-openrouter → model_name "x-ai/grok-beta" (line 810) confirmed as a bare undated alias — M6 stands.

Additional corroboration beyond the original verdict (I queried OpenRouter's live public `/api/v1/models/{id}/endpoints` API, not just the repo):
- deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528 currently has 4 live endpoints (DeepInfra fp4, SiliconFlow fp8, StreamLake unknown, Novita fp8) — i.e. an unpinned request to this exact model_name really can land on int4/fp8/unknown precision, exactly the M1 failure mode.
- qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25 currently has 12 live endpoints across DeepInfra/Novita/Parasail/Alibaba/Venice/Nebius/Friendli/AtlasCloud/StreamLake/Google(x2)/GMICloud, 8 of them fp8 and 4 "unknown" quantization — strong real-world confirmation of M1/M3 for this exact config.
- x-ai/grok-beta and mistralai/magistral-medium-2506 currently show 0 live endpoints (likely deprecated/superseded slugs), which if anything reinforces the M6 "version drift / stale alias" concern for grok-3-openrouter, and confirms M1/M3 were correctly *not* the basis for flagging Grok (single-vendor proprietary model, consistent with the taxonomy's LEGIT carve-out).

One minor inaccuracy in the verdict's supporting narrative, not enough to change the verdict: it describes magistral-medium-2506 as "clearly open-weight," but Mistral's Magistral Medium is actually the proprietary/API-only sibling (only Magistral Small is Apache-2.0 open-weight). This doesn't rescue the config under the LEGIT carve-out, though — LEGIT requires provider pinning AND provenance logging, and magistral-medium-2506 has neither, so the M1/M4 gap is real regardless of weight-openness; only the "open-weight" adjective in the prose is imprecise.

Sanity checks per the review steps: no proprietary single-served model was wrongly hit with M1/M3/M8 (Grok and native Gemini/Anthropic/OpenAI paths are correctly excluded from the HIGH-severity M1/M3 framing); no missed M9 (no response_format/JSON-schema usage anywhere); the route is genuinely critical (arcprize org repo feeding the public ARC-AGI benchmark, confirmed by README: "Run ARC-AGI tasks against multiple model adapters... with built-in... scoring", used by CLI batch tooling in cli/run_all.py). The safe/unsafe call, severity, confidence, and all five mistake IDs (M1, M3, M4, M6, M8) are justified by the code and are, if anything, understated relative to what OpenRouter's live provider data now shows.

rows.title: AgentLab (ServiceNow Research)
rows.venue: other (framework backing 2024 TMLR submission "The BrowserGym Ecosystem for Web Agent Research"; public HuggingFace leaderboard)
rows.summary: AgentLab is ServiceNow Research's open-source framework for building and benchmarking LLM web agents (GenericAgent, WebArena/VisualWebArena agents, etc.) against BrowserGym-based benchmarks (WebArena, VisualWebArena, WorkArena, MiniWoB), feeding an official public leaderboard and the BrowserGym Ecosystem TMLR paper.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is a first-class, directly-instantiated LLM backend for agent rollouts. `OpenRouterModelArgs` (src/agentlab/llm/chat_api.py:79-89, and a second copy in src/agentlab/llm/response_api.py:966-982) builds an `OpenAI`-SDK client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 and every action-generating LLM call during a benchmark episode goes straight through it. Named, ready-to-run agent configs use it directly, e.g. WA_AGENT_SONNET / VWA_AGENT_SONNET in src/agentlab/agents/visualwebarena/agent.py:299-317 (`CHAT_MODEL_ARGS_DICT["openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet:beta"]`), and multiple open-weight configs in src/agentlab/agents/generic_agent/agent_configs.py and hint_use_agent/agent_configs.py using `openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct`, `llama-3-70b-instruct`, `llama-3.1-70b-instruct`, `llama-4-maverick`, plus `openrouter/openai/o3-mini`, `o1-mini-2024-09-12`. The full model catalog (src/agentlab/llm/llm_configs.py:256-377) also defines `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1`, `qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct`, `openai/gpt-oss-120b`, `openai/gpt-oss-20b`. The agent's chosen action on every benchmark step is parsed directly from this call's output, so it is a critical route to the reported task-success-rate numbers.
rows.mistakes: The actual API call site (`ChatModel.__call__`, src/agentlab/llm/chat_api.py:290-309: `self.client.chat.completions.create(model=self.model_name, messages=messages, n=n_samples, temperature=temperature, max_completion_tokens=self.max_tokens, logprobs=self.log_probs)`) never sets an `extra_body`/`provider` payload — confirmed by grep across the entire repo (`extra_body|require_parameters|data_collection|quantizations|"order"|allow_fallbacks`) returning zero hits in src/, tutorials/, tests/. The parallel path in src/agentlab/llm/response_api.py:620-651 (`OpenAIChatCompletionModel._call_api`) is identical: no provider config, and it additionally passes `tool_choice`/forced-tool-call params (lines 638-647) used to extract the agent's environment action from tool_calls — an unconstrained-route risk analogous to M9 (forced structured output with no require_parameters) since a provider that silently ignores tool_choice would corrupt the extracted action. Concretely: (1) M1 — open-weight models (llama-3.1-405b/70b/8b, llama-3-70b, llama-4-maverick, deepseek-r1, qwen-2-72b, gpt-oss-120b/20b) can be served at any quantization OpenRouter chooses on a given call, directly moving WebArena/VisualWebArena/MiniWoB task-success rates for those named agents. (2) M2 — temperature/n_samples are passed with no require_parameters, so a provider could silently ignore temperature=0.1/0.0 (reproducibility mode sets temperature to 0.0 in VisualWebArenaAgentArgs.set_reproducibility_mode) without AgentLab knowing. (3) M3 — no order/only/sort anywhere means every one of these calls is default load-balanced; a single benchmark run of hundreds of episodes can silently mix multiple backend providers for the same named agent, adding run-to-run and even within-run variance to the reported success rate. (4) M4 — `LLMTracker`/`Stats` (src/agentlab/llm/tracking.py:35-56, 91-95) only ever record input_tokens/output_tokens/cost; grep for `.provider`, `generation_id`, `completion.id` across src/agentlab/llm/*.py shows no field of the served provider or OpenRouter generation id is ever captured or stored with results, so a given episode's outcome cannot be traced to which backend produced it. (5) M5 — data_collection is never restricted, so prompts (including live web page content/screenshots for VWA) may be sent to data-collecting sub-providers by default. (6) M6 — several open-weight slugs are bare/undated (`meta-llama/llama-4-maverick`, `deepseek/deepseek-r1`, `qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct`) in contrast to the dated slugs used for the direct-OpenAI configs in the same dict (e.g. `gpt-4.1-2025-04-14`), so the exact checkpoint behind results isn't pinned. (7) M8 — this is a comparison/leaderboard framework: the same table of agents mixes proprietary single-served models called directly (OpenAIModelArgs/AzureModelArgs/AnthropicModelArgs/BedrockModelArgs — low risk) with open-weight models called only via unpinned OpenRouter — any 'model X beats model Y on WebArena' conclusion drawn across this mixed backend set is confounded by unmeasured provider/quantization variance on the OpenRouter side. (8) M10 — README.md has a detailed, good-faith '↻ Reproducibility' section (lines 248-289) enumerating software-version drift, API models silently changing, live-website drift, temperature, and non-deterministic tasks as reproducibility risks, and even ships a `reproducibility_journal.csv` + `ReproducibilityAgent` — but never once mentions OpenRouter provider routing, quantization, or which inference backend actually served a call, despite `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` being documented as a first-class env var (README.md:287) right next to it. That section is exactly where this should have been disclosed and was not.
rows.one_line_fix: In OpenRouterChatModel.__call__ (chat_api.py) and OpenAIChatCompletionModel._call_api (response_api.py), pass extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "require_parameters": True, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}} on every chat.completions.create call, and store the response's provider/generation id in AgentInfo/stats alongside each episode's result.
rows.importance: 606 GitHub stars; official framework from ServiceNow Research (BrowserGym/WorkArena authors), backs the 2024 TMLR submission 'The BrowserGym Ecosystem for Web Agent Research' (arXiv:2412.05467) and the public ServiceNow/browsergym-leaderboard on HuggingFace; ships traces on HuggingFace (agentlabtraces/agentlabtraces).
rows.audit_notes: Two independent, structurally near-identical OpenRouterModelArgs implementations exist in the repo (chat_api.py and response_api.py) — both share the exact same absence of any provider-routing config, so this isn't a one-off oversight in a single code path but a repo-wide pattern. Notably the README's Reproducibility section is unusually thorough about other reproducibility threats (live-website drift, silent LLM updates, seed effects) which makes the complete silence on OpenRouter provider/quantization variance more conspicuous — it reads like a genuine blind spot rather than a considered omission. Confirmed the exact discovery-evidence snippet verbatim at src/agentlab/agents/visualwebarena/agent.py:299-302 and 314-317.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned ServiceNow/AgentLab at HEAD (commit cbc35a9, 2026-03-17) and checked every cited evidence location; all of it holds up almost line-for-line.

1) chat_api.py: OpenRouterModelArgs at lines 78-89 (class decorator 78, body 79-89, matches cite '79-89'); ChatModel.__call__ signature at line 290, the exact API call block `self.client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=..., n=n_samples, temperature=temperature, max_completion_tokens=self.max_tokens, logprobs=self.log_probs)` spans lines 302-309 (cite '290-309') -- confirmed verbatim, no extra_body/provider kwarg anywhere in that call.

2) response_api.py: OpenRouterModelArgs at lines 966-982 (cite matches almost exactly), building OpenAIChatCompletionModel with base_url=openrouter.ai/api/v1. OpenAIChatCompletionModel._call_api spans exactly lines 620-651 (cite '620-651' is exact), and the tool_choice/force_call_tool block is exactly lines 638-647 (cite exact) -- 'required' tool_choice forced with no require_parameters, feeding directly into _extract_env_actions_from_toolcalls, which is the parsed benchmark action. This is a real analog of M9 risk (correctly NOT added to the mistake_ids list since it isn't literally response_format/json_schema -- the verdict is properly conservative here).

3) Repo-wide grep (not just src/) for `extra_body|require_parameters|data_collection|quantizations|allow_fallbacks|"order"` returns zero hits anywhere in the repository, including yaml/toml/json config files -- confirms M1, M2, M3, M5 have no mitigation anywhere, not just in the two call sites quoted.

4) tracking.py LLMTracker (lines 35-58) only accumulates input_tokens/output_tokens/cost; repo-wide grep for generation_id/completion.id/response.id/.provider returns nothing -- confirms M4.

5) llm_configs.py lines 256-377: verified all cited OpenRouter catalog entries exist verbatim (deepseek-r1, llama-3.1-405b/70b/8b, llama-3-70b, llama-4-maverick, qwen-2-72b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b/20b, o3-mini, o1-mini-2024-09-12, claude-3.5-sonnet:beta, claude-3.7-sonnet, gpt-5-nano/mini/chat). Bare/undated slugs (llama-4-maverick, deepseek/deepseek-r1, qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct, openai/o3-mini) sit in the same file as dated direct-OpenAI slugs like gpt-4.1-2025-04-14 (confirmed at line 30) and o3-mini-2025-01-31 (line 36) -- confirms M6's specific contrast claim.

6) visualwebarena/agent.py: WA_AGENT_SONNET and VWA_AGENT_SONNET confirmed at lines ~297-311 (close to cite '299-317'), both using openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet:beta -- correctly NOT flagged for M1 (Anthropic doesn't quantize proprietary weights), and the verdict's mistake text properly scopes M1 to only the open-weight models, showing appropriate restraint against false accusation.

7) set_reproducibility_mode in visualwebarena/agent.py:80-81 confirmed to set `self.temperature = 0.0`, matching the M2 reproducibility-mode claim.

8) README.md: Reproducibility section confirmed at lines 248-279 (cite '248-289' close enough, actual content ends ~279 but adjoining env-var section extends to 291), discusses software-version drift, silent LLM updates, live-website drift, temperature, non-deterministic tasks -- and never mentions OpenRouter, provider routing, or quantization. `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` documented at exactly line 287 (cite is exact) directly under that same Variables section -- confirms M10 precisely.

9) grep for seed passed to any chat completion call: none found (only benchmark/task seeds, unrelated to LLM sampling) -- confirms the verdict's correct omission of M7. grep for response_format/json_schema: zero hits -- confirms correct omission of formal M9. grep for :floor/:nitro/sort-by-price: zero hits -- confirms correct omission of M12.

10) generic_agent.py:83,98 confirms self.chat_llm (built from chat_model_args, which for many named configs is OpenRouterModelArgs) feeds get_action, i.e. the environment action taken on every benchmark step -- confirms critical_route=true.

Everything checked corroborates the verdict as filed. I found no evidence contradicting it, no proprietary model wrongly flagged M1/M3, no missed require_parameters/provider pin anywhere in the codebase, and no over-claimed M9/M7/M11/M12. The verdict is accurate and appropriately scoped; I confirm it unchanged.

rows.title: Aider (AI pair-programming CLI + polyglot code benchmark / leaderboard)
rows.summary: Aider is a popular (47k+ star) open-source AI pair-programming CLI that supports many LLM backends via litellm, including OpenRouter. It also ships a "polyglot" coding benchmark (benchmark/benchmark.py) whose results populate a widely-cited public leaderboard comparing coding ability across frontier and open-weight models.
rows.openrouter_use: Two uses: (1) end-user CLI tool — any user can point aider at `openrouter/<provider>/<model>`; (2) the polyglot benchmark harness (benchmark/benchmark.py) instantiates the exact same `aider.models.Model` -> `litellm.completion` code path to run 225 coding exercises per model and record pass rates that are published on the public Aider leaderboard. At least 11 of the ~151 published leaderboard rows were run with `aider --model openrouter/...` (e.g. openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2, openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b, openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b, openrouter/google/gemma-3-27b-it, openrouter/all-hands/openhands-lm-32b-v0.1, openrouter/x-ai/grok-4, openrouter/x-ai/grok-3-beta, openrouter/openai/o1, plus stealth models openrouter/openrouter/quasar-alpha and openrouter/openrouter/optimus-alpha), several of which are open-weight models.
rows.venue: Community/industry — 47,473+ GitHub stars; leaderboard is a de facto reference cited in model-release comparisons
rows.importance: 47k+ stars, one of the most popular AI coding CLIs; its polyglot leaderboard is routinely cited by third parties (blog posts, model launch announcements) as an independent measure of coding capability, so corruption of individual leaderboard rows propagates into public capability claims about those models.
safeguards_present: provenance_disclosure_of_command_string_on_leaderboard_page
rows.evidence: Corrected count: `aider/website/_data/polyglot_leaderboard.yml` contains 69 total rows (`grep -c "^- dirname:"`), of which exactly 11 use `aider --model openrouter/...` (≈16%, not "~151 rows"/"~7%" as stated). New M2 evidence: aider/models.py:143 (`use_temperature: Union[bool, float] = True` default) + :997 (`if self.use_temperature is not False: kwargs["temperature"] = ...`) send a sampling parameter on virtually every completion call; `grep -n "require_parameters" aider/resources/model-settings.yml` returns zero matches in the full 3128-line file, so no OpenRouter-routed leaderboard model ever pins require_parameters against silent param-dropping. New M5 evidence: same file already cited (openrouter.md:40-41) plus confirmed `grep -n "data_collection" aider/resources/model-settings.yml` → zero matches, i.e. no shipped model ever sets `data_collection: deny`, consistent with the docs' permissive-training guidance.
rows.mistakes: M1 (unpinned quantization) and M3 (probabilistic provider routing): none of the OpenRouter-run leaderboard rows set `quantizations` or `order`/`only`/`sort`, so open-weight entries (kimi-k2, qwen3-32b, gpt-oss-120b, gemma-3-27b-it, openhands-lm-32b-v0.1) could have been served at any quantization by whichever provider won that call's load-balancing draw — directly threatens the reported pass_rate_1/pass_rate_2 numbers for those specific leaderboard rows, since backend/quantization choice alone can move code-benchmark accuracy by double digits. M4 (no provenance logging): benchmark.py's results dict never records the serving provider or generation id, so a later reader cannot verify or reproduce which backend actually produced a given leaderboard row's score. M6 (model version drift): several OpenRouter slugs used on the leaderboard are bare/undated (e.g. `openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2`, `openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b`), so the underlying weights behind that slug can change server-side after the leaderboard entry was published, silently invalidating the recorded score as a description of "the model" going forward. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the single ranked polyglot leaderboard table directly compares OpenRouter-routed, unpinned open-weight models against direct-API proprietary models (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/xAI called directly) — any performance gap involving an OpenRouter-routed row is partly confounded by unverified provider/quantization rather than being solely a capability difference, undermining the leaderboard's core comparative claim. M10 (no reporting): the canonical methodology post for this benchmark (2024-12-21-polyglot.md) never discusses OpenRouter routing risk, quantization, or inference-stack provenance, despite ~7% of published rows depending on it.
rows.one_line_fix: For leaderboard-producing runs, pin OpenRouter routing per model-settings entry (extra_params.extra_body.provider: {order/only, quantizations: ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], require_parameters: true, data_collection: "deny", allow_fallbacks: false}) and extend benchmark.py's results dict to capture and persist the response's actual serving `provider` field (or GET /api/v1/generation?id=...) alongside each row.
rows.audit_notes: Aider's own documentation (openrouter.md) demonstrates the maintainers understand OpenRouter's multi-provider routing risk in general — they even ship a ready-made YAML snippet for order/require_parameters/data_collection pinning — but that knowledge was never applied to the one place where it matters most for external credibility: the benchmark harness that feeds the public leaderboard. Also notable: the docs actively steer end users toward *permissive* data_collection ("enable providers that may train on inputs ... to allow use of all models"), which is the opposite of the recommended `data_collection: deny` safeguard, seemingly for model-availability convenience.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned Aider-AI/aider (depth 1, current main) and checked every file/line cited in "evidence". All of it checks out almost exactly:

- aider/openrouter.py:29-30 — `OpenRouterModelManager` class hitting `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models`, used only for pricing/context metadata. Confirmed verbatim.
- aider/models.py:1010-1011 (`if self.extra_params: kwargs.update(self.extra_params)`) and :1036 (`res = litellm.completion(**kwargs)`) — exact match. `extra_params` is the only OpenRouter-routing knob; nothing sets provider order/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection by default.
- aider/coders/base_coder.py:1369 (`kwargs = dict(self.main_model.extra_params) or dict()`) — confirmed in the cache-warming path, same conclusion.
- aider/website/docs/llms/openrouter.md:40-41 — confirmed verbatim: "Be sure to 'enable providers that may train on inputs' to allow use of all models" — the opposite of `data_collection: deny`. The pinning recipe (order/allow_fallbacks/data_collection deny/require_parameters) is at lines 57-70, opt-in via a separate `.aider.model.settings.yml` — confirmed.
- aider/resources/model-settings.yml — confirmed kimi-k2 (line 803), gemma-3-27b-it (940-946), quasar-alpha (981), grok-3/4 variants (986-1071), optimus-alpha (1071) all lack any `extra_params.extra_body.provider` block. Confirmed system-wide: `grep -n "require_parameters\|data_collection" aider/resources/model-settings.yml` → zero hits in the entire 3128-line file. qwen3-32b, gpt-oss-120b, and openhands-lm-32b-v0.1 have no model-settings entry at all (pure defaults).
- benchmark/benchmark.py results dict (~941-964) — confirmed: records model, edit_format, cost, duration, tokens, chat_hashes, etc.; no provider/generation-id field anywhere in the file.
- aider/website/_posts/2024-12-21-polyglot.md — confirmed zero matches for "openrouter", "provider", or "quantiz" (grep -i, exit code 1).
- aider/website/docs/leaderboards/index.md — confirmed a "Command" column rendering `row.command`, i.e. the raw `aider --model ...` string is shown per leaderboard row — supports the claimed partial safeguard.
- Leaderboard rows: confirmed exactly 11 rows use `aider --model openrouter/...` (o1, gemma-3-27b-it, quasar-alpha, grok-3-beta, grok-3-mini-beta, optimus-alpha, openhands-lm-32b-v0.1, qwen3-32b, grok-4, kimi-k2, gpt-oss-120b) — matches the verdict's list exactly.

One factual correction needed: the summary/mistakes text says "11 of the ~151 published leaderboard rows" / "~7% of published rows." I counted `aider/website/_data/polyglot_leaderboard.yml` directly (`grep -c "^- dirname:"`) = 69 total rows, not ~151. 151 doesn't correspond to any leaderboard file or combination I could find in this repo (o1_polyglot=10, qwen3=10, refactor=14, edit=98; no combination equals 151). The correct proportion is 11/69 ≈ 16%, roughly double what the verdict states — this makes the finding modestly *more* significant, not less, but the "~151"/"~7%" figures should be corrected to "~69"/"~16%" in any published version.

Two real mistakes are supported by the verdict's own evidence but were left out of mistake_ids:

1. M2 (silent parameter dropping) — should be added. `models.py:143` sets `use_temperature: Union[bool, float] = True` by default, and `send_completion` (line 997: `if self.use_temperature is not False: ... kwargs["temperature"] = ...`) sends a `temperature` sampling parameter on essentially every completion call unless a model-settings entry explicitly sets `use_temperature: false`. None of the OpenRouter leaderboard models (kimi-k2, qwen3-32b, gpt-oss-120b, gemma-3-27b-it, grok-3/4 variants, openhands-lm-32b-v0.1) set `use_temperature: false`, and — as independently confirmed above — `require_parameters` is never set anywhere in the shipped `model-settings.yml`. So every OpenRouter-routed leaderboard call sends a sampling parameter with no guarantee the winning provider honors it rather than silently dropping it — a textbook, high-severity M2 violation that compounds M1/M3.

2. M5 (data-policy leakage) — should be added. The verdict's own cited evidence (`openrouter.md:40-41`, and the audit_notes callout) already establishes that the documentation actively steers users toward permissive `data_collection` and that no shipped model-settings entry sets `data_collection: deny`. This is exactly M5's definition ("data_collection left 'allow'") and was already substantiated in the evidence text, just not reflected in mistake_ids.

Sanity checks: no `response_format`/`json_schema` usage found anywhere in aider/benchmark (M9 correctly not flagged — no LLM-as-judge/structured-output route exists in this codebase). No `seed` parameter usage found (M7 correctly not flagged). No `:floor` or price-sort routing found (M12 correctly not flagged). The proprietary/single-served exception (M1/M3/M8 N/A) is correctly NOT applied to kimi-k2/qwen3-32b/gpt-oss-120b/gemma-3-27b-it/openhands-lm-32b-v0.1, which are genuinely open-weight, multi-host-capable models — appropriately scoped as "mixed." The route is correctly judged critical: benchmark.py's `Model`→`litellm.completion` path is identical to the CLI's own path, and its output populates a leaderboard that the "importance" field's citation pattern (external blog posts/launch announcements) is plausible and not central to the taxonomy call.

Net: the safe/unsafe verdict (unsafe), severity (high), and awareness (aware_partial — maintainers demonstrably understand OpenRouter routing risk per their own docs but never applied it to the benchmark) are all well-supported by re-fetched code. The only changes needed are (a) a citation-accuracy fix on the "151 rows" denominator, and (b) adding M2 and M5 to mistake_ids, both of which are already implicit in the verdict's own evidence text.

rows.title: AppWorld
rows.summary: AppWorld is an interactive coding/function-calling agent benchmark (750 tasks over 9 apps/457 APIs) from Stony Brook NLP with an official public leaderboard; it ships agent implementations (function-calling agent, ReAct code agent) plus a HAL-leaderboard export pipeline that turns agent rollout logs into published, comparable scores.
rows.openrouter_use: Production leaderboard-submission agent rollouts: `experiments/configs/{simplified_function_calling_agent,simplified_react_code_agent}/*/*_openrouter/{test_normal,test_challenge}.jsonnet` run the actual leaderboard test splits for four open-weight models (deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b, gpt-oss-20b) exclusively through OpenRouter via the litellm client (`"client_name": "litellm"`, `"name": "openrouter/<org>/<model>"`). Output flows straight into `scripts/appworld_to_hal_leaderboard.py`, which republishes the same logs to the HAL leaderboard.
rows.importance: 461 GitHub stars, ACL 2024 Best Resource Paper, official public leaderboard (Stony Brook NLP) that continues to accept new-model submissions (e.g., 2025-era deepseek-r1-0528 and gpt-oss configs added well after the 2024 paper) and feeds a HAL leaderboard export pipeline — i.e., this is a live, ongoing scored comparison, not a one-off exploratory run.
rows.evidence: experiments/configs/simplified_function_calling_agent/deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter/test_challenge.jsonnet:4-15 — model_config = {"client_name": "litellm", "name": "openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528", "temperature": 0.0, "seed": 100, ...} with no provider/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection/order/only/sort keys at all. Identical pattern confirmed in sibling configs: experiments/configs/_generator/models/meta.py:5-27 (llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter), experiments/configs/_generator/models/openai.py:486-600 (gpt-oss-120b/-20b *-reasoning_openrouter, 6 variants). The call path structurally cannot carry OpenRouter provider constraints even if added: experiments/code/simplified/language_model.py:157-197 (non_cached_lm_call) enumerates an explicit param whitelist with no provider/extra_body field, and line 196-197 does `if kwargs: raise ValueError(f"Unexpected kwargs in non_cached_lm_call: {kwargs}")`. Provenance: language_model.py:766-770 (may_log_call) logs `arguments`/`response` verbatim, but `response` is `to_dict(response_)` (line 276) of litellm's standard OpenAI-schema ModelResponse, which carries no OpenRouter "provider"/generation-id field — never captured. scripts/appworld_to_hal_leaderboard.py:158-206 (the actual leaderboard-export function) reads that same lm_calls.jsonl and republishes it unchanged, so the public leaderboard entry also carries zero provenance. experiments/configs/_generator/models/deepseek.py:60-62 comment "Worked on together_ai more reliably than openrouter" shows awareness that provider choice affects behavior generically, but this was never turned into pinning/require_parameters/quantizations for the models kept on OpenRouter. Grep across the whole tree for extra_body/quantizations/data_collection/require_parameters/generation_id/allow_fallbacks turned up zero OpenRouter-provider-routing hits (the only extra_body usages, in nvidia.py/alibaba.py, are unrelated local-vLLM chat_template_kwargs). README.md, experiments/README.md and guides/*.md contain no mention of reproducibility, quantization, or provider-routing risk.
rows.mistakes: M1 (unpinned quantization, High): deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b are all open-weight and called via a bare "openrouter/…" slug with no `quantizations` filter, so each leaderboard run may silently be served at a different precision (int4→fp32) run-to-run, threatening every reported score for these four models on the public AppWorld/HAL leaderboard. M2 (silent parameter dropping, High): temperature=0, seed=100, tool_choice="auto", parallel_tool_calls=true, reasoning_effort are all sent with no `require_parameters`, so a route that doesn't honor one can silently ignore it rather than error — corrupting the function-calling agent's behavior without any visible failure. M3 (probabilistic provider routing, High): no order/only/sort anywhere, so each of the many task-level calls (750 tasks × up to 50 steps) load-balances across whichever backend OpenRouter picks that instant — different tasks within the same reported run, and repeat runs across time, can hit different providers. M4 (no provenance logging, High): neither lm_calls.jsonl nor the HAL leaderboard export records which provider/quantization actually served each call, so a published score can never be traced back to what was actually run, let alone reproduced. M5 (data-policy leakage, Med): `data_collection` is never set (defaults to "allow"), so AppWorld task prompts/tool schemas may be retained/trained-on by whichever third-party backend gets routed to. M6 (model version drift, Med): llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b are bare undated slugs (deepseek-r1-0528 at least has a date suffix), so the served checkpoint isn't guaranteed stable across leaderboard resubmissions. M7 (seed→determinism assumption, Med): seed=100 is passed as if it yields reproducible generations, but combined with M1/M3 (unpinned quantization/provider) this gives no real determinism guarantee for the leaderboard numbers. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound, High): the AppWorld leaderboard directly compares these unpinned, OpenRouter-routed open-weight models against each other and against single-served, effectively-pinned proprietary models (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google called directly) — differences in the open-weight rows may reflect which backend/quantization got picked rather than genuine model capability. M10 (no reporting, Med): none of README.md, experiments/README.md, or guides/*.md discloses the OpenRouter routing setup or its reproducibility implications to leaderboard users/readers.
rows.one_line_fix: Add an `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": false, "require_parameters": true, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}}` field to every `openrouter/...` model_config, plumb a provider/extra_body kwarg through `non_cached_lm_call`/`cached_lm_call` to `litellm.completion` (currently any unlisted kwarg raises `ValueError` there), and record the served provider/generation id into `lm_calls.jsonl` before it feeds the HAL/AppWorld leaderboard export.
rows.audit_notes: Fun/interesting: the codebase actually shows partial provider-awareness elsewhere — deepseek-v3-0324 is explicitly routed via together_ai instead of OpenRouter with the comment "Worked on together_ai more reliably than openrouter" (deepseek.py:60-62), and several `openai.py` entries carry "NOTE: did not work with openai" / "did not work with together_ai" comments — so the authors clearly A/B-tested backends for functional compatibility (does tool-calling/reasoning work at all), but never applied any OpenRouter routing-pinning safeguards to the models they did leave on OpenRouter. This is a fairly clean "open-weight + zero safeguards + feeds a public continuously-updated leaderboard" case — about as close to the taxonomy's worst-case pattern as it gets, and notably the call-path code (`non_cached_lm_call`) has a hardcoded kwarg whitelist that would itself need a code change (not just a config change) before any provider-pinning fix could even be applied.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-fetched the actual repo (git clone --depth 1 StonyBrookNLP/appworld) and verified essentially every cited file/line. Confirmed accurate, byte-for-byte: (1) test_challenge.jsonnet / test_normal.jsonnet for deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter contain exactly the cited model_config with client_name/name/temperature/seed/tool_choice/parallel_tool_calls and zero provider/quantizations/require_parameters/order/only/sort/data_collection keys. (2) experiments/configs/_generator/models/meta.py:5-27 confirms llama-3.1-70b-instruct as bare undated "openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-70b-instruct" slug; openai.py:486-530 confirms gpt-oss-120b/-20b bare slugs with identical omissions. (3) language_model.py:156-197 (non_cached_lm_call) has the exact whitelist described, ending in `if kwargs: raise ValueError(...)` — confirmed via direct read, and confirmed by grep that "extra_body" appears nowhere in language_model.py, so even the one place in the codebase that *does* declare an extra_body field (configs/_generator/models/__init__.py:90, used only for local-vLLM chat_template_kwargs in nvidia.py/alibaba.py) has no functioning path through this call layer. (4) Repo-wide grep for extra_body/quantizations/data_collection/require_parameters/generation_id/allow_fallbacks returns only the two unrelated local-vLLM hits, exactly as claimed. (5) README.md/experiments/README.md/guides/*.md contain zero mentions of reproducibility/quantization/routing (grep confirmed empty) — M10 holds. (6) deepseek.py:60-62 comment about together_ai is verbatim accurate. (7) 461 GitHub stars confirmed via `gh api`; live public leaderboard at appworld.dev/leaderboard with pack/unpack/make CLI commands confirmed in README.md — critical_route:true is justified.

The one place the evidence overstates its case is M4. I built and ran the actual pinned litellm floor version (litellm==1.78.2, matching experiments/pyproject.toml's `litellm>=1.78.2`) and replicated AppWorld's exact `to_dict`/`_to_dict` functions verbatim against litellm's real `convert_to_model_response_object` (the function litellm's OpenrouterConfig.transform_response calls). Empirically: litellm has a generic passthrough — `for k,v in response_object.items(): if k not in special_keys: setattr(model_response_object, k, v)` — present in both the 1.78.2 floor and current litellm. Since litellm's ModelResponseBase subclasses openai._models.BaseModel (extra="allow"), any non-standard top-level key in OpenRouter's raw JSON response (OpenRouter's documented API does include a top-level "provider" field, e.g. "provider": "Nebius") survives into `dict(obj)`, which is exactly what AppWorld's `to_dict()` uses, and nothing in language_model.py's generate()/may_log_call() strips it before writing to lm_calls.jsonl. I verified this end-to-end with a runnable script (constructed a raw OpenRouter-shaped response with "provider":"DeepInfra", ran it through litellm's real convert_to_model_response_object, then through AppWorld's copied to_dict()) — the provider field survives intact into what gets logged. scripts/appworld_to_hal_leaderboard.py:158-206 also passes `lm_calls_datum["output"]` through to the HAL log unfiltered, so it would carry into the leaderboard export too.

However, this only applies to the two models using `"api_type": "chat_completions"` (deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct — confirmed default/explicit chat_completions). The other two flagged models, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b, use `"api_type": "responses"` (confirmed in openai.py:486-530), which routes through a genuinely different function: `responses_to_chat_completion_output()` (language_model.py:525-588), which I read in full — it rebuilds the response dict from scratch as `{"choices": [...], "usage": response["usage"]}`, discarding any top-level fields including provider/id. So M4 is real and total for gpt-oss-120b/-20b, but only accidental/incomplete for deepseek-r1-0528/llama-3.1-70b-instruct — the field leaks through unintentionally (never surfaced, extracted, validated, or relied upon anywhere in the pipeline — no code reads response["provider"], no report mentions it), so it does not function as actual provenance logging even where present. M4 should stay in the mistake list but the evidence text "response carries no OpenRouter provider/generation-id field — never captured" is factually wrong as a blanket claim for 2 of 4 models and needs this correction.

This nuance doesn't change the bottom line: M1/M2/M3/M5/M6/M8/M10 are all independently confirmed, high-confidence, and M1+M2+M3 alone (all High severity, all fully verified with zero mitigating evidence anywhere in the tree) are sufficient to sustain "unsafe"/"high severity" for a route that feeds a live public leaderboard. I checked for false-reassurance misses too: no response_format/JSON-schema usage on any OpenRouter-routed leaderboard path (the only response_format hits are in unrelated CI test configs, not the flagged model configs), so M9 correctly does not apply and was correctly omitted.</reasoning>
<parameter name="fixed_evidence">CORRECTION TO M4 (the only substantive fix needed): The claim "response carries no OpenRouter 'provider'/generation-id field — never captured" is only true for gpt-oss-120b/gpt-oss-20b (api_type="responses", whose output is rebuilt from scratch by responses_to_chat_completion_output() at language_model.py:525-588, dropping all top-level fields). For deepseek-r1-0528 and llama-3.1-70b-instruct (api_type="chat_completions", the default), I empirically verified — by installing the actual pinned litellm==1.78.2 floor and running AppWorld's exact to_dict()/_to_dict() code against litellm's real convert_to_model_response_object() with a mock OpenRouter-shaped raw response — that OpenRouter's documented top-level "provider" field (and the response "id", which doubles as the OpenRouter generation id) DOES survive into what may_log_call() writes to lm_calls.jsonl and into what appworld_to_hal_leaderboard.py:158-206 republishes, via litellm's generic pydantic extra="allow" field passthrough (present in litellm's convert_to_model_response_object at both the 1.78.2 floor and current main, confirmed by reading both source trees). This capture is accidental (a side effect of litellm's OpenAI-BaseModel extra-field handling, not an AppWorld design choice), never surfaced/read/reported/validated anywhere in the pipeline, and absent entirely for the two responses-API models — so M4 is correctly retained but should be described as "partial/accidental and unused, total absence for gpt-oss-120b/-20b" rather than a uniform "never captured" across all four models. All other cited evidence (jsonnet configs, non_cached_lm_call whitelist/ValueError, repo-wide grep results, README silence, deepseek.py together_ai comment, star count, live leaderboard) was independently re-verified against the freshly cloned repo and confirmed accurate as stated.

rows.title: CAMEL (Communicative Agents for "Mind" Exploration of LLM Society)
rows.summary: CAMEL is a general-purpose open-source multi-agent LLM framework (NeurIPS 2023) implementing role-playing dialogue between an "AI User" and "AI Assistant" agent; it now supports ~dozens of model backends, including OpenRouter as a first-class ModelPlatformType for running agents on open-weight and proprietary models.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable model backends (`ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER` -> `OpenRouterModel`) that researchers use to instantiate CAMEL ChatAgents (AI User / AI Assistant roles) for role-play / multi-agent-society experiments over open-weight models (Llama 3.1 405B/70B, Llama 4 Maverick/Scout, OlympicCoder-7B) as well as some proprietary/preview models (e.g. openrouter/horizon-alpha), cheaply. Confirmed via camel/models/model_factory.py:105 (`ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER: OpenRouterModel`) and the official example examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py.
rows.importance: 17,400+ GitHub stars; NeurIPS 2023 paper establishing the role-playing multi-agent-society paradigm; foundational infrastructure reused by downstream projects (e.g. OASIS). CAMEL is a widely-used framework, so any default-unsafe OpenRouter behavior propagates to many downstream research users rather than being a one-off script bug.
rows.one_line_fix: Add a `provider`/`extra_body` field to `OpenRouterConfig` (matching the pattern already used by DeepSeekConfig, QwenConfig, ModelScopeConfig, AnthropicConfig, VLLMConfig, OrcaRouterConfig in the same directory) defaulted to `require_parameters=True`, `data_collection="deny"`, and a documented `order`/`only`/`quantizations` pattern; and have OpenRouterModel capture and log the response's served `provider` field / generation id on every call.
rows.mistakes: CAMEL's blessed, documented OpenRouter integration provides literally zero support for any OpenRouter provider-routing safeguard, and this is a real current gap in the actively-maintained framework (not just a one-off script), even though the original NeurIPS 2023 paper's own reported numbers predate OpenRouter support and are not directly threatened. Evidence: camel/configs/openrouter_config.py declares only OpenAI-style sampling fields (temperature, top_p, n, stream, stop, max_tokens, penalties, response_format, tool_choice) -- no `provider`/`extra_body` field exists (M1/M2/M3/M5). Because camel/configs/base_config.py:29-35 sets `model_config = ConfigDict(extra="forbid", frozen=True)` and OpenRouterConfig does not override it, `OpenRouterConfig(provider={...})` raises a pydantic ValidationError -- the documented API structurally cannot express quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection/order/only/sort. This is a striking contrast within the same codebase: camel/configs/vllm_config.py:109, orcarouter_config.py:107, deepseek_config.py:115, modelscope_config.py:59, anthropic_config.py:91, and qwen_config.py:81 all expose `extra_body: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None` for exactly this kind of provider passthrough -- OpenRouterConfig, the one router that most needs it, is the outlier that omits it. A user could technically hand-build a raw `model_config_dict` dict with an `extra_body` key (since camel/models/base_model.py:349-360 deep-copies model_config_dict and camel/models/openai_compatible_model.py:358-363 spreads it as `**request_config` straight into `self._client.chat.completions.create(...)`), but this workaround appears in zero examples, zero tests, and zero docs (grepped whole repo for `"provider":`/`'provider':` routing dicts, `quantizations`, `require_parameters`, `data_collection` -- 0 hits outside this audit). No provenance logging exists anywhere in the OpenRouter path: grepping for `system_fingerprint`/`generation_id`/`.provider` across camel/ found exactly one hit, in camel/models/litellm_model.py:139, for the unrelated LiteLLM backend -- OpenRouterModel/OpenAICompatibleModel never reads or stores OpenRouter's served-`provider` field or generation id (M4). Model slugs are bare/undated, e.g. camel/types/enums.py:172-179 (`meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b-instruct`, `meta-llama/llama-4-maverick`, `openrouter/horizon-alpha`) (M6). `response_format` (JSON mode) is a first-class OpenRouterConfig field (openrouter_config.py:98) with no `require_parameters` gate, so any judge/structured-output use built on OpenRouterModel risks silent schema-drop on unsupporting providers (M9). Finally, the one official OpenRouter example, examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py:20-24, and the docs/key_modules/models.md:363-430 OpenRouter tutorial both default to `ModelType.OPENROUTER_LLAMA_4_MAVERICK_FREE` (a `:free`-tier, most-quantized/most-throttled route) with plain `OpenRouterConfig(temperature=0.2)` and no pinning whatsoever (M12), and neither the docs nor any example ever mentions quantization drift, provider pinning, or data-collection risk to users. Net effect: any downstream research (multi-agent role-play "society" studies, the exact use case CAMEL exists for) that uses CAMEL's documented OpenRouter path to generate reported dialogue/behavior findings over open-weight models gets zero reproducibility or provenance guarantees by default, and no supported way to add them short of bypassing the library's own config abstraction.
rows.evidence: All originally cited evidence re-verified against camel-ai/camel HEAD (commit 70c6a52, 2026-07-17): camel/configs/openrouter_config.py:91-103 (no provider/extra_body field); camel/configs/base_config.py:29-35 (extra="forbid", frozen=True); camel/configs/{vllm,orcarouter,deepseek,modelscope,anthropic,qwen}_config.py (all have sibling extra_body fields); camel/models/base_model.py:349-360 and camel/models/openai_compatible_model.py:358-363 (deepcopy + **request_config passthrough into openai SDK's create(), whose extra_body kwarg is the only route provider-pinning could take); camel/models/litellm_model.py:139 (sole system_fingerprint hit repo-wide); camel/types/enums.py:172-179 (bare undated slugs); examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py (only official example, uses :free tier, no pinning); docs/key_modules/models.md:363-430 (OpenRouter tab, no risk disclosure in either code sample); camel/models/model_factory.py:105 (registry entry); test/models/test_openrouter_model.py (9 tests, all about attribution headers only, zero coverage of provider/quantization/provenance). Additionally verified beyond the original citations: camel/models/openrouter_model.py (thin OpenAICompatibleModel subclass, only adds attribution headers, no provider-routing logic); camel/types/openai_types.py:15 (ChatCompletion is literally openai's own pydantic model, extra="allow" per openai/_models.py:128, so a generic opt-in response-dump logger would incidentally preserve OpenRouter's 'provider' field if CAMEL_MODEL_LOG_ENABLED is set — an unused, undocumented, off-by-default partial mitigation the original audit's narrow grep missed but which does not reach ordinary users via ChatAgentResponse.info); camel/agents/chat_agent.py _step_get_info/get_info_dict (user-facing response.info never includes a provider field); grep confirms response_format usage is confined to chat_agent.py and multi_hop_generator_agent.py (no dedicated judge/critic file uses it, so M9 is a structural/systemic risk rather than an observed instance).
