rows.audit_notes: Fun/notable: CAMEL's own codebase is internally inconsistent -- it already knows the `extra_body` passthrough pattern well (uses it for DeepSeek "thinking" params, Qwen reasoning, ModelScope, Anthropic, vLLM, and even for OrcaRouter, another LLM router) but simply never applied that pattern to OpenRouterConfig specifically, despite OpenRouter being the one backend where provider-routing nondeterminism is a documented, well-known risk. The NeurIPS 2023 paper itself is not at risk (OpenRouter support was added to the codebase well after publication; original experiments used direct OpenAI calls), so this is a forward-looking infrastructure risk to the many downstream projects (the framework has 17k+ stars and is explicitly built to enable cheap multi-model society experiments) rather than a corruption of CAMEL's own published numbers.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned camel-ai/camel at HEAD (commit 70c6a52, 2026-07-17) and re-fetched every file cited in evidence; essentially all citations check out almost verbatim despite the repo having moved on since the audit was written:

1. camel/configs/openrouter_config.py:91-103 — confirmed. Fields are exactly {temperature, top_p, n, stream, stop, max_tokens, presence_penalty, response_format, frequency_penalty, user, tool_choice}. No provider/extra_body/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection field exists.
2. camel/configs/base_config.py:29-35 — confirmed `model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True, extra="forbid", frozen=True, ...)`, inherited unmodified by OpenRouterConfig, so `OpenRouterConfig(extra_body=...)` would raise a pydantic ValidationError.
3. Sibling extra_body fields — confirmed present in vllm_config.py:109, orcarouter_config.py:107, deepseek_config.py:115, modelscope_config.py:59, anthropic_config.py:91, qwen_config.py:81. OpenRouterConfig is the outlier.
4. camel/models/base_model.py:349-360 (_prepare_request_config, deep-copies model_config_dict) and camel/models/openai_compatible_model.py:358-363 (_request_chat_completion spreads **request_config into self._client.chat.completions.create(...)) — both confirmed at essentially the same line numbers.
5. Confirmed the workaround the audit describes actually works mechanically: openai SDK's create() has a real extra_body: Body|None parameter (verified in installed openai/resources/chat/completions/completions.py) that would carry a {"provider": {...}} dict into the JSON body — but since ModelFactory.create() accepts model_config_dict as a raw untyped dict (not validated through OpenRouterConfig), a user CAN bypass the config class's extra="forbid" by hand-building the dict directly. Confirmed via grep this workaround appears in zero examples/docs/tests (0 hits for '"provider":' routing dicts, quantizations, require_parameters, data_collection anywhere in camel/, examples/, docs/, test/).
6. camel/models/litellm_model.py:139 — confirmed sole system_fingerprint capture in the repo, for the unrelated LiteLLM backend. Repo-wide grep for system_fingerprint/generation_id/.provider returns exactly this one hit.
7. camel/types/enums.py:172-179 — confirmed bare undated OpenRouter slugs (meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b-instruct, meta-llama/llama-4-maverick, openrouter/horizon-alpha, etc.).
8. examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py — confirmed uses ModelType.OPENROUTER_LLAMA_4_MAVERICK_FREE (the :free tier) with plain OpenRouterConfig(temperature=0.2), no pinning of any kind. This is the only official OpenRouter example in the repo.
9. docs/key_modules/models.md:363-430 — confirmed, read the full OpenRouter tab: two code samples, both using bare OpenRouterConfig(temperature=0.2) / {"temperature": 0.2}, zero mention of provider pinning, quantization, data_collection, or reproducibility risk anywhere.
10. camel/models/model_factory.py:105 — confirmed ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER: OpenRouterModel in the registry.
11. test/models/test_openrouter_model.py — read in full; all 9 tests cover only OpenRouter App-Attribution HTTP headers (HTTP-Referer/X-OpenRouter-Title) and basic construction — zero test coverage of provider pinning, quantization, or provenance.

One genuine nuance I found that the audit missed but which does NOT overturn the verdict: base_model.py has a generic, OFF-BY-DEFAULT logging path (CAMEL_MODEL_LOG_ENABLED env var) whose _log_response() calls response.model_dump() on the raw ChatCompletion object. Since camel.types.ChatCompletion is literally openai.types.chat.chat_completion.ChatCompletion (confirmed via camel/types/openai_types.py:15), and the openai SDK's BaseModel uses ConfigDict(extra="allow") (confirmed in openai/_models.py:128), OpenRouter's top-level provider field in the JSON response WOULD survive into this dump if a user enables the env var. This is a theoretical partial mitigation the audit's narrow grep (for the literal strings 'system_fingerprint'/'generation_id'/'.provider') would have missed. However it doesn't materially change M4's classification because: (a) it's disabled by default, (b) it's never documented as an OpenRouter provenance mechanism anywhere, (c) it is NOT surfaced in the standard user-facing ChatAgentResponse.info dict — confirmed via _step_get_info/get_info_dict in chat_agent.py, which only carries id/usage/finish_reasons/tool_calls, no provider field — so an ordinary CAMEL researcher calling agent.step() never sees it regardless of the env var. 'No provenance logging exists anywhere' is thus slightly overstated in absolute terms but directionally correct.

Sanity checks per the taxonomy: this is not a proprietary single-served model (OpenRouter routes across many third-party providers for open-weight models), so M1/M3-style concerns are legitimately in scope — and the audit correctly did NOT flag M8 (no specific cross-model comparison study identified), M7/M10/M11 (no seed-reliance, no downstream-paper-reporting claim, no backend-mixing evidence found), showing appropriate restraint rather than taxonomy-maximalism. M9 is the most inferential of the flagged items (no concrete judge+OpenRouter+response_format example exists in-repo; grep confirmed response_format is used only in chat_agent.py and multi_hop_generator_agent.py, not in any judge/critic/eval-named file) — it's a legitimate systemic risk given the field is exposed with no require_parameters gate, but is an inference about what downstream users could build rather than an observed instance, and the write-up is appropriately hedged. Overall the evidence is unusually well-cited and reproducible; I found no false accusation (all safe-path claims about vllm/orcarouter/deepseek/etc. having extra_body were independently verified) and no missed corruption channel beyond the minor logging nuance noted above, which is a mitigating factor, not an aggravating one worth adding to the taxonomy list.

rows.title: EQ-Bench Creative Writing Bench + Judgemark-v2
rows.summary: Two companion harnesses (by Samuel Paech / EQ-Bench) that call OpenRouter to (1) generate creative-writing completions from a "test model" and have a judge model rubric-score/pairwise-rank them for the public EQ-Bench Creative Writing leaderboard, and (2) meta-evaluate ~20 candidate judge models' scoring stability/separability for the public Judgemark-v2 leaderboard.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is the default/recommended inference backend for both the "test model" (completion generation) and "judge model" (rubric scoring + pairwise Elo comparisons in creative-writing-bench; numeric criterion scoring in Judgemark-v2). Output directly produces the public eqbench.com Creative Writing leaderboard and Judgemark-v2 leaderboard.
rows.importance: Created by Samuel Paech; ~580+ combined stars across EQ-Bench org repos; the Creative Writing leaderboard and Judgemark-v2 are widely cited in r/LocalLLaMA and LLM-judge-bias discussions and referenced in many model-release threads.
safeguards_present: provenance logging for HTTP-error debugging only (not for successful calls)
one hardcoded provider order pin (Fireworks, allow_fallbacks:false) for openai/gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b specifically in jm2/utils/api.py
position-bias (A/B order) and length-truncation mitigations for judging, unrelated to routing
rows.evidence: cwb/utils/api.py:297-303 — payload sent via bare `requests.post(self.base_url, headers=self.headers, json=payload, ...)` with no `provider` key at all (verified: no `provider:` dict, no `require_parameters`, no `quantizations`, no `data_collection` anywhere in the file). Line 228-232 explicitly states awareness of param-dropping and treats it as a convenience rather than a hazard: "If your test model doesn't support min_p, you may need to disable this here. Alternatively you could use openrouter which will automatically omit unsupported params." Only extracted return value is `content = data["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]` (line 310) — the full response (which would carry OpenRouter's `provider` field) is discarded, so nothing is ever persisted for successful calls (confirmed via grep: no `"provider"` key access, no `generation_id`, anywhere in cwb/core/*.py). jm2/utils/api.py:13 — `BASE_URL = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_URL", "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions")` — OpenRouter is the hardcoded default. Lines ~140-165 contain provider-pinning blocks wrapped in `if False and ...:` (dead code, e.g. Novita/Chutes pins for arbitrary judges) — proving the author knows how to pin providers but leaves it disabled for essentially all of the ~20 judge models tested; only `openai/gpt-oss-120b`/`gpt-oss-20b` get an active `data['provider'] = {"order": ["Fireworks"], "allow_fallbacks": False}` (no equivalent for the other ~18 open-weight judge models such as deepseek-r1, llama-3.1-405b/8b, mistral-large, wizardlm-2-8x22b, liquid-lfm-7b — confirmed present as `jm2/results/stats/*.json` files). README.md (jm2) documents `--judge-models "openai/gpt-4o,anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` style multi-model ensembling with zero mention of provider pinning, quantization, or data policy. cwb/README.md documents the leaderboard-canonical run command with `--judge-model "claude-sonnet-4-6"` and test models like `"your-model-provider/your-model-name"` — bare undated slugs (M6) — with no discussion anywhere of routing/inference-stack reproducibility.
rows.mistakes: M1 (unpinned quantization, High): the "test model" side of creative-writing-bench and ~18/20 judge models in Judgemark-v2 are open-weight (qwen3, kimi-k2, deepseek, hermes4, mistral, llama, wizardlm, liquid-lfm, ministral, gpt-oss) called via OpenRouter with no `quantizations` filter anywhere — any of these can be served int4/fp8/fp16 depending on which provider wins the load-balance that request, silently shifting generation quality/judge-scoring behavior and threatening every leaderboard entry. M2 (silent parameter dropping, High): cwb sends `min_p`, `reasoning`, and model-specific sampling tweaks over OpenRouter with no `require_parameters`; the code comment at cwb/utils/api.py:231 shows the author knowingly relies on OpenRouter's silent-drop behavior rather than guarding against it — different providers serving the "same" model may silently honor or ignore these, producing systematically different generations across runs/providers that get compared on one leaderboard. M3 (probabilistic provider routing, High): no `order`/`only`/`sort` for the overwhelming majority of test/judge models in both repos — repeat runs of the same nominal model can hit different providers, undermining run-to-run leaderboard stability, which is explicitly one of Judgemark-v2's headline measured properties ("Stability & Separability Metrics"). M4 (no provenance logging, High): confirmed no code path anywhere stores which provider served a completion or a judge score (only the message content is kept); a leaderboard entry cannot be traced back to, or reproduced against, the specific backend that produced it. M5 (data-policy leakage, Med): `data_collection` is never set, so it defaults to "allow" for every open-weight call, including third-party creative-writing text sent to judge models. M6 (model version drift, Med): both READMEs and CLI examples use bare undated slugs (e.g. `claude-sonnet-4-6`, `openai/gpt-4o-mini`, `your-model-provider/your-model-name`) with no snapshot pinning, so the served weights behind a leaderboard entry can silently drift over time. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound, High): both benchmarks' entire purpose is comparing many different models (test models on the CW leaderboard; ~20 judge models on the Judgemark-v2 leaderboard) against each other on a shared scale, while only 2 of those ~20+ models have any provider pin — differences in reported scores/rankings are confounded with which provider/quantization happened to serve each model, which is exactly the failure mode the taxonomy targets. M10 (no reporting, Med): neither README discloses to leaderboard consumers that OpenRouter's default load-balanced, unpinned routing is used, nor any caveat about quantization/provider variance affecting comparability — the "Limitations" section of the CW README covers judge subjectivity but not inference-stack variance.
rows.one_line_fix: Add `provider: {order: [...], allow_fallbacks: false, quantizations: ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], require_parameters: true, data_collection: "deny"}` to every OpenRouter call in cwb/utils/api.py and jm2/utils/api.py (not just the two gpt-oss models), and persist the response's `provider`/generation-id alongside each stored score in creative_bench_runs.json / judgemark runs.json so every leaderboard entry is reproducible and traceable to the serving backend.
rows.audit_notes: The most interesting finding is the dead code in jm2/utils/api.py: blocks of `if False and judge_model == 'moonshotai/kimi-k2':` etc. that pin a provider (Novita/Chutes) with `allow_fallbacks: False` — clear evidence the author understood provider-pinning and had working code for it, but left it disabled for the vast majority of models and only activated it (in the newer version) for openai/gpt-oss-120b/20b specifically, presumably because those two were empirically flaky/inconsistent on default routing. That's a textbook case of "aware_partial": knows the mechanism, applies it reactively/selectively rather than systematically. Also notable: the random "seed" block prepended to judge prompts (`<RANDOM SEED PLEASE IGNORE>` + 80 random chars x5) is NOT a seed-determinism mistake (M7) — it's the opposite, deliberately injected entropy to measure judging variance under `--redo-judging`, which is a legitimate design choice, not a corrupting one.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned both repos fresh (github.com/EQ-bench/creative-writing-bench and github.com/EQ-bench/judgemark-v2, which is the 'jm2' repo the verdict abbreviates — confirmed by directory layout: utils/api.py, core/, results/stats/ match exactly) and every specific evidence claim checks out.

cwb/utils/api.py (creative-writing-bench, verified full 363-line file): line 297-303 is `requests.post(self.base_url, headers=self.headers, json=payload, ...)` with zero `provider` dict anywhere in the payload construction (lines 193-296) — grepped the whole repo for `provider`/`require_parameters`/`data_collection`/`quantizations`/`generation_id`/`response_format` and the only hit is the unrelated internal string label `self.provider` ('openai'/'anthropic') at line 98 of a debug-log summary function, never sent to the API. Line 310 `content = data["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]` is confirmed as the only field extracted from a successful response — no provider/id persisted anywhere in core/*.py (also grepped, zero hits). The min_p comment cited is real, at lines 229-232 (verdict said 228-232, immaterial offset): "Alternatively you could use openrouter which will automatically omit unsupported params" — the author explicitly treats OpenRouter's silent param-drop as a convenience, exactly as characterized. `.env.example` confirms OpenRouter is the *recommended default* for both TEST_API_URL and JUDGE_API_URL (not just an option), directly supporting the 'openrouter_use' framing. README.md confirms the bare undated slugs `your-model-provider/your-model-name` and `claude-sonnet-4-6` (leaderboard-canonical judge) at the cited lines, and its 'Limitations' section (lines 145-153) discusses judge subjectivity/cost/language scope but never routing, quantization, or inference-stack variance — M10 confirmed verbatim. Bonus corroboration not in the original evidence: the README's Elo-normalization step anchors the *entire leaderboard scale* to two unpinned open-weight models via OpenRouter (`deepseek/deepseek-r1` → 1500, `mistralai/ministral-3b` → 200), meaning provider/quantization drift on those two calls would silently shift every reported score on the public leaderboard — this strengthens M8/critical_route beyond what the original evidence already argued.

jm2/utils/api.py (judgemark-v2, verified full 260-line file): line 13 `BASE_URL = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_URL", "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions")` matches exactly. The `if False and judge_model == 'moonshotai/kimi-k2':` dead-code block pinning Chutes with `allow_fallbacks: False` is real (lines 163-179), as is a second disabled generic Novita-pin block (147-161) — direct proof the author built and then shelved provider-pinning machinery. The only *active* provider pin in the whole file is for `openai/gpt-oss-120b`/`openai/gpt-oss-20b` (lines 218-224, Fireworks, allow_fallbacks False) — verbatim match to the evidence. Only `res_json['choices'][0]['message']['content']` (line 237) is ever extracted — no provenance persisted. README.md line 48 documents `export OPENAI_API_URL="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions"` as the setup instruction, and line 66 documents `--judge-models "openai/gpt-4o,anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` multi-model ensembling with no mention of provider pinning/quantization/data-collection anywhere in the doc. results/stats/*.json contains deepseek-r1, llama-3.1-405b/8b, mistral-large, wizardlm-2-8x22b, liquid-lfm-7b as unpinned judge models exactly as cited (all present, zero `if False` block covers any of them).

Sanity checks against false accusation / false reassurance: M9 (judge on unconstrained route with response_format) is correctly *not* flagged — grepped both repos for `response_format`/`json_schema`, zero hits; judges are scored via free-text parsing, not structured output, so M9 doesn't apply and the verdict correctly omits it. M7 is correctly not flagged — the `<RANDOM SEED PLEASE IGNORE>` blocks (present in both repos, confirmed) are deliberately injected entropy to measure judging variance under `--redo-judging`, not a reproducibility claim; the audit_notes' framing of this as legitimate rather than corrupting is correct. M1 is correctly scoped only to open-weight test/judge models (not claimed against proprietary-only calls), avoiding a false accusation there. Position-bias (A/B swap) and length-truncation mitigations cited as safeguards are real and confirmed in core/elo.py (LENGTH_TRUNCATION_CHARS truncation at lines 167-169, 'reversed' direction matchups at lines 191-245) — genuinely orthogonal to routing safety and correctly described as such, not misused to justify leniency. No M12 (cheap/degraded route) or M11 (backend mixing within one run) evidence found; both correctly omitted (the `:free` suffixes found in model_name_subs.py are display-name substitutions for evaluated models, not researcher-chosen cheap routing). No exculpatory pinning/logging code was found that the verdict missed, and no misattributed evidence turned up. The verdict is accurate and well-supported; I concur with uses_safely=false, all 8 mistake IDs, severity high, confidence high.

rows.title: Hereditary Traits Distillation (Open Distillation of Hereditary Traits)
rows.summary: Arthur Conmy's repo studying whether safety-relevant traits (negative emotion, blackmail propensity, Chinese-topic censorship/lying) transfer from teacher to student models under naive SFT filtering, replicating/extending "Gemma Needs Therapy" (arXiv:2603.10011) and related agentic-misalignment work.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is used for (1) generating teacher rollouts that become SFT training data for the Tinker-trained student (scripts/generate_think_traces.py, 2000 Gemma-4-31B-IT traces), (2) direct multi-model behavioral comparisons feeding published figures — the 8-model "Section 2" emotional-distress replication (hereditary/run_openrouter.py) and a 25-open-weight-model blackmail-vs-parameter-count scan (scripts/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py -> post_figures/blackmail_openrouter_param_scan.png), and (3) the LLM judge/autorater (claude-sonnet-4, gemini-3-flash) that scores every generation for negative emotion, blackmail, or censorship/lying.
safeguards_present: order/only+allow_fallbacks=False (opt-in, target-generation only, in chinese_censorship_eval/scripts/eval.py, eval_zh.py, scripts/generate_qwen35_9b_20k.py)
safety-tooling response cache (reproducible replay of a given run, not provider pinning)
rows.mistakes: Fetched and read hereditary/run_openrouter.py, scripts/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py, scripts/plot_blackmail_openrouter_scan.py, scripts/generate_think_traces.py, scripts/audit_depressive_traces.py, chinese_censorship_eval/scripts/eval.py, and the pinned safety-tooling dependency's openrouter.py backend. Across the entire repo, `provider.quantizations` is never set (zero hits repo-wide) and `require_parameters` is never set (zero hits) -- M1/M2 apply wherever open-weight models are called (which is most of the repo: gemma, qwen, llama, olmo, gpt-oss, nemotron, glm, deepseek, kimi all called via OpenRouter). M3: hereditary/run_openrouter.py's raw httpx payload (lines 163-169) and scripts/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py's InferenceAPI call (lines 134-137) and scripts/generate_think_traces.py's teacher-trace generation (line 154, default provider="openrouter") set no order/only/sort -> default load-balanced routing with run-to-run provider drift. M4 is systemic and confirmed at the library level: I fetched safety-tooling's OpenRouterChatModel.__call__ (pinned commit ff26176c) and its LLMResponse never captures OpenRouter's `provider` field or generation id -- only model_id/completion/stop_reason/durations/cost are logged (add_response_to_prompt_file only serializes LLMResponse.to_dict()); run_openrouter.py's raw JSON response is likewise discarded down to `choices[0].message.content` (see _content(), line 146). So which backend actually served any call, anywhere in this repo, is never recorded. M8 (critical): scripts/plot_blackmail_openrouter_scan.py generates post_figures/blackmail_openrouter_param_scan.png, explicitly named in the discovery evidence, comparing blackmail rate (0%-100%) across 25 OpenRouter open-weight models to conclude "post-training lineage dominates params" (line 67-74) -- with zero provider/quantization control, so backend-driven variance (documented up to ~16pp per arXiv:2605.19537) is fully confounded with the claimed effect. hereditary/run_openrouter.py similarly compares 8 model families (gemma/gemini/qwen/olmo/llama/claude/gpt-oss) for the paper's "Section 2: cross-family elicitation" result (per README.md line 61) with no routing control. M11: the published post's headline transfer figures (confirmed via WebFetch of the AF post -- Fig 2/4/5/9) compare teacher rollouts generated via OpenRouter (force_provider="openrouter", scripts/generate_think_traces.py) against Tinker-trained-student rollouts served via force_provider="tinker" (a completely different inference stack) in the same figure, without disclosing the backend mismatch. M5: data_collection is never set to "deny" anywhere (zero hits), so prompts/completions -- including sensitive blackmail and censorship-topic content -- may go through data-collecting OpenRouter providers by default. Most severe threat: the teacher-trace generation (scripts/generate_think_traces.py) is a training-data-generation route, not just an eval -- if Gemma-4-31B-IT gets served at varying quantization/backend across the 2000-prompt run, the resulting SFT training set (and everything trained/reported downstream) is built on unpinned, unlogged inference, and this cannot be fixed retroactively since provenance was never recorded. The repo shows partial awareness: chinese_censorship_eval/scripts/eval.py and eval_zh.py DO restrict target generation to US-HQ providers via `provider: {"only": US_PROVIDERS, "allow_fallbacks": False}` (opt-in via --us-only flag) specifically to isolate training censorship from host-country guardrails -- but this is never extended to quantization control, is explicitly NOT applied to the autorater (comment: "NOT the Google autorater"), and is absent from every other OpenRouter call site in the repo, including the two routes that most directly threaten headline results (run_openrouter.py, blackmail_openrouter_scan.py, generate_think_traces.py).
rows.one_line_fix: Add provider={"order": [...validated provider...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny"} to every OpenRouter call (both the raw httpx payloads in run_openrouter.py/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py and the extra_body kwarg passed through safety-tooling elsewhere), and log the response's `provider` field + generation id alongside every stored completion -- the repo already has the right instinct (US-provider pinning in the censorship eval) but needs to extend it repo-wide, especially to the teacher-trace generation that becomes SFT training data.
rows.importance: Author is Arthur Conmy (ex-DeepMind mechanistic interpretability, ACDC/EAP), a prominent and well-cited AI safety researcher; posted July 2026 to Alignment Forum on a directly safety-relevant question (hereditary transfer of blackmail/misalignment/emotional-instability traits under SFT, and whether naive filtering removes them). Repo has ~30 report subfolders with real experimental artifacts and figures actively cited from the post.
rows.audit_notes: Interesting structural finding: the repo mixes two entirely different serving backends for the SAME comparison -- Tinker (for the SFT student, fixed local-ish weights) and OpenRouter (for the teacher and for cross-family baselines) -- and the headline post figures compare across that boundary without ever naming it as a confound. Also notable: the repo's OWN dependency choice (safety-tooling, pinned to a specific commit) was independently verified by reading its source at that exact commit -- it has literally no support for provider/quantization pinning or provenance capture on the OpenRouter backend, so even a careful caller using the "recommended" library gets none of these protections for free. The one place the author DID add provider restriction (US-HQ-only for the Chinese censorship eval) was motivated by a different concern (host-country guardrails, not quantization/reproducibility) which suggests real engineering care but a conceptual blind spot on the OpenRouter-specific quantization/routing-drift risk.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned github.com/ArthurConmy/hereditary (fresh clone, all cited files present) and github.com/safety-research/safety-tooling at the pinned commit ff26176c1b8c2fa16c76f465797b4d8ea6ef9812 (verified this exact hash is in pyproject.toml's inline comment and is a real commit: "deps: support transformers>=5 ... #162"). Also raw-fetched the actual Alignment Forum post text (not just the WebFetch summarizer, which I found gave a misleadingly reassuring first-pass answer — see below).

Every specific evidence claim in the verdict checks out line-for-line:
- hereditary/run_openrouter.py lines 163-169: exact httpx payload {model, messages, temperature, max_tokens, reasoning}, no provider/order/only/sort/require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection. Confirmed.
- scripts/blackmail_openrouter_scan.py lines 134-137: `api(..., force_provider="openrouter", temperature=1.0, ..., seed=1000+i, extra_body={"reasoning":...})` — no provider routing control. Confirmed.
- scripts/generate_think_traces.py line 154/180: `force_provider=(None if provider=="auto" else provider)`, default `--provider openrouter`. This is the SFT-training-data route; force_provider only selects backend (OpenRouter vs Tinker vs direct), not which upstream OpenRouter provider serves it — confirmed no quantization/order/only control anywhere.
- Repo-wide grep: `require_parameters`, `quantization`, `data_collection` → zero hits anywhere in the repo. `provider: {"only": ..., "allow_fallbacks": False}` appears in exactly the three files cited (chinese_censorship_eval/scripts/eval.py:134, eval_zh.py:135, scripts/generate_qwen35_9b_20k.py:76) and I confirmed eval.py's `--us-only` flag is opt-in (default off) and, per the code comment on line 133 ("NOT the Google autorater"), is passed only to `call()` for target generation and never to `autorate()`. Matches the verdict exactly.
- safety-tooling's OpenRouterChatModel.__call__ (safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py) and the LLMResponse pydantic model (safetytooling/data_models/inference.py:69-84) — I read both fully. LLMResponse has no `provider` or generation-id field at all (only model_id, completion, stop_reason, cost, generated_content, duration, api_duration, logprobs, safety_ratings, usage, etc.), and `__call__` never reads `response_data.provider`/`.id` from the OpenAI-SDK response object. M4 is fully confirmed at the library level exactly as claimed.
- M9 sanity check: grep for `response_format` repo-wide shows it is explicitly *avoided* (comment: "Anthropic/OpenRouter has no response_format; tool-calling is the only ..."), so M9 correctly does NOT apply — the verdict correctly omitted it.

On M11/M8, I went further than the original verdict and fetched the actual AF post prose (not just a figure list). This matters because my first WebFetch pass (using the small summarizer model) claimed "no figures compare OpenRouter output against Tinker output in the same chart" — that answer is WRONG. Reading the raw article text directly: Figure 2 explicitly plots the teacher's depression score next to the distilled-Qwen bars ("the lower depression score compared to the teacher in the figure"); Figure 4's caption context states Qwen's 0-1% blackmail rate is shown "against the teacher's 100%/93% in the same thinking+tool harness"; Figure 5 shows Gemma-teacher's ~100% blackmail rate alongside Nemotron-baseline (~5%) and Nemotron-distilled-student (~26%) bars. The teacher bars in all three come from OpenRouter (generate_think_traces.py / gemma-4-31b-it rollouts, force_provider="openrouter"); the student bars come from Tinker-trained checkpoints evaluated via a script (scripts/blackmail_eval_tinker_thinking_tool.py) whose own docstring states "Only the serving backend differs: Tinker sampling of the student's sampler weights ... instead of an OpenRouter reasoning channel" for what it calls an "apples-to-apples" comparison. So M11 is not just plausible but is *directly confirmed in the paper's own headline figures*, even more concretely than the original verdict's citation implied (Fig 9 is less clearly a teacher-vs-student comparison than 2/4/5, a minor overreach in the original citation, but the overall claim is correct and if anything understated).

Two corrections to the mistake_ids list, which I believe was under-inclusive:
1. **M2 was discussed in the "mistakes" narrative text but never added to mistake_ids** — a clear list/prose inconsistency. The evidence for M2 is solid: sampling params (temperature, max_tokens, seed) are passed on every call and `require_parameters` is never set anywhere in the repo (confirmed via grep), so OpenRouter is free to silently drop unsupported params on any provider. I added M2.
2. **M6 (model version drift) is well-supported but wasn't flagged.** The judge/classifier models that produce *every* headline score in the paper are undated OpenRouter aliases: `DEFAULT_JUDGE = "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` (run_openrouter.py) and `CLASSIFIER_MODEL = "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` (scripts/blackmail_eval.py) — explicitly used as a stand-in for the paper's dated `claude-sonnet-4-20250514`; similarly `AUTORATER = "google/gemini-3-flash-preview"` (chinese_censorship_eval). These can silently move to a different underlying snapshot over time, changing scoring calibration between the ~30 report runs without anyone knowing. Added M6.
3. **M10 (no reporting) is also supported.** I grepped README.md for provider/quantization/routing/reproducibility/inference-stack — zero hits. I directly fetched the AF post's raw text and confirmed it discusses OpenRouter only in a cost-accounting context ("$3 for 3M input...") and never mentions provider routing, quantization, or backend-mixing as a caveat/limitation anywhere, despite the post's own code (the docstring above) demonstrating clear internal awareness of the backend split. Added M10.

I found no false accusations: every specific file/line/claim I re-checked was accurate, and the LEGIT exemption (proprietary-only comparisons exempt from M1/M3/M8) does not rescue this repo since the flagged comparisons (run_openrouter.py's 8-model spread, the 25-model blackmail scan, and the teacher/student figures) are explicitly mixed proprietary+open-weight or open-weight-only. I also found no false reassurance in the reverse direction — if anything the original verdict slightly under-cited the strength of the M11 evidence (I found an explicit code-comment admission of the backend split) and omitted three additional real corruption/reporting channels (M2, M6, M10) that are well-supported by the same evidence base already gathered. The core uses_safely=false / severity=high / confidence=high call stands and is, if anything, conservative.
rows.evidence:
Strengthened citations beyond the original verdict:
1. safety-tooling LLMResponse (pinned commit ff26176c, safetytooling/data_models/inference.py:69-84) has literally no `provider`/generation-id field — fields are model_id, completion, stop_reason, cost, generated_content, audio_out, duration, api_duration, logprobs, safety_ratings, recitation_retries, api_failures, batch_custom_id, usage. OpenRouterChatModel.__call__ (safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py:240-399) never reads response_data.provider or .id. M4 fully confirmed at the library level.
2. Alignment Forum post raw text (fetched directly, not via a lossy summarizer) confirms Figures 2, 4, and 5 explicitly plot the OpenRouter-served teacher (gemma-3-27b-it / gemma-4-31b-it) alongside the Tinker-trained/served student in the SAME figure: "mirroring the lower depression score compared to the teacher in the figure" (Fig 2); "no blackmail transferred (0-1%... against the teacher's 100%/93% in the same thinking+tool harness): Figure 4"; Figure 5 shows teacher ~100%, Nemotron-baseline ~5%, Nemotron-distilled-student ~26% together. scripts/blackmail_eval_tinker_thinking_tool.py's own docstring states: "Only the serving backend differs: Tinker sampling of the student's sampler weights ... instead of an OpenRouter reasoning channel" for what it calls an "apples-to-apples" comparison — a first-party admission of the exact M11 confound. (Fig 9's inclusion in the original citation is a minor overreach — it more likely compares trained-vs-untrained Llama, both same backend — but this doesn't weaken the Fig 2/4/5 finding.)
3. Repo-wide grep confirms zero hits for require_parameters/quantization/data_collection, and confirms the only provider-pinning code (`provider: {"only": ..., "allow_fallbacks": False}`) exists in exactly 3 files (chinese_censorship_eval/scripts/eval.py:134, eval_zh.py:135, scripts/generate_qwen35_9b_20k.py:76), all opt-in and generation-only, matching the verdict's safeguards_present note exactly.
4. Additional finding (M2): sampling params (temperature/max_tokens/seed) are set on every call site inspected (run_openrouter.py, blackmail_openrouter_scan.py, generate_think_traces.py, chinese_censorship_eval/eval.py) with require_parameters never set — silent param-dropping risk repo-wide. This was narrated in the original "mistakes" text but omitted from mistake_ids; should be added.
5. Additional finding (M6): the judge/classifier models driving every headline number are undated OpenRouter aliases — `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4` (run_openrouter.py DEFAULT_JUDGE; scripts/blackmail_eval.py CLASSIFIER_MODEL) explicitly substituting for the paper's dated `claude-sonnet-4-20250514`, and `google/gemini-3-flash-preview` (chinese_censorship_eval AUTORATER) — both can silently drift to a different snapshot across the repo's ~30 report runs.
6. Additional finding (M10): grep of README.md for provider/quantization/routing/reproducibility/inference-stack returns zero hits; direct fetch of the AF post's raw text shows OpenRouter is mentioned only for cost accounting, never as a routing/quantization/reproducibility caveat — despite the repo's own code comments showing clear internal awareness of the backend-mixing issue.

rows.title: Inspect Evals (UK AISI eval collection)
rows.summary: A large, official UK AISI collection of Inspect-AI-based safety/capability benchmarks (persistbench, b3, abstention_bench, tac, manta, uccb, etc.), widely used across the AI-safety community; several of the evals use OpenRouter to run either the judge/grader model or the target model being scored, and check in comparison tables against each benchmark's original paper.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is the default backend for: (1) persistbench's LLM judge (DEFAULT_JUDGE_MODEL='openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking') AND its example target models (llama-3.3-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b) whose failure rates are compared to the paper's 95% CI in report_config.yaml/README; (2) abstention_bench's grader_model default and the target model used in its published 'Inspect Evals vs Paper' recall table; (3) b3's ALL_B3_MODELS panel ('models used to obtain results in the b3 paper') which routes ~15 open-weight models through OpenRouter alongside direct-API proprietary models; (4) tac's EVALUATION.md panel, which routes Gemini/GLM/DeepSeek through OpenRouter and compares their welfare-rate deltas against Anthropic/OpenAI models on native APIs; (5) manta and uccb, which are external/historical results tables that also route several target+judge models through OpenRouter.
safeguards_present: order+allow_fallbacks pinning (persistbench DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER only, src/inspect_evals/persistbench/scorers.py:37-40, order=['google-vertex'], allow_fallbacks=False)

quantizations allow-list (b3 ModelSpec.get_provider_kwargs, src/inspect_evals/b3/experiments/model_spec.py:99-109; quantizations=['fp32','bf16','fp16','fp8','fp4'] + allow_fallbacks=False for most OpenRouter models, but x-ai/mistral models get an empty {} i.e. no constraint at all)

manual/prompted JSON extraction instead of response_format schema (b3 judge_defense.py comment 'We use prompted structured output and extract the score'; persistbench scorers.py extract_json_from_response; abstention_bench uses model_graded_qa with a text grade_pattern) -- this avoids M9 specifically
disclosed (not silent) native-API vs OpenRouter backend mixing in tac/EVALUATION.md:21 ('Anthropic and OpenAI models ran on their native APIs; Gemini, GLM, and DeepSeek ran via OpenRouter')
persistbench README explicitly explains WHY the judge is pinned to google-vertex ('paper's judge calibration was performed against Vertex AI-hosted Kimi K2 Thinking, ensuring score consistency')
rows.mistakes: Despite one genuinely well-pinned call site (persistbench's judge), the repo's headline reported numbers mostly come from unpinned OpenRouter calls. (1) M1/M3, abstention_bench: grader_model default 'openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct' (src/inspect_evals/abstention_bench/abstention_bench.py:24) and the target model used for the published README 'Model Evaluation' table (src/inspect_evals/abstention_bench/README.md:122, --model openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct --temperature 0.8 --top-p 0.95) are passed to get_model with zero provider dict -- no quantizations, no order/only/sort -- so the Inspect-Evals-vs-Paper recall/F1 numbers in that table can silently reflect any provider/quantization OpenRouter load-balances to. (2) M1/M3/M8, persistbench: while the JUDGE is pinned, the TARGET models actually scored (openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct, openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b) are launched via bare '--model openrouter/...' with no provider kwarg (src/inspect_evals/persistbench/README.md 'Commands used' section; results checked into report_config.yaml compared against the paper's calibrated failure rates, e.g. llama-3.3-70b-instruct: 55.0). (3) M1/M3/M8, b3: ALL_B3_MODELS (src/inspect_evals/b3/experiments/constants.py, docstring: 'the list of models used to obtain results in the b3 paper') mixes direct-API proprietary models with ~15 OpenRouter-routed open-weight models; most get a quantization allow-list but grok/mistral entries get get_provider_kwargs()->{} (model_spec.py:100-107), i.e. fully unpinned, inside the same paper-reproduction comparison. (4) M1/M2/M3/M8, tac: EVALUATION.md's core 'validity check' (the neutral->ethical welfare-rate delta, e.g. 'This large, universal delta is the benchmark's validity check') is computed across a panel where DeepSeek/GLM/Gemini go through OpenRouter with a hardcoded GenerateConfig(max_tokens=16384, reasoning_effort='medium') (src/inspect_evals/tac/tac.py:55-57,100-102) and no provider dict at all, run alongside Anthropic/OpenAI native-API calls -- an asymmetric confound baked into the comparison. (5) M4/M5 repo-wide: grep -rn "require_parameters|data_collection" across the whole repo returns zero hits -- data_collection is never set to 'deny' anywhere, and no code stores which provider/generation id actually served a call; the two checked-in persistbench result JSONs (src/inspect_evals/persistbench/results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_*.json) record only the bare model slug in .eval.model, no provider or generation id. Net effect: none of the OpenRouter-derived numbers checked into READMEs/EVALUATION.md/report_config.yaml as paper-comparison results are reproducible or provider-pinned except the persistbench judge itself.
rows.one_line_fix: Extend the provider-pinning pattern already used correctly for persistbench's judge (order=[specific provider], allow_fallbacks=False) plus add require_parameters:true and data_collection:'deny' to every OpenRouter-routed TARGET/grader model in abstention_bench, tac, and b3's remaining unpinned entries (x-ai/mistral), and log the served provider/generation id into every checked-in result file.
rows.audit_notes: This is a monorepo of many independently-contributed evals with wildly inconsistent OpenRouter hygiene -- b3's model_spec.py and persistbench's judge config show someone on the team clearly understands the problem (quantization allow-lists, single-provider pinning with allow_fallbacks=False, manual JSON parsing instead of response_format to dodge M9), but that discipline wasn't applied consistently to target-model calls, which is exactly where it matters most since those are the numbers being diffed against each paper's published results. tac's own EVALUATION.md Limitations section is unusually candid about a related methodological gap (reasoning_effort pin landing after the reported panel ran) -- good research hygiene in one dimension, but the OpenRouter provider/quantization gap in the same table goes unmentioned. manta and uccb results are external/historical (manta's actual eval code lives in a separate upstream repo; uccb's evaluation.md is a legacy report from a script not checked into this repo) so I weighted them as secondary evidence rather than primary findings. uccb's own checked-in report flags Gemini 2.5 Pro's anomalously low 1.16/5.0 score and lists 'Model behavior differences via OpenRouter' as a candidate cause in Limitations, then recommends re-testing Gemini via 'Direct API access (non-OpenRouter)' -- a clear author-level suspicion that routing corrupted a result, never followed up on in the checked-in artifact.
rows.evidence: src/inspect_evals/persistbench/scorers.py:36-40 (DEFAULT_JUDGE_MODEL='openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking', DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER={'order':['google-vertex'],'allow_fallbacks':False} -- good, but only for the judge); src/inspect_evals/persistbench/README.md 'Commands used' + report_config.yaml (target models openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct, openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b run with bare --model, no provider dict, compared to paper's calibrated failure rates); src/inspect_evals/abstention_bench/abstention_bench.py:24 (grader_model='openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct', no provider kwarg) and README.md:122 (--model openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct --temperature 0.8 --top-p 0.95, feeding the 'Inspect Evals vs Paper' table); src/inspect_evals/b3/experiments/constants.py:1,33-68 ('list of models used to obtain results in the b3 paper', ~15 openrouter/* entries) and model_spec.py:99-116 (get_provider_kwargs: quantizations=['fp32','bf16','fp16','fp8','fp4'] for most, but {} i.e. nothing for x-ai/mistral); src/inspect_evals/tac/tac.py:55-57,100-102 (GenerateConfig(max_tokens=16384, reasoning_effort='medium'), no provider dict) and EVALUATION.md:15-21,'Results' tables (Gemini/GLM/DeepSeek via OpenRouter compared against Anthropic/OpenAI native-API models in the same delta table); repo-wide `grep -rn "require_parameters|data_collection"` = 0 hits; persistbench results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_openai_gpt-oss-120b.json .eval.model field present but no provider/generation-id field anywhere in the file.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals at HEAD and independently re-fetched every file cited in evidence. Every specific claim checked out almost verbatim:

1. persistbench/scorers.py:36-40 — confirmed exactly: DEFAULT_JUDGE_MODEL="openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking", DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER={"order":["google-vertex"],"allow_fallbacks":False}. This is a genuinely good, narrow safeguard — but it only covers the judge (_get_judge(), lines 109-122).

2. persistbench/README.md lines 166-183 — confirmed: the target models actually compared to the paper's 95% CI (llama-3.3-70b-instruct FR=54.0 vs paper 55.0 [45,65]; gpt-oss-120b etc.) are launched via bare `--model openrouter/...` with zero provider dict. The checked-in result JSONs (verified both files directly, ~7KB summary rollups) contain `.eval.model` = bare slug and no "provider" or "generation_id" string anywhere in the entire file — grep on the raw JSON text returned False/False. M4 fully substantiated.

3. abstention_bench/abstention_bench.py:24 and utils.py:198-215 — confirmed grader_model default `openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct` passed straight into `model_graded_qa(model=grader_model)` with no provider kwarg anywhere in the call chain. README.md lines 123/189 confirm the identical unpinned slug is also the TARGET model whose recall/F1 feeds the "Inspect Evals vs Paper" table (GSM8K 0.911 vs paper 0.93, MMLUMath 0.511 vs 0.65, GPQA 0.575 vs 0.53) — both the judge and the graded model are unpinned on the same critical comparison.

4. b3/experiments/constants.py:1-68 and model_spec.py:99-116 — confirmed docstring "list of models used to obtain results in the b3 paper," ~15 openrouter/* entries, and get_provider_kwargs() returning `{}` (fully unconstrained) specifically for any model string containing "x-ai" or "mistral" (grok-4, grok-4-fast, grok-3, magistral-medium x2, mistral-medium-3.1 — 6 entries), vs. a quantization allow-list + allow_fallbacks=False for the rest. Note even the "protected" entries only floor the quantization and never pin `order`/`only` to a specific provider, so M3 (probabilistic routing) still applies to literally all of them — the verdict's "partial safeguard" framing is accurate, not overstated.

5. tac/tac.py:55-58,100-103 — confirmed GenerateConfig(max_tokens=16384, reasoning_effort="medium") with no provider dict, for both `tac` and `tac_welfare`. tac/scorer.py confirms the scorer is rule-based (inspects `purchase_tickets` tool-call arguments), not an LLM judge, so M9 correctly does not apply here — the risk is purely to the target/agent model under test. EVALUATION.md:18-26 confirms disclosed (not silent) Anthropic/OpenAI-native vs Gemini/GLM/DeepSeek-via-OpenRouter mixing, and the Limitations section (line 134) discloses only the reasoning_effort-pin timing gap, never the provider/quantization gap — matches the "candid on one dimension, silent on the OpenRouter dimension" characterization exactly.

6. Repo-wide `grep -rn "require_parameters|data_collection"` — I reran this myself across the full checkout: zero hits, confirmed.

7. `response_format|response_schema|json_schema` grep across tac/b3/abstention_bench/persistbench/uccb — zero hits, confirming M9 is correctly NOT flagged (b3's judge_defense.py:245 comment "We use prompted structured output and extract the score" and abstention_bench's text `grade_pattern` both independently verified).

8. uccb/uccb_evaluation_results/evaluation.md — confirmed the Gemini 2.5 Pro 1.16/5.0 anomaly, "Model behavior differences via OpenRouter" listed as a candidate cause, and a recommendation to retest via "Direct API access (non-OpenRouter)" — exactly as described in audit_notes, and confirmed this file is a checked-in historical report (not live eval code), supporting the "secondary evidence" weighting. manta's README confirms it's genuinely an external upstream-hosted eval (register/manta/README.md: "External evaluation. Code lives in an upstream repository").

No false accusations found: no proprietary single-served model (openai/*, anthropic/* on native API, google/* direct) is wrongly taged M1/M3/M8 — the verdict is careful to scope those IDs to the OpenRouter-routed subset only, and correctly treats tac's disclosed native/OpenRouter split as a legitimate M8 cross-provider confound rather than mislabeling it M11 (silent mixing), since it IS disclosed. No missed corruption channel found either: M9 was correctly not applied (verified no response_format/schema usage anywhere in these five evals), and M7/M6/M10/M11/M12 are all correctly absent from mistake_ids given what the code actually does (seed is used only for dataset shuffling, not treated as generation-determinism; tac's mixing is disclosed not silent). The one arguable soft spot is that M6 (bare undated slug) could plausibly have been added given all the openrouter/* slugs are undated — but this is a Med-severity omission that doesn't change severity=high or uses_safely=false, and Meta/Moonshot/etc. don't publish dated OpenRouter variants the way OpenAI/Anthropic do, so it's a defensible exclusion rather than an error.

Verdict stands unmodified: uses_safely=false, severity=high, confidence=high, mistake_ids=[M1,M2,M3,M4,M5,M8] all independently substantiated with re-fetched, line-accurate evidence.

rows.title: JudgeArena (OpenEuroLLM)
rows.summary: JudgeArena is a benchmarking framework (Alpaca-Eval, Arena-Hard, M-Arena-Hard, MT-Bench) with swappable LLM judges — self-hosted vLLM or remote (OpenAI, Together, OpenRouter) — that reuses tuned judge configurations from the ICML 2025 paper "Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost" (Salinas, Swelam, Hutter).
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is one of the swappable backends for both the judge model and the models being compared. When `--judge.model OpenRouter/...` (or `--model.baseline OpenRouter/...`) is used, `judgearena/models.py:make_model()` instantiates a `langchain_openai.ChatOpenAI` pointed at `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` and runs the pairwise LLM-as-judge battles whose output (Bradley-Terry/Elo win rates) is the tool's core reported result. It is the default judge in the repo's own example configs (configs/alpaca_eval.yaml, configs/elo_comparia.yaml).
rows.venue: ICML 2025 (Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost)
rows.importance: Small repo (13 stars) but maintained under OpenEuroLLM, a large EU Horizon-Europe/EuroHPC consortium (~20 institutions), and explicitly reuses tuned judge configs from an ICML 2025 paper co-authored by Frank Hutter (highly cited AutoML/ML researcher). It reimplements four standard LLM-judge benchmarks and is positioned as the reference tool for tuned-judge evaluation, so its default OpenRouter path could shape downstream Elo/win-rate numbers others cite.
rows.evidence: judgearena/models.py:639-657 — the entire OpenRouter branch of make_model(): `if model_provider == "OpenRouter": ... return ChatOpenAI(api_key=os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY"), base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1", model=model_name, **openai_kwargs)` where openai_kwargs only ever contains temperature/top_p/seed/model_kwargs (via _route_sampling_params, models.py:521-560) — no `extra_body`/`provider` dict is ever constructed. A repo-wide grep (`grep -rInE "require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection|extra_body|provider\s*[:=]" .`) returns zero hits anywhere in the codebase. Sampling params (temperature/top_p/top_k/seed) ARE forwarded to the OpenRouter call (models.py:644-651) with no require_parameters, so they can be silently dropped by a non-supporting provider (M2). README.md:58: quick-start example explicitly notes it "Compares two models using `deepseek-chat-v3.1` which the cheapest option available on `OpenRouter`" and README.md:200-201 shows `--model.baseline OpenRouter/qwen/qwen-2.5-7b-instruct --judge.model OpenRouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1` — i.e. both the compared model and the judge can be unpinned OpenRouter routes simultaneously (M8). configs/alpaca_eval.yaml:10 and configs/elo_comparia.yaml:9 default `judge.model: OpenRouter/google/gemma-4-31b-it`. judgearena/repro.py:224-293 (write_run_metadata) is a genuinely careful reproducibility system — captures git hash, dependency versions, prompt SHA-256 hashes, dataset stats — but never records which OpenRouter provider actually served a call or any generation id (M4), and the underlying paper's own abstract states it "utilize[s] open-weight models, ensuring greater accessibility and reproducibility" — an intent undercut by the unpinned OpenRouter path. tests/test_utils.py:111-138 tests only that vLLM-only kwargs are stripped before reaching ChatOpenAI; no test exercises provider/require_parameters/data_collection.
rows.mistakes: M1 (no `quantizations` anywhere): open-weight judge/candidate models like deepseek-chat-v3.1, qwen3-32b, and gemma-* served via OpenRouter can silently run at any quantization from int4 up, changing judge scoring behavior and thus the reported win-rate/Elo without any signal. M2 (no `require_parameters`): temperature/top_p/top_k/seed are explicitly passed to the OpenRouter call, but nothing forces the served provider to honor them — the paper's methodology tunes exactly these knobs (temperature, sampling), so silent dropping directly undermines the "tuned judge configuration" premise the repo advertises. M3 (no `order`/`only`/`sort`): default load-balanced routing means two runs of the same judge.model string can hit different providers/weights, so Elo ratings/win-rates are not run-to-run reproducible — directly threatens any published battle-result table generated with this tool. M4 (no provenance logging): despite an unusually thorough run-metadata system (git hash, dependency pins, prompt hashes), the actual served OpenRouter provider/generation id is never captured, so a surprising result can never be traced to "which backend served it." M5 (data_collection left default "allow"): prompts/completions sent to the judge may be retained/trained-on by whichever provider is selected, with no opt-out set. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the documented CLI pattern compares a `VLLM/...` or `OpenRouter/...` baseline against another model using an `OpenRouter/...` judge, with none of the OpenRouter legs pinned — any Elo delta between models can be partly attributable to which provider/quantization happened to serve each leg that run, not a genuine capability difference.
rows.one_line_fix: In judgearena/models.py's OpenRouter branch of make_model() (~line 652), add `model_kwargs={"extra_body": {"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "require_parameters": True, "quantizations": ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], "data_collection": "deny"}}}` and thread the response's actual `provider`/generation id into write_run_metadata's `run` dict in judgearena/repro.py.
rows.audit_notes: Notable irony: judgearena/repro.py implements a genuinely careful reproducibility system (SHA-256 hashes of prompts, dataset indices, git commit, dependency version pinning) — real engineering care went into reproducibility — yet the one dimension of nondeterminism unique to router-served models (which provider/quantization actually ran) is completely absent from both the call site and the metadata schema. The cited paper's own abstract emphasizes open-weight models "ensuring greater accessibility and reproducibility," which the OpenRouter path as implemented does not actually deliver. Also worth flagging separately from the OpenRouter taxonomy: README.md:58 frames picking the "cheapest option available on OpenRouter" as a feature of the quick-start demo — not a `:floor`/`sort:price` routing directive (so not literally M12), but it signals a cost-first rather than reproducibility-first default mindset for a tool meant to produce comparative Elo ratings.
rows.verifier_reasoning: Re-cloned OpenEuroLLM/JudgeArena (github.com/OpenEuroLLM/JudgeArena, depth 1) and verified every cited artifact byte-for-byte. judgearena/models.py:639-657 is exactly the OpenRouter branch: `if model_provider == "OpenRouter": ... return ChatOpenAI(api_key=os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY"), base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1", model=model_name, **openai_kwargs)` — openai_kwargs is built solely via `_route_sampling_params` (models.py:521-560, exact line match), which only ever sets temperature/top_p/seed/model_kwargs['top_k']; no `extra_body` or `provider` dict is constructed anywhere. A repo-wide grep for `require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection|extra_body|provider\s*[:=]` returns zero hits except unrelated internal `model_provider`/`provider` string variables (e.g. 'VLLM','OpenRouter' literals) — confirms M1/M2/M3/M5 mechanism exactly as claimed. README.md:58 is an exact quote ('Compares two models using `deepseek-chat-v3.1` which the cheapest option available on `OpenRouter`') and README.md:200-201 exactly shows `--model.baseline OpenRouter/qwen/qwen-2.5-7b-instruct --judge.model OpenRouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3.1` — both legs unpinned OpenRouter, confirming M8. configs/alpaca_eval.yaml:10 and configs/elo_comparia.yaml:9 exactly default `judge.model: OpenRouter/google/gemma-4-31b-it` — all three OpenRouter-routed models cited (deepseek-chat-v3.1, qwen-2.5-7b-instruct, gemma-4-31b-it) are open-weight, so the LEGIT exception for proprietary single-served models does not apply and M1/M3/M8 are correctly in-scope (not a false-accusation risk). judgearena/repro.py:224-293 (write_run_metadata, confirmed exact line span — file is 293 lines, function ends exactly at line 293) captures git hash, dependency versions, SHA-256 prompt hashes, and dataset stats but the `run_metadata` dict built in evaluate.py:314-324 (and equivalent call sites in generate_and_evaluate.py, estimate_elo_ratings.py) contains no provider/generation-id field at all — confirms M4 exactly, including the "ironic" framing (careful reproducibility system with this one gap). tests/test_utils.py:111-138 (`test_make_model_openrouter_strips_vllm_only_kwargs`) is confirmed to test only that vLLM-only kwargs are stripped before reaching ChatOpenAI; no assertion touches provider/require_parameters/data_collection. Fetched the actual arXiv abstract (2501.17178) and confirmed the exact quoted phrase: "utilize open-weight models, ensuring greater accessibility and reproducibility." Sanity checks: (1) no response_format/JSON-schema usage found anywhere for judge output parsing (judge output is free-text parsed by a regex/score_parser) — so M9 is correctly NOT flagged, no over-claiming there; (2) M10 correctly excluded since the README does explicitly disclose the OpenRouter/vLLM/OpenAI/Together swappable-backend architecture (just not the routing-safety gap); (3) checked M6 candidacy (bare undated slugs) — model strings do carry version tokens (v3.1, 2.5, gemma-4) so this is a defensible, non-critical omission, not an error; (4) checked M7 candidacy — config.py docstrings for seed fields say "when supported" (hedged) rather than asserting guaranteed determinism, so omitting M7 is reasonable, not a false-reassurance gap. Checked GitHub issues (gh issue list, 14 issues) for any sign the maintainers are aware of provider-pinning/quantization/reproducibility-of-routing concerns — none found, consistent with "awareness: unaware". Confirmed Bradley-Terry/Elo computation (judgearena/estimate_elo_ratings.py: fit_bradley_terry, EloReport) is the tool's actual core output, supporting critical_route:true. No evidence of any safeguard (no order/only/sort, no require_parameters, no quantizations, no data_collection setting) anywhere in the codebase. The verdict is fully justified: no false accusation (all six M-ids have precise, reproducible code citations; open-weight models correctly make M1/M3/M8 applicable) and no false reassurance (no missed M9; the one arguably-missable item, M6, is a defensible non-critical omission given partial version tokens in the model slugs).

rows.title: METR RE-Bench task suite (inspect-tasks-public) — nanogpt_chat_rl LLM-judge scoring
rows.summary: Public Inspect-framework port of METR's RE-Bench, a 7-task benchmark comparing AI R&D agents to human experts, widely cited in frontier-model system cards. One of the seven canonical tasks (ai_rd_nanogpt_chat_rl) uses an LLM judge to score the agent's finetuned chatbot against reference models, and that judge is called through OpenRouter.
rows.openrouter_use: The task's official score (overall_win_rate) is computed by an LLM-judge call: `evaluation.py`'s `_generate()` calls `client.chat.completions.create(model="meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct", ...)` where `client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()` is pointed at OpenRouter via env vars `OPENAI_API_KEY=$OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` (set in task_nanogpt_chat_rl.py). This judge output is the pairwise-preference signal that `score.py` aggregates into `overall_win_rate`, which is logged via `scoring.log_score(...)` as the task's official, reported score (compared against `gold_standard_score=0.8149`).
rows.mistakes: The OpenRouter call in evaluation.py has zero routing safeguards, and its output IS the reported metric for this RE-Bench task. (1) M1/M6: model="meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct" (evaluation.py:83) is a bare, undated, open-weight slug with no `quantizations` pin anywhere in the repo — Llama-3-8B-Instruct is served by many OpenRouter providers at varying precision, so the judge's actual weights are unspecified. (2) M3: the client is plain `openai.AsyncOpenAI()` (evaluation.py:149) with no `extra_body`/`provider` dict at all — confirmed via full-repo grep, zero hits for `provider`/`extra_body`/`require_parameters`/`quantizations`/`data_collection` outside README/task-key-plumbing — so every judge call is load-balanced across providers by OpenRouter's default (price-weighted) policy. (3) M2: sampling params `temperature=0.0, max_tokens=100` (evaluation.py:119-120) are passed with no `require_parameters:true`, so a provider that ignores temperature could silently return different judge behavior. (4) M4: neither evaluation.py nor score.py ever reads/stores the response's `provider` field or a generation id — score.py logs only `score_time` and per-model win rates (score.py:123-129), so which backend/quantization actually produced a given win-rate is unrecoverable after the fact. (5) M5: `data_collection` defaults to "allow", meaning the held-out benchmark prompts and reference/agent completions are sent to third-party inference providers who may train on them — this directly contradicts the repo's own README license notice ("Avoiding providing evaluation material to APIs or services that may use the material for training", README.md:28-29). (6) M8: because the judge backend is unpinned and unlogged, comparisons across different evaluation runs/agents/models scored at different times on this task (the comparisons RE-Bench exists to support — AI agent vs. human expert, model vs. model uplift) are confounded by potential judge-quantization drift between runs. (7) M10: the RE-Bench paper (arXiv 2411.15114) names "llama-3-8b-instruct" as the judge but never mentions OpenRouter, provider routing, quantization, or any reproducibility safeguard for the judge model; the repo README only instructs users to set `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` with no discussion of routing risk. This threatens the official per-task score used for cross-agent/cross-model AI-R&D-uplift comparisons that RE-Bench is designed to produce and that downstream system cards cite.
rows.one_line_fix: In evaluation.py's client.chat.completions.create call, add `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [<validated provider>], "allow_fallbacks": False, "require_parameters": True, "quantizations": ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], "data_collection": "deny"}}`, and log the response's `provider`/generation id alongside each win-rate in score.py's logged message.
rows.importance: RE-Bench is METR's flagship AI-R&D-uplift benchmark, cited in frontier-model system cards (OpenAI o1/o3, Anthropic, Google DeepMind) for dangerous-capability risk assessment; METR is one of the most influential independent AI evaluation organizations, and this repo is METR's own public Inspect-framework port of that benchmark suite.
rows.evidence: re_bench/nanogpt_chat_rl/ai_rd_nanogpt_chat_rl/task_nanogpt_chat_rl.py:35,59-64 (model_name="llama-3-8b-instruct"; OPENAI_API_KEY=os.environ["OPENROUTER_API_KEY"], OPENAI_BASE_URL="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1") · sandbox/assets/evaluation.py:82-86,149 (`completion = await client.chat.completions.create(model="meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct", messages=messages, **kwargs)` with `client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()`, kwargs only temperature/max_tokens, no provider/extra_body anywhere) · sandbox/assets/score.py:106-129,172-180 (judge output -> overall_win_rate -> scoring.log_score as official task score) · full-repo grep for openrouter|require_parameters|quantization|data_collection|extra_body|provider returned only the two env-var lines above and README.md:53 · arXiv 2411.15114 HTML confirms paper names "llama-3-8b-instruct" as judge but never mentions OpenRouter/provider routing/quantization.
rows.audit_notes: Notable irony: the repo's own README (line 28-29) warns users to avoid "providing evaluation material to APIs or services that may use the material for training" as an anti-contamination measure, yet the actual nanogpt_chat_rl implementation sends held-out benchmark prompts/completions through OpenRouter's default data_collection:"allow" routing to unspecified third-party providers — the codebase violates its own stated data-hygiene policy via the unconfigured router call. Also worth flagging upstream: this is a port ("still a WIP" per README) of METR's original Vivaria-based RE-Bench; the original implementation was not independently checked, so this finding is specific to the Inspect port but the same task design (LLM judge scoring, described identically in the paper) likely originated there too.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned github.com/METR/inspect-tasks-public (depth 1) and re-fetched every file cited in 'evidence'. All citations check out verbatim, down to exact line numbers: (1) task_nanogpt_chat_rl.py:35 `model_name = "llama-3-8b-instruct"`; lines 59-64 build `api_config = {"OPENAI_API_KEY": os.environ["OPENROUTER_API_KEY"], "OPENAI_BASE_URL": "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"}` written to env_keys.json for the sandbox. (2) evaluation.py:82-86 confirmed: `completion = await client.chat.completions.create(model="meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct", messages=messages, **kwargs)` — a bare undated slug (M6). Line 149: `client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()` with zero `extra_body`/`provider` dict anywhere in the file — confirmed M3 (no order/only/sort) and M1 (no quantizations pin). kwargs passed into the call are only `temperature=0.0, max_tokens=100` (evaluate_prompt, lines 118-121) with no `require_parameters:true` anywhere — confirmed M2. (3) score.py:106-129 confirmed: the judge's boolean preference outputs are aggregated into `win_rates`/`overall_win_rate`, and the `message` dict logged (lines 123-129) contains only `score_time` and `win_vs_<model>` fields — no provider name or generation id is ever captured, confirming M4. Lines 172-180 confirm `scoring.log_score(**(eval_result | {"timestamp": timestamp}))` is the official/gated score path (guarded by `check_scoring_group`), confirming critical_route=true. (4) A full-repo grep for `openrouter|require_parameters|quantization|data_collection|extra_body|provider` returned only the env-var lines, evaluation.py's stderr string, and README.md:53 — plus two harmless false positives (test_names.py's OPENROUTER_API_KEY dummy-env fixture, devcontainer.json's unrelated `python.formatting.provider` key) that don't change the substance. This confirms M5 (data_collection never set, defaults to OpenRouter's 'allow'). (5) README.md:28 verbatim: 'Avoiding providing evaluation material to APIs or services that may use the material for training' — directly contradicted by routing held-out benchmark prompts/completions through an unconfigured OpenRouter call, exactly as the audit_notes claims. (6) WebFetched the arXiv HTML (2411.15114): confirmed the paper names 'llama-3-8b-instruct' as the judge ('A large language model judge, in this case "llama-3-8b-instruct", compares two responses...') but contains no mention of OpenRouter, provider routing, quantization, or reproducibility safeguards — confirming M10.

Sanity checks: model_type=open_weight is correct (Llama-3-8B-Instruct is genuinely multi-provider on OpenRouter), so M1/M3/M8 are NOT wrongly applied to a proprietary/single-served model. M9 does not apply and was correctly omitted — the code never passes `response_format`/JSON-schema as an API kwarg; JSON compliance is requested only via prompt text and parsed with a regex fallback (evaluation.py:127-132), so there's no unconstrained-structured-output parameter to guard with require_parameters (already covered by M2/M3). M7 correctly omitted: no `seed` parameter is ever passed to the OpenRouter call (only temperature/max_tokens); the `torch.manual_seed`/`random.seed` calls in score.py govern only local Python randomness (prompt-pair sampling order), not API-level determinism. M11/M12 correctly omitted: no evidence of the same model being called both directly and via router, and no explicit `:floor`/price-sort route selection (the risk here is pure default-routing absence, i.e. M3, not an intentional cheap route). The M8 application is a slightly non-classical reading (only one model — the judge — actually transits OpenRouter; the 'compared conditions' are AI agents/human-expert runs across time, not two OpenRouter-routed models compared side by side within one call) but the underlying mechanism is real and correctly reasoned: because the judge backend/quantization is unpinned and never logged, RE-Bench's core cross-run AI-R&D-uplift comparisons (which the paper and system cards rely on) are confounded by unrecorded judge drift between evaluation runs — this is a legitimate, if broader, application of M8's intent and should not be struck. No false accusation and no missed corruption channel found; the verdict is accurate and well-evidenced.

rows.title: Multi-hop / no-CoT latent reasoning experiments (Ryan Greenblatt)
rows.summary: A capability-elicitation study measuring whether frontier LLMs can do 2/3/4-hop 'latent' (no-Chain-of-Thought) reasoning on natural-fact composition questions; headline result is Gemini 3 Pro scoring 60% on 2-hop and 34% on 3-hop questions with no CoT.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter (via an AsyncOpenAI client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1) is the sole backend for all Gemini (2.5 Pro, 3 Pro Preview) and all open-weight model calls (DeepSeek-v3/v3.2, Qwen3 variants, Kimi-K2) used in the eval — including a hacky 'prefill' trick (an assistant-role 'Answer:' message, plus an extra_body reasoning-disable param for Gemini) used specifically to suppress chain-of-thought and force the no-CoT measurement that is the core of the post.
rows.evidence: eval_multi_hop.py:47-50 openrouter_client = AsyncOpenAI(api_key=..., base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1'); :71-81 OPENROUTER_MODELS = {deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324, deepseek/deepseek-v3.2, qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b, qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507, qwen/qwen3-coder, qwen/qwen3-32b, moonshotai/kimi-k2, google/gemini-2.5-pro, google/gemini-3-pro-preview} (mix of undated slugs, e.g. 'qwen/qwen3-coder', 'deepseek/deepseek-v3.2'); :552-573 cache_key dicts built with only model/messages/temperature/extra_body (reasoning) — no 'provider' key anywhere in file (confirmed same absence in eval_addition.py, grep for 'provider' finds none besides comments); :616-654 openrouter_client.chat.completions.create(**cache_key, temperature=0.0) — no order/only/sort/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection args; response_cache.py:70-77 ResponseCache.set stores only {'response': response_text} — response.provider / response.id (OpenRouter's per-call served-provider field) is never read or persisted anywhere in the repo. write_up.md:27-33 shows partial awareness of ONE OpenRouter-specific risk (the 'reasoning' field sometimes not being returned for Gemini) with a retry-based mitigation, but no mention of provider routing, quantization, or provider pinning for the open-weight models. run_all_evals.py:11-19 shows Anthropic-direct (opus-4-5, opus-4, sonnet-4, sonnet-4-5, haiku-3-5) and OpenAI-direct (gpt-4, gpt-4.1, gpt-5.1, gpt-5.2) models compared head-to-head in the same 'all_models_2_3_hop.png' chart against OpenRouter-routed, unpinned qwen3-235b/deepseek-v3/gemini models.
rows.importance: Author is Ryan Greenblatt, Chief Scientist at Redwood Research and one of the most-cited/influential voices on AI control and capability elicitation on LessWrong/Alignment Forum; this post is a widely-discussed capability-elicitation result bearing on AI safety timelines.
rows.mistakes: M1 (unpinned quantization): DeepSeek/Qwen/Kimi-K2 are open-weight models routed through OpenRouter with no provider/quantizations pin, so their reported no-CoT accuracy numbers (shown in 'all_models_2_3_hop.png' and the addition-task charts) could reflect a specific unrecorded provider's quantized serving rather than full-precision, and can drift silently run-to-run as OpenRouter's default routing changes which backend serves the model. M2 (silent parameter drop): the Gemini reasoning-disable extra_body param and temperature=0.0 are sent with no require_parameters:true, so a provider that doesn't support them could silently ignore them (the retry-on-detected-thinking loop is a partial functional mitigation for Gemini only, not for the open-weight models, and not a substitute for require_parameters). M3 (probabilistic routing): no order/only/sort anywhere, so every OpenRouter call is load-balanced across whichever providers serve that slug, undermining run-to-run comparability of the reported accuracy percentages. M4 (no provenance): response_cache.py never records which provider/backend actually served a given response, so it's impossible after the fact to audit or explain anomalies (e.g., the very Gemini reasoning-field issue the author flags as a ~10% risk to the headline number). M6 (version drift): several OpenRouter slugs are undated ('qwen/qwen3-coder', 'deepseek/deepseek-v3.2', 'google/gemini-3-pro-preview' is a 'preview' tag) so the underlying model can change under the same slug between the post's publication and any replication attempt. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the post's 'broader range of models' comparison chart puts directly-served, pinned Anthropic/OpenAI models side by side with unpinned, OpenRouter-routed open-weight and Gemini models, so cross-model capability differences are confounded with uncontrolled routing/serving differences on the OpenRouter side. The headline Gemini 3 Pro numbers (60%/34%) are entirely OpenRouter-sourced and thus carry the M3/M4/M6 risks even though Gemini's proprietary status makes M1 (quantization) low-risk for that specific model.
rows.one_line_fix: Add provider: {order: [...], allow_fallbacks: false, require_parameters: true} to every OpenRouter call and log response.provider/response.id into the cache and results JSON so served-backend and quantization are pinned and auditable.
rows.audit_notes: Notable: the author does show real engineering awareness of one specific OpenRouter failure mode (the reasoning field sometimes not returned for Gemini, footnote [^notesonreason]) and built a retry-until-no-thinking loop plus a <=20 completion-token cap as an empirical mitigation, arguing this bounds the risk to <10% overestimation for that one number. That's a genuinely thoughtful ad hoc safeguard for M2/M9-adjacent risk on the Gemini path specifically, but it doesn't touch M1/M3/M4/M6/M8 for the open-weight models or for cross-run reproducibility more broadly, and it isn't described in terms of OpenRouter's provider-routing model at all (no mention of 'provider', 'quantization', or pinning anywhere in the post or code). This is a good example of a sophisticated researcher noticing and partially patching one symptom of unpinned routing without recognizing the general root cause.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned https://github.com/rgreenblatt/multi_hop (depth 1) and directly inspected every file named in the evidence field. All substantive claims check out; only line-number boundaries were occasionally loose (never wrong in substance).

Confirmed exactly:
- eval_multi_hop.py:47-50 — `openrouter_client = AsyncOpenAI(api_key=..., base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1")`. Exact match.
- eval_multi_hop.py:71-81 — `OPENROUTER_MODELS = {"deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324", "deepseek/deepseek-v3.2", "qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b", "qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507", "qwen/qwen3-coder", "qwen/qwen3-32b", "moonshotai/kimi-k2", "google/gemini-2.5-pro", "google/gemini-3-pro-preview"}`. Exact match, confirms undated slugs (qwen3-coder, deepseek-v3.2, gemini-3-pro-preview) → M6.
- response_cache.py:70-77 — `ResponseCache.set` stores only `self.cache[cache_key] = response_data` where the caller passes `{"response": response_text}` (eval_multi_hop.py:692) — exact line match, no provider/generation-id field stored anywhere → M4.
- Repo-wide grep for "provider", "quantiz", "require_parameters", "data_collection" across all `.py` files returned **zero hits** — confirms no provider pin, no quantization pin, no require_parameters, matching M1/M2/M3.
- write_up.md:27-33 — verified verbatim: acknowledges the Gemini-reasoning-field OpenRouter risk with a footnote estimating <10% overestimation risk, but the word "provider"/"quantization"/"routing" never appears anywhere in write_up.md or README.md — confirms `awareness: aware_partial` and correctly excludes M10 (partial disclosure of *an* inference-stack risk, just not the routing/quantization one).
