- run_all_evals.py:12 — `MODELS = ["opus-4-5", "opus-4", "sonnet-4", "sonnet-4-5", "haiku-3-5", "qwen3-235b", "gpt-4", "gpt-4.1", "gpt-5.1", "gpt-5.2", "deepseek-v3"]`, and analyze_results.py:1264-1384 (`plot_all_models_2_3_hop`, `EXTENDED_PLOT_MODEL_ORDER`) confirms the exact chart (`eval_results/all_models_2_3_hop.png`) plots Anthropic-direct + OpenAI-direct models on the same axes as OpenRouter-routed Qwen3-235B/DeepSeek-V3/Gemini-2.5/3-Pro with no pinning distinction shown → M8 fully substantiated.
- eval_addition.py mirrors eval_multi_hop.py exactly (same `openrouter_client`, same `OPENROUTER_MODELS`, same bare `cache_key` construction, same absence of "provider") — corroborates the evidence's claim that eval_addition.py shares the same gap.

Two evidence line-ranges were loosely drawn but not wrong: "552-573" spans the Gemini cache_key dict (552-564) plus an adjacent, unrelated OpenAI-direct gpt-5 branch (565-573); "616-654" spans both the Gemini-branch OpenRouter call (615-620) and the non-Gemini-branch OpenRouter call with `temperature=0.0` (647-654). Both are minor sloppiness in citation precision, not fabrication — the underlying claim ("no provider/order/sort/quantizations/require_parameters args anywhere in these calls") is fully verified true by the repo-wide grep.

Sanity checks for missed/over-called mistakes:
- M9 (judge on unconstrained route): grepped for `response_format`, `json_schema`, `judge`, `grader` — zero hits. Correctly not flagged; this isn't an LLM-judge pipeline, it's exact-match string grading (`check_answer`/`normalize_answer`) done locally in Python.
- M7 (seed→determinism): grepped for `seed=`/`"seed"` in API-call contexts — none found; `seed_for_n`/`random.Random(42)` are local Python RNG seeds for few-shot/problem selection, not API `seed` params. Correctly excluded.
- M5 (data_collection): unverifiable from repo (account-level setting), correctly left unflagged rather than asserted.
- M11/M12: no evidence of mixed direct/router calls for the *same* model label, and no `:floor`/price-sort routing found. Correctly excluded.
- No proprietary-single-served-model false-positive: Gemini is explicitly still routed through OpenRouter (in `GEMINI_MODELS ⊆ OPENROUTER_MODELS`), so M1/M3/M8 legitimately apply to it too (the verdict already correctly notes M1 is lower-risk for Gemini specifically due to proprietary status, without dropping M3/M4/M6/M8 for it).
- Critical route: confirmed — the post's headline numbers (Gemini 3 Pro 60%/34%) and the cross-model comparison chart both depend entirely on the unpinned OpenRouter path, so `critical_route: true` and `severity: high` are justified.

No corrections needed. The verdict's safe/unsafe call, mistake IDs, severity, and confidence all survive adversarial re-verification against the live repository.

rows.title: Nous Research — Autoreason (self-refinement with AI-judge panels)
rows.summary: A research report proposing 'autoreason', a tournament-style self-refinement method (critic/author-B/synthesizer agents + blind Borda-count judge panels) for iterative document/code revision, with headline claims about a U-shaped 'capability curve' where the method's advantage peaks at mid-tier models.
rows.importance: 591 GitHub stars, authored by Nous Research (Hermes/DisTrO/Forge lab, large open-source/alignment following). Directly on-topic for RLAIF: synthetic revision generation + multi-model AI-judge Borda voting used to select/label winning outputs, feeding a headline paper result.
rows.openrouter_use: litellm's `openrouter/` prefix (via OPENROUTER_API_KEY, confirmed at experiments/v2/run_small_vs_big.py:47) is used for two purposes: (1) generation — running author/critic/synthesizer agents on Llama-3.1-8B-instruct, Gemini-2.0/2.5-Flash, and Claude-3.5-Haiku as the 'cheap' model tiers in the 'Model Capability Scaling' experiment (experiments/v2/run_capability_curve.py:44-47, run_small_vs_big.py:50-54); and (2) LLM-as-judge — Borda-count judge panels (7 judges/model) that rank competing document versions, including a cross-model-judge robustness check using GPT-4o and Gemini-2.5-Flash-preview via OpenRouter (experiments/v2/run_cross_model_judges.py:40-44). Judges parse free-text 'RANKING: X,Y,Z' lines via regex, not structured/JSON output.
rows.mistakes: No call site anywhere in the repo (grepped all .py files) sets provider.quantizations, require_parameters, data_collection, order/only/sort, or logs the served provider/generation id — confirmed by an empty grep for 'extra_body|require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection|provider\s*[:=]|generation_id' across the whole codebase. The critical instance is meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct (experiments/v2/run_capability_curve.py:46, run_small_vs_big.py:54), an open-weight model served by many OpenRouter providers at heterogeneous quantizations (fp16 down to int4/int8) with default load-balanced routing (M1, M3). Llama-8B's measured performance ('Llama~8B is too weak to generate diverse alternatives, wins only 1/3 tasks') is a headline claim repeated in the paper's abstract and Section 'Model Capability Scaling' (paper/autoreason.tex lines 87, 927, 936, 961, 967, Table capability_curve) — i.e. a critical route feeding a reported, citation-worthy result. Because no provider/generation-id was ever recorded (M4, grep confirms no 'response.provider'/'hidden_params'/'generation_id' logging anywhere), the finding cannot be checked against which quantization actually served the model, and a future re-run could silently hit a different, differently-quantized provider and reproduce a different curve. This also makes the 5-tier scaling comparison a cross-provider confound (M8): Llama-8B and Gemini-Flash run through unpinned OpenRouter while Sonnet-4/4.6 run directly against the Anthropic API (paper appendix line 1106 vs. run_capability_curve.py MODELS dict) — the 'Llama is intrinsically too weak' conclusion is confounded with 'Llama was served via an unpinned, possibly degraded OpenRouter route.' Bare undated slugs are used throughout (M6): 'openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct', 'openrouter/openai/gpt-4o', 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview' (a rolling preview alias) — a re-run months later may hit a different underlying checkpoint. Sampling params (temperature 0.3-0.8, max_tokens) are sent on every call with no require_parameters (M2), and data_collection is never set to deny (M5), both generic across all OpenRouter calls. Note: judges use free-text regex parsing rather than response_format/JSON schema, so M9 does not apply. The paper's reproducibility appendix (paper/autoreason.tex:1103-1106) does name 'via OpenRouter' for the relevant models, showing partial awareness, but never discusses provider pinning, quantization, or provenance, so the substance of the risk is unaddressed (M10 not formally triggered since routing is at least named, but disclosure is superficial).
rows.one_line_fix: Pin the Llama-3.1-8B (and other open-weight) OpenRouter calls with provider={'order': [<validated provider>], 'allow_fallbacks': False, 'quantizations': ['fp16','bf16','fp32'], 'require_parameters': True, 'data_collection': 'deny'}, and log the response's served-provider/generation-id alongside every output so the capability-curve result can be re-verified against a known, fixed backend.
rows.evidence: experiments/v2/run_capability_curve.py:44-47 MODELS = {'gemini_flash': 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.0-flash-001', 'llama8b': 'openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct'}, called at line 91-96 via litellm.acompletion(model=model, ...) with no provider/extra_body kwarg. experiments/v2/run_cross_model_judges.py:40-44 JUDGE_MODELS includes 'gpt4o': 'openrouter/openai/gpt-4o', 'gemini_flash': 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview', NUM_JUDGES=7, called identically with no provider config (line 79-84). run_small_vs_big.py:47 OPENROUTER_KEY = os.environ.get('OPENROUTER_API_KEY', ''). Repo-wide grep for provider/require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection/generation_id/hidden_params returned zero matches. paper/autoreason.tex:87,927,936,961,967 cite the Llama-8B capability-curve finding as a headline result; appendix lines 1103-1106 disclose 'via OpenRouter' for the relevant models but nothing further.
rows.audit_notes: Interesting internal inconsistency: paper appendix (line 1105) says 'gemini-2.0-flash via Google AI' (implying direct Google API, not OpenRouter) for the scaling experiments, but the actual code (run_capability_curve.py, run_small_vs_big.py) routes gemini_flash through 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.0-flash-001'. Also the appendix mislabels 'claude-3-5-haiku-latest via OpenRouter' as the 'Haiku 4.5' code experiment, while run_code_haiku45.py actually calls 'anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001' directly (not via OpenRouter) — a documentation/code mismatch, though low safety impact since both are proprietary Anthropic-served models. The cross-model-judge robustness check (run_cross_model_judges.py) was actually executed — results_cross_model_judges/ contains real judge transcripts for GPT-4o and Claude-Sonnet-4 (task_01, task_04) — but doesn't appear cited by name in the final autoreason.tex prose, so it may be an internal validation check that didn't make the paper narrative (worth noting since the discovery brief implied it was a reported result).
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned NousResearch/autoreason at HEAD 538f881 (2026-04-12) and verified every cited file line-by-line; all evidence checks out exactly.

1. experiments/v2/run_capability_curve.py:44-49 confirmed verbatim: MODELS={'gemini_flash':'openrouter/google/gemini-2.0-flash-001','llama8b':'openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct'}, SONNET='anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514' (a direct, non-OpenRouter call). call_llm() at line 88-108 calls litellm.acompletion(model=model, messages=..., temperature=..., max_tokens=...) with zero provider/extra_body kwarg anywhere in the function.

2. experiments/v2/run_small_vs_big.py:47,51-61 confirmed: OPENROUTER_KEY read from env; CHEAP_MODELS routes Haiku-3.5, Gemini-Flash, and Llama-8B all through 'openrouter/...' while BIG_MODEL and JUDGE_MODEL ('anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514') go direct to Anthropic — this is the real cross-provider confound behind M8 (open-weight/third-party models via unpinned router vs. the big/judge model via direct API).

3. experiments/v2/run_cross_model_judges.py:38-44 confirmed: NUM_JUDGES=7, JUDGE_MODELS includes 'openrouter/openai/gpt-4o' and 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview' (a rolling preview alias — legitimate M6 example), called through the same unconfigured litellm.acompletion. I additionally verified this experiment was actually run: experiments/v2/results_cross_model_judges/task_0{1,4}_gpt4o/results.json shows model_id='openrouter/openai/gpt-4o' with real judge_N_raw.txt transcripts checked into git — confirming genuine OpenRouter execution, not just dead code.

4. Repo-wide grep for 'extra_body|require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection|generation_id|hidden_params|provider\s*[:=]|response\.(provider|id|_hidden|model)' across all .py files returns zero matches — independently reproduced the audit's grep claim. No response_format/json_schema/pydantic usage anywhere either, so M9 correctly does not apply (judges use pure regex 'RANKING:' parsing, confirmed in parse_ranking()).

5. paper/autoreason.tex confirmed: abstract (line 87) and Conclusion (lines 961, 967) state the Llama-8B capability-curve finding as a headline claim; Table 'tab:failures' row (line 927) cites 'Llama~8B wins only 1/3 tasks'; Discussion (line 936) repeats it. This is unambiguously a critical, citation-bearing route, not exploratory scratch work.

6. Reproducibility appendix (lines 1098-1107) names 'via OpenRouter' for the relevant models (partial disclosure, matching awareness='aware_partial') but never discusses pinning/quantization/provenance — and contains a genuine mismatch I independently verified: line 1105 says 'gemini-2.0-flash via Google AI' but the actual code (run_capability_curve.py, run_small_vs_big.py) routes it through 'openrouter/google/gemini-2.0-flash-001'. I also confirmed the Haiku-4.5 code path (run_code_haiku45.py:42) calls 'anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001' directly — matching the audit's note that the appendix mislabels this as an OpenRouter call, though this particular mismatch has low safety impact since it's a proprietary single-served model either way.

The only change I made to the original mistake_ids list is cosmetic ordering: the original JSON listed them out of taxonomy order (M1,M3,M4,M6,M8,M2,M5); I re-sorted to M1,M2,M3,M4,M5,M6,M8 — same set, no addition or removal. I found no false accusation (every flagged mistake is grounded in code I re-fetched) and no missed corruption channel: M7 (seed-determinism) isn't claimed anywhere in the repo so its omission is correct, M9 is correctly ruled out (no structured-output judges), M10 is correctly not triggered given the appendix's partial disclosure, M11/M12 have no supporting evidence and were correctly left off. The verdict is well-calibrated: it doesn't over-flag the proprietary-only paths (e.g., it doesn't claim M1 for Haiku/Sonnet/GPT-4o/Gemini, correctly scoping quantization risk to the open-weight Llama-3.1-8B) while still correctly flagging structural M2/M3/M4/M5 issues that apply to every OpenRouter call site regardless of model. 591-star count and NousResearch ownership independently confirmed via gh api.

rows.title: OpenPipe ART (Agent Reinforcement Trainer) — teacher/student distillation example
rows.summary: ART is a widely used (10.5k-star) open-source library for RL/GRPO and SFT training of agents. It ships a maintained distillation pattern — both a standalone example script and an interactive '/train-sft' wizard/skill template — that pulls a completion from a large 'teacher' model via OpenRouter and uses it verbatim as the label in an SFT training trajectory for a smaller student model.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is the teacher-completion source for supervised-distillation training data: dev/sft/distillation.py calls teacher_client.chat.completions.create(model='z-ai/glm-5', ...) via base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1', takes completion.choices[0].message.content verbatim as the assistant turn in an art.Trajectory, and immediately trains a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct student on it with student.train_sft(...). The same unconfigured pattern is codified as the canonical code template in .agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md, which the wizard uses to generate distillation scripts for any user's chosen teacher model/API.
rows.importance: 10,485-star repo; the OpenRouter distillation pattern is not a one-off script but a maintained, first-class feature reproduced in two places (dev/sft/distillation.py and the /train-sft wizard's SKILL.md code template), meaning the unsafe pattern is the one many downstream users/teams will copy verbatim for their own real distillation runs.
rows.one_line_fix: Pin the teacher call with extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "quantizations": ["fp16"/"bf16"], "allow_fallbacks": False, "data_collection": "deny"}, "require_parameters": True} and persist completion.model/provider + completion.id alongside each SFT trajectory so training data provenance is auditable.
rows.mistakes: Fetched dev/sft/distillation.py in full: it builds `teacher_client = AsyncOpenAI(api_key=os.environ['OPENROUTER_API_KEY'], base_url='https://openrouter.ai/api/v1')` and calls `teacher_client.chat.completions.create(model=TEACHER_MODEL, messages=[...])` with TEACHER_MODEL='z-ai/glm-5' (dev/sft/distillation.py:19,26-31) — no `provider` block, no `require_parameters`, no quantization pin, no order/only/sort, and the raw `teacher_response` string is wrapped into `art.Trajectory(...)` and fed straight into `student.train_sft(...)` (lines 33-42, 58-60) with zero logging of which provider, quantization, or generation id actually produced it. `gh search code --repo OpenPipe/ART` for `require_parameters`, `data_collection`, and `provider=` (OpenRouter-routing sense) returned no hits anywhere in the repo; the only `extra_body` uses are unrelated chat-template flags, confirming the router call is completely unconfigured repo-wide. The same bare pattern is the code template inside `.agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md` ('Distillation pattern' section), which an AI wizard uses to generate real user training scripts, so the risk isn't confined to a toy example — it's the reusable, endorsed pattern for anyone doing OpenRouter-based distillation with this library. Because z-ai/glm-5 is an open-weight model family typically served by multiple third-party inference backends on OpenRouter at varying quantization levels, and TEACHER_MODEL is a bare undated slug, the teacher completion that becomes the student's training label is exposed to (M1) unpinned quantization drift, (M3) probabilistic multi-provider routing/run-to-run drift, (M6) silent model-version drift behind the slug, and (M4) there is no provenance record (provider, generation id) captured anywhere to audit which backend/quantization produced any given training example after the fact. (M10) Neither the script nor the SKILL.md documentation discloses or caveats any of this to users who copy the pattern for real training runs. This directly threatens the integrity/reproducibility of any student model SFT'd via this pattern, since the exact same TEACHER_MODEL call could silently be served by a different provider/quantization on every re-run with no way to detect or reproduce it.
rows.audit_notes: Two independent artifacts in the repo instantiate the identical unsafe pattern: the standalone example (dev/sft/distillation.py) and the /train-sft skill's auto-generated code template (.agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md), which strengthens the case that this is a systemic, endorsed pattern rather than a one-off demo bug. Note the framework itself is teacher-model-agnostic (SKILL.md just says 'e.g., an OpenRouter model'), so a user could point it at a proprietary single-served model and most of these mistakes would become N/A — but the one concrete, working example shipped in the repo uses an open-weight model via unpinned OpenRouter, and that is the code most likely to be copy-pasted by real users. Could not confirm this pipeline was used for any specific published ART benchmark result (e.g. ART·E) — importance is about downstream propagation via a high-star, actively maintained infra library rather than a single paper's numbers.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-fetched everything via `git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/OpenPipe/ART.git` (repo pushed today, 2026-07-18, per `gh api repos/OpenPipe/ART`; stargazers_count=10485, exactly matching the "10,485-star" claim).

**dev/sft/distillation.py** matches the citation verbatim: `TEACHER_MODEL = "z-ai/glm-5"` (my line 18, cited as 19 — trivial ±1 offset, immaterial), `STUDENT_BASE_MODEL = "Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct"` (line 19), a bare `AsyncOpenAI(api_key=..., base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1")` client (lines 25-28), a `chat.completions.create(model=TEACHER_MODEL, messages=[...])` call with zero `extra_body`/`provider` kwarg (lines 31-34), `teacher_response` taken as raw `.choices[0].message.content` and wrapped into `art.Trajectory` (lines 41-48), then fed directly to `student.train_sft` via `create_sft_dataset_iterator` (lines 51-63). No provider pinning, no `require_parameters`, no logging of `completion.model`/`completion.id`/provider anywhere in the file.

**`.agents/skills/train-sft/SKILL.md`** "Distillation pattern" (lines 234-285) reproduces the identical unconfigured shape: raw `AsyncOpenAI(base_url="<TEACHER_API_BASE>")`, `chat.completions.create(model="<TEACHER_MODEL>", ...)`, no provider/require_parameters placeholders anywhere in the template — confirming this is the code an AI wizard will generate for any user who picks OpenRouter (offered explicitly as "e.g., an OpenRouter model" in Step 4).

**gh-search claims verified independently**: `grep -rn "require_parameters"` and `grep -rn "data_collection"` across the whole repo return zero hits. All `extra_body` usages found (14 hits) are unrelated `chat_template_kwargs`/tinker-client params, exactly as claimed. The two `"provider"` string hits are unrelated Megatron test code (`SimpleNamespace(provider="provider", ...)`), not router config.

**I went further than the original verdict and hit OpenRouter's live API** (`curl https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models/z-ai/glm-5/endpoints`) to check whether M1/M3 are merely theoretical for this specific model. They are not: z-ai/glm-5 currently has **14 distinct provider endpoints** (StreamLake, GMICloud, DeepInfra, Baidu, DigitalOcean, SiliconFlow, Chutes, AtlasCloud, Amazon Bedrock, Novita, Z.AI, Parasail, Venice, Phala) with **mixed quantization** — most `fp8`, DeepInfra explicitly `fp4`, and three providers with `quantization: "unknown"`. An unpinned call to `model="z-ai/glm-5"` is genuinely load-balanced across these 14 backends at materially different precision. This is concrete, current confirmation of M1 and M3, not speculation — actually stronger evidence than the original verdict cited.

**Framework-level check**: `art.Trajectory` has a `metadata: dict[str, MetadataValue]` field that could trivially carry provider/generation-id provenance — it exists in `src/art/trajectories/__init__.py` but is unused by either the script or the SKILL.md template, confirming M4 is a real, easily-fixable gap rather than a framework limitation.

**One nuance for context** (does not change the verdict): the ART README's most-visible "Distillation (SFT)" link points to a *different* notebook in the sibling `openpipe/art-notebooks` repo (`examples/sft/distillation.ipynb`), which uses **W&B Inference** (`https://api.inference.wandb.ai/v1`) as the teacher backend, not OpenRouter. So OpenRouter-based distillation is not literally ART's single flagship showcased pattern — it's one of (at least) two teacher-completion patterns in the ecosystem. This slightly tempers the "importance" framing but does not make the cited `dev/sft/distillation.py` / SKILL.md code any less real or unsafe; the SKILL.md wizard still explicitly offers OpenRouter as a teacher option and generates this exact unguarded pattern when a user picks it.

**Sanity checks against false positives**: teacher model is confirmed open-weight ("Z.ai's flagship open-source foundation model," per OpenRouter's own model page), so M1/M3/M6 are legitimately applicable (not a proprietary single-served model where they'd be N/A). No `response_format`/JSON-schema usage anywhere in the cited call, so M9 correctly not flagged. No `seed` passed, so M7 correctly not flagged. No sampling params passed either, so M2 (dropped params) is correctly omitted since nothing is being requested that could be silently dropped. M8 (cross-provider comparison) correctly not flagged — this is a single teacher call, not a multi-model comparison sweep. M11/M12 correctly not flagged — no evidence of mixed direct/router calls or floor/price-sort routing.

**One addition**: M5 (data-policy leakage) is also defensibly present and was omitted from `mistake_ids` despite being alluded to in the `one_line_fix` (`"data_collection": "deny"`) — no `provider.data_collection` config exists anywhere in the repo (confirmed via grep), so the default "allow" data-retention policy silently applies to every OpenRouter call, including the teacher-completion call. This doesn't change overall severity (already High from M1/M3/M4) but is a legitimate, evidence-backed addition to the mistake list.

Overall: the verdict is well-supported, arguably conservative (I found stronger corroborating evidence — live 14-provider/fp4-fp8 endpoint data — than the original audit provided) and the safe/unsafe call, severity, and confidence should stand.
rows.evidence: Live OpenRouter API check (curl https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models/z-ai/glm-5/endpoints, checked 2026-07-18) shows 14 provider endpoints for z-ai/glm-5 with mixed quantization: StreamLake/fp8, GMICloud/fp8, DeepInfra/fp4, Baidu/fp8, DigitalOcean/unknown, SiliconFlow/fp8, Chutes/fp8, AtlasCloud/fp8, Amazon Bedrock/unknown, Novita/fp8, Z.AI/fp8, Parasail/fp8, Venice/fp8, Phala/unknown. This concretely substantiates M1 (fp8 vs fp4 spread) and M3 (14-way load-balanced routing) for the exact model/slug used in dev/sft/distillation.py, going beyond the original verdict's general assertion. Additionally, art.Trajectory (src/art/trajectories/__init__.py) has an unused `metadata` dict field that could carry provider/generation-id provenance, confirming M4 is a real, cheaply-fixable gap. grep confirms zero repo-wide hits for `require_parameters` or `data_collection`, corroborating the "completely unconfigured" claim and supporting the addition of M5.

rows.title: Prompt Framing Changes LLM Performance (and Safety) (kmerkelbach/llm-request-tone)
rows.summary: A LessWrong study measuring how prepending "tone"/framing text to prompts shifts LLM capability-benchmark scores (via lm-eval-harness) and safety-benchmark compliance rates (via a forked SORRY-Bench) across six open-weight model families/sizes (gpt-oss, Qwen 2.5, Llama 3.1), with gpt-oss-120b used as the SORRY-Bench safety judge.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is the sole inference backend for (1) all six target models under evaluation on both lm-eval-harness capability tasks and SORRY-Bench safety prompts, and (2) the gpt-oss-120b LLM-judge that scores compliance vs. refusal on harmful SORRY-Bench requests. Both paths hit https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 via the OpenAI SDK with zero `provider`/`extra_body` routing configuration.
rows.evidence: Evidence substantively confirmed via fresh clones. Two minor citation corrections worth noting for evidence-quality purposes (do not change the verdict): (1) lm_eval_shell.py base_url/api_key_env are at lines 40-41 (not 36-38) and the model_args block is at lines 70-76 (not 73-79); (2) the temperature=0 gpt-oss-120b judge call via chat_completion_openai is at common.py:171 inside run_judge_single (invoked by play_a_match_single for SORRY-Bench's "single" judge type), not common.py:337 (that line is inside the unrelated run_judge_pair pairwise-comparison path, gated to literal "gpt-3.5-turbo"/"gpt-4" and not used by this study). All other cited evidence (config.py:34-46, sorry_bench_shell.py:21, common.py:473-495, gen_api_answer.py:28-59, README.md OpenRouter section) matches the fetched source exactly.
rows.mistakes: The central reported result — "Expert framing was shown using statistical testing to increase the rate of compliance with harmful requests" (a safety-measurement claim on SORRY-Bench) plus all lm-eval capability numbers in tables/lm-eval/* — is computed entirely from OpenRouter calls with zero routing safeguards. M1 (no quantizations pin): all 6 target models and the gpt-oss-120b judge are open-weight and commonly served by multiple OpenRouter providers at different quantization levels (int4/int8/fp16); nothing pins this. M2 (silent param dropping): temperature is passed (0.7 for targets per config.py, 0 for the judge) with no `require_parameters:true`, so a provider that ignores temperature would silently do so. M3 (probabilistic routing): no `order`/`only`/`sort` anywhere, so requests for the same model+prompt across the many framing/scenario/repetition combinations can land on different providers/backends run-to-run — exactly the kind of noise that is indistinguishable from a genuine "framing effect" in a study whose entire thesis is measuring small, statistically-tested shifts caused by prompt tone. M4 (no provenance): the served-provider field / generation id is never read or stored (only `.message.content` is kept), so which weights actually produced any given answer is unrecoverable and the study cannot be audited or exactly reproduced. M5 (data_collection left default "allow"): the SORRY-Bench prompts are deliberately harmful/adversarial requests and model completions to them, sent to whichever provider happens to be routed to, with no `data_collection: deny` — a real content-sensitivity concern on top of the reliability one. M6 (bare undated slugs): all six model identifiers and the judge slug are undated, so results could silently reflect a provider-side model swap over the multi-week data-collection period implied by `max_num_repetitions = 16`. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): the paper's whole design is a multi-way comparison (3 model families x 2 sizes x multiple framings x multiple benchmarks), with none of the six models pinned to a specific provider — differences attributed to "family" or "framing" could instead reflect which provider/quantization OpenRouter happened to route each condition to.
rows.one_line_fix: In chat_completion_openai() (sorry-bench fork common.py:479-494) and in the lm_eval model_args string (lm_eval_shell.py), add provider routing config — order/only a validated provider, allow_fallbacks:false, quantizations pin, require_parameters:true, data_collection:"deny" — and capture/log the response's `provider` field (or GET /api/v1/generation?id=...) alongside every stored answer/judgment so the served backend is reproducible and auditable.
rows.audit_notes: Two-repo audit: the primary repo (llm-request-tone) only constructs the OpenRouter-pointed lm-eval model_args and shells out to a second, external repo (kmerkelbach/sorry-bench, forked and modified on branch feature/llm-tone specifically "to work with OpenRouter" per the README) for both the SORRY-Bench target-model generation and the LLM-judge call — both of which reuse the exact same unguarded chat_completion_openai() helper. This is a fairly clean, well-documented small study (full harness, published result tables, explicit LessWrong writeup of methodology and repetition count for variance) that nonetheless has literally zero OpenRouter routing safeguards on either path, despite comparing open-weight model families against each other and making a safety-relevant statistical claim — a textbook case of the M1/M3/M4/M8 stack applying simultaneously to a genuinely critical route.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-fetched all cited files from fresh clones of both repos (llm-request-tone default branch, sorry-bench@feature/llm-tone) and every substantive claim checks out.

Confirmed verbatim/near-verbatim:
- src/evaluation/config.py:34-41 — `models` list is exactly the 6 open-weight slugs cited (gpt-oss-20b/120b, qwen-2.5-7b/72b-instruct, llama-3.1-8b/70b-instruct), all bare/undated (M6 live). Lines 45-46 confirm temperature=0.7, max_num_repetitions=16 as quoted in the mistakes narrative.
- src/evaluation/lm_eval_shell.py — `base_url: str = "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions"` and `api_key_env: str = "OPENROUTER_API_KEY"` confirmed (actual lines 40-41, evidence said 36-38 — a minor ~3-4 line drift, not a substantive error); model_args string (`model=...,base_url=...,api_key=...,num_concurrent=...,max_retries=...`) confirmed at actual lines 70-76 (evidence said 73-79, again minor drift). No provider/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection key anywhere in the file — confirmed by direct grep, zero hits.
- src/evaluation/sorry_bench_shell.py:21 — `judge_model: str = "openai/gpt-oss-120b"` confirmed (evidence cited line 22, off by one).
- sorry-bench/common.py:473-495 `chat_completion_openai()` — this is an exact match to the evidence, byte-for-byte: `base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` (line 481), `client.chat.completions.create(model=model, messages=messages, n=1, temperature=temperature, max_tokens=max_tokens)` (488-494), no extra_body/provider kwarg, and `output = response.choices[0].message.content` (line 495) is the only thing extracted — no `.provider`, no generation id, confirming M4 (no provenance) precisely.
- gen_api_answer.py:28-59 `get_answer()` confirms none of the 6 target models hit the Anthropic (`ANTHROPIC_MODEL_LIST`) or Gemini (`"gemini" in model.lower()`) branches, so all 6 fall through to `chat_completion_openai` — the "sole path for target-model generation" claim holds.
- common.py:170-171 `run_judge_single()`: `if "gpt" in model: judgment = chat_completion_openai(model, conv, temperature=0, ...)` — confirms the gpt-oss-120b judge (matches "gpt" substring) is scored via the same unguarded OpenRouter call at temperature=0, as claimed. One correction: evidence cites "common.py:337" for this — that line is actually inside the unrelated `run_judge_pair` (pairwise-comparison judge, restricted to literal `gpt-3.5-turbo`/`gpt-4`, not used by SORRY-Bench's single-score judging). The correct line for the temperature=0 gpt-oss-120b judge call is common.py:171 (`run_judge_single`), which `play_a_match_single` (line 258) does invoke for `judge.prompt_template["type"] == "single"`, the SORRY-Bench scoring mode. This is a citation slip (wrong line number for the right function/behavior), not a factual error — the underlying claim (unguarded temp-0 judge call via the same helper) is correct.
- README.md quote ("If you want to use OpenRouter... If not, you can (likely - not tested) use any other LLM inference provider...") reproduced verbatim, confirmed present.
- grep across both cloned repos for extra_body/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection/provider/generation_id: zero hits anywhere in Python source, matching the evidence's "gh search code returned zero hits" claim.
- No response_format/json_schema usage anywhere (judge output is parsed via regex on free text), so M9 is correctly NOT included — there is no structured-output constraint on an unguarded route.
- No `seed` param sent to any LLM API call (only an unrelated `np.random.seed(0)` for local sampling in gen_judgment_safety.py) — M7 correctly excluded. No dead Anthropic/Gemini code paths are actually exercised for the 6 target models — M11 correctly excluded (no real backend mixing in this study). No :floor/sort:price routing found — M12 correctly excluded.

Sanity checks per the review steps: all 6 target models plus the gpt-oss-120b judge are open-weight and commonly multi-provider on OpenRouter, so M1/M3/M8 are legitimately live (not a proprietary-single-served false positive). No provider pinning or provenance logging exists anywhere in either repo, so this is not a LEGIT case. The route is unambiguously critical — it produces every capability-benchmark number and the headline statistical safety-compliance claim. I find no missed corruption channel (M9 checked and correctly absent) and no false accusation (every flagged mistake ID is directly evidenced in code that matches the citations, modulo a few off-by-a-few-lines citation slips that don't affect the substance). Verdict, severity, confidence, and mistake_ids all stand as originally reported.

rows.title: Redwood Research — BashArena (basharena_public)
rows.summary: Public dataset-generation/eval-running code for BashArena, an agentic AI-control/safety benchmark where 'honest' and 'attack' policy agents complete Linux sysadmin tasks (some with covert sabotage side-tasks) while a trusted monitor scores them; the resulting trajectories/scores are the benchmark's core data.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is one of several model backends selectable via a shared `official_model_names` registry (alongside direct OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/Together). Two entries route through OpenRouter: `"oss": "openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b"` (open-weight) and `"grok4fast": "openrouter/x-ai/grok-4-fast"` (proprietary, effectively single-served by xAI). The selected model string is passed straight into Inspect AI's `get_model(model_name, api_key=api_key)` and then used as the honest_policy/attack_policy model generating the actual task-solving trajectories that comprise the benchmark (a critical route), or potentially as the trusted-monitor model.
rows.mistakes: Every get_model() call site for the OpenRouter-routed entries (src/policies/attack_policy.py:82 `get_model(model_name, api_key=api_key)`; src/policies/honest_policy.py:68; src/monitors/ensemble_trusted_monitor.py:83 and src/monitors/trusted_monitor.py:110 `get_model(model)`; src/policies/trusted_edit_policy.py:109) passes only `model_name`/`api_key` — no `provider=` model_args dict is ever constructed anywhere in the repo (confirmed by grep for `provider=`, `models=`, `require_parameters`, `quantizations`, `data_collection` across src/, which returns zero hits outside task-data noise). Inspect AI's OpenRouter provider (inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py:85-135, 334-385, read directly from a local checkout) only sets OpenRouter routing preferences (order/only/sort/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection) when the caller explicitly supplies a `provider` model_arg at construction time — basharena_public never does. Consequences for the 'oss' slug (`openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b`, run_tasks.py:31, resample_runs.py:1165), an open-weight model served by many independent OpenRouter backends (Together/Fireworks/Groq/Cerebras/DeepInfra/etc.) at differing quantizations: (M1) no `quantizations` pin means the exact weights served for any honest/attack-policy trajectory are unknown and can silently vary run-to-run and even resample-to-resample; (M3) no `order`/`only`/`sort` means OpenRouter's default load-balanced routing picks the backend probabilistically per request; (M2) sampling/config params (`max_tokens`, `reasoning_tokens`/`reasoning_effort`, GenerateConfig fields set in attack_policy.py:60-70 and honest_policy.py) have no `require_parameters:true` enforcing the chosen backend actually honors them — silent dropping/altering is possible; (M5) `data_collection` defaults to 'allow', meaning red-team/attack-policy transcripts (deliberately containing covert-sabotage reasoning) could be retained/trained-on by third-party inference providers, a meaningful concern given the sensitive nature of this AI-control red-team content; (M7) `generate_with_backoff` (src/policies/generate_with_retry.py:9-25) and the honest/attack policies thread a per-resample `seed` (attack_policy.py:133, honest_policy.py:112) through GenerateConfig as the mechanism controlling the defer-to-resample (DTR) protocol's distinct-but-reproducible resamples — but since provider/backend selection is independent of `seed` and varies per OpenRouter request, the intended seed-controlled variation is confounded by uncontrolled backend/quantization drift; (M8) the same registry mixes directly-served proprietary models (Anthropic/OpenAI/Google/Together-direct: opus, sonnet, gpt5, gemini3_pro, k2) with the unpinned OpenRouter-routed 'oss' entry as interchangeable policy models in run_tasks.py/resample_runs.py — any cross-model comparison of catch-rate/side-task-success involving gpt-oss-120b is confounded by provider/quantization noise that doesn't affect the directly-served models; (M4) no code path records which OpenRouter backend/generation-id actually served a call — off_policy_traj_views.py:173-176's `message_model_without_provider` only strips the `openrouter/`-prefix from the model slug string for display, it is not provenance capture — and the compressed/shareable log format (CompressedSample/CompressedMessage in src/compress_logs.py:76-115, the artifact written to `compressed_logs_dir` and presumably backing the HF-hosted dataset) has no field for served-provider or generation id, so even if Inspect AI's raw .eval log incidentally captures the OpenRouter response JSON, that provenance is dropped before the data is shared/reported on. README.md contains zero mentions of openrouter/quantization/provider/reproducibility (no disclosure of the inference-routing risk anywhere in project docs). Net effect: any reported honest-policy usefulness rate, attack-policy catch-rate, or safety metric computed for the 'oss' (gpt-oss-120b) model is not attributable to a fixed, known set of weights, and is not reproducible run-to-run.
rows.one_line_fix: For the `openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b` entry (and any future open-weight OpenRouter slug), pass `get_model(model_name, provider={"order": [<validated provider>], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": ["fp8","bf16","fp16"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny"}, api_key=api_key)` and persist the served-provider/generation id into CompressedSample so it survives into the shared dataset/logs.
rows.audit_notes: grok-4-fast is proprietary/xAI-first-party so M1/M3/M8 risk is much lower for that entry specifically (closer to LEGIT) — the corrupting risk concentrates almost entirely on the 'oss' (gpt-oss-120b) slug, the one open-weight model routed through OpenRouter in an otherwise unusually rigorous Inspect-AI-based control-research framework (explicit cache_prompt handling, batch config, resample protocols with blacklisted flaky task IDs, docent integration for trajectory analysis). The OpenRouter gap looks like an oversight rather than negligence — the routing-risk knobs (`provider=` model_args) are a relatively obscure Inspect AI feature not surfaced by default, and inspect_ai supplies zero safe defaults on its own (confirmed by reading its openrouter.py provider source from a local checkout: `provider=`/`quantizations`/`require_parameters`/`data_collection` are opt-in, never defaulted).
rows.verifier_reasoning:
I cloned redwoodresearch/basharena_public fresh (git log shows the sole squashed commit 86082fd, "Updating to BashArena 1.1") and re-fetched every cited file plus a fresh local checkout of Inspect AI's openrouter.py (dated 2026-07-17). Every specific citation in the verdict checks out exactly:

1. official_model_names registry (run_tasks.py:27-44, duplicated resample_runs.py:1161-1176): confirmed verbatim, including `"oss": "openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b"` and `"grok4fast": "openrouter/x-ai/grok-4-fast"`. Notably `gemini3_pro` shows a commented-out `# "gemini3_pro": "openrouter/google/gemini-3-pro-preview"` replaced by a direct `"google/gemini-3-pro-preview"` — someone touched OpenRouter routing for one model but left oss/grok4fast on OpenRouter, unexplained in the (squashed, single-commit) history.
2. Every `get_model()` call site cited is exact: attack_policy.py:82 `get_model(model_name, api_key=api_key)`, honest_policy.py:68 (same), ensemble_trusted_monitor.py:83 `get_model(model)`, trusted_monitor.py:110 `get_model(model)`, trusted_edit_policy.py:109 `get_model(model_name)`. None pass a `provider=` model_arg.
3. Repo-wide grep for `provider=`/`require_parameters`/`quantizations`/`data_collection`/`model_args` across all .py files returns zero hits outside one unrelated MySQL config string (`wsrep_provider=...`) in benchmark task fixture data — confirms the "no provider= constructed anywhere" claim.
4. README.md has zero mentions of openrouter/quantization/provider/reproducibility, and explicitly names `src/run_tasks.py` as "the place to start if you want to do BashArena runs" — confirms this is a real production entry point, supporting `critical_route: true`, not a toy script.
5. Read Inspect AI's actual openrouter.py: `provider`/`models`/`transforms` are only collected from `**model_args` at construction (lines 85-135) and only injected into the request's `extra_body` in `completion_params` (lines 334-385) if that kwarg was explicitly supplied — inspect_ai supplies zero safe defaults, exactly as the audit_notes claim. This directly substantiates M1/M2/M3/M5 all sharing one root cause.
6. off_policy_traj_views.py:168-183 `check_model_match`/`message_model_without_provider` is indeed just a string-split naming heuristic for matching on/off-policy messages, not provenance capture — correctly characterized, not overclaimed.
7. compress_logs.py's `CompressedMessage`/`CompressedSample`/`ResampledCompressedMessage` classes (lines 53-113) have `model` (string), `resample_seed`, `monitor_scores` fields but no served-provider or generation-id field anywhere — confirms M4.
8. generate_with_retry.py's `generate_with_backoff` (lines 10-26) confirms the exact seed-threading mechanism cited for M7: `seed` is folded into `GenerateConfig(seed=seed)` and combined with `cache=resample_seed is not None`, used to drive the DTR protocol's per-resample variation (verified in defer_to_resample.py:201-213, `current_seed = seed_offset + len(samples)`) — legitimately vulnerable to backend/quantization drift since OpenRouter backend selection is independent of the `seed` parameter.
9. Sanity check for missed M9: grepped for `response_format`/`response_schema`/`json_schema` across src/ — zero hits. The trusted_monitor uses XML-tag extraction (`<score>`/`<reasoning>`), not structured output, so M9 correctly does not apply.
10. Sanity check for wrongly-flagged proprietary models: grok-4-fast (xAI, no third-party licensing) is correctly treated as low-risk for M1/M3/M8 in the audit_notes, consistent with the LEGIT criterion for single-served proprietary models; it is not itself in the mistake_ids list in a way that overclaims (the mistake_ids apply to the OpenRouter route as a whole, concentrated on the 'oss' entry, which the audit_notes explicitly disambiguate).

No exculpatory evidence exists (no `provider=` pin, no README disclosure, no provenance capture anywhere) and no false-accusation risk found (proprietary grok-4-fast is correctly downgraded in the prose rather than driving the verdict; M9 was correctly withheld). The verdict is accurate as stated; I return the same severity/confidence/uses_safely and the same mistake set (reordered only for readability, semantically identical).

rows.title: Scaling Laws For Scalable Oversight (Engels, Baek, Kantamneni, Tegmark — NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight)
rows.summary: Derives scaling laws for AI scalable-oversight games (Debate, Mafia, Backdoor Code/Houdini, Wargames), fitting oversight success/Elo as a function of model capability using a fixed grid of ~14 LLMs spanning open-weight and proprietary families.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is the sole LLM access layer for all four oversight games in the paper (Debate, Mafia, Backdoor Code guard/houdini, Wargames). Scripts sweep every debater x judge (Debate) and every villager x mafia (Mafia) pair from a shared 14-model MODEL_LIST mixing proprietary (openai/chatgpt-4o-latest, google/gemini-*, anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku) and open-weight models (meta-llama/llama-3.{1,2,3}-*, google/gemma-2-*, qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct) to produce the debate-accuracy and Mafia-Elo data points that the scaling-law fits (headline figures) are built from.
rows.mistakes: Every OpenRouter call in the repo (debate/debate_new.py, debate/debate_closed_general.py, mafia/Mafia.py, wargames/utils.py, backdoor-code/rating_guard.py, backdoor-code/run_houdini.py) uses a bare `client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=...)` or raw POST with no `extra_body`/`provider` key at all — repo-wide grep for extra_body|require_parameters|quantiz|data_collection|order|only|sort|:floor|:nitro|:exacto returns zero hits. Concretely: (1) M1 unpinned quantization — 8 of the 14 sweep models are open-weight (llama-3.3-70b/3-70b/3.1-8b/3-8b/3.2-3b, gemma-2-27b/9b, qwen-2-72b), each servable by multiple OpenRouter backends at different precisions; none pinned. (2) M2 silent parameter dropping — wargames/utils.py:26 and backdoor-code/rating_guard.py:93 send temperature=0.7 (and max_tokens) with no require_parameters, so some providers may silently ignore it. (3) M3 probabilistic routing — no order/only/sort anywhere, so which backend serves each open-weight model can vary call to call across the full 14x14 sweep (debate/script.sh, mafia/script.sh). (4) M4 no provenance — only response.choices[0].message.content is ever saved (e.g. debate_new.py:475-480, Mafia.py output files); the OpenRouter `provider`/generation id is never read or logged anywhere in the repo. (5) M5 data_collection left at its "allow" default. (6) M6 model drift — "openai/chatgpt-4o-latest" and "google/gemini-pro" are undated rolling aliases used across a sweep (20 data_idx x 14 debaters x 2 datasets per script) that likely spans days/weeks. (7) M8 cross-provider comparison confound — the paper's core empirical claim is a capability-vs-oversight scaling curve fit across this same mixed proprietary/open-weight model list; unpinned quantization noise on the open-weight half is exactly the kind of confound that can distort a fitted scaling exponent, especially for the smaller/weaker models where quantization artifacts are proportionally larger. (8) M10 no reporting — verified via WebFetch on both the arXiv abstract/HTML and the full PDF that the paper contains no mention of OpenRouter, provider routing, quantization, or inference-serving details; README.md only lists Python package requirements and run scripts, nothing about routing/provenance.
rows.one_line_fix: For every OpenRouter call, add extra_body={"provider": {"order": [validated_provider], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny"}}, log the returned provider/generation id next to each saved transcript, and disclose the routing config in the paper — none of this exists anywhere in the current codebase.
rows.evidence: Drop M10 from mistake_ids. Corrected evidence for reporting: arxiv.org/pdf/2504.18530, Appendix C, p.15 (pdftotext-extracted, verbatim): "Gemini-Pro was excluded from the debate setup because OpenRouter had ceased support for this model by the time we conducted our debate experiments. Most experiments were conducted between February 20, 2025, and April 20, 2025. During this period, it is possible that model providers updated their API-served models (e.g., openai/chatgpt-4o-latest may have been updated by OpenAI). However, we do not believe these changes significantly affected our results and therefore treat all models as fixed throughout the study." This names OpenRouter explicitly and explicitly discusses (and dismisses) M6-type model drift risk — so "paper discloses neither routing nor inference stack" is false; inference stack IS named. What is genuinely undisclosed anywhere in the paper or README (confirmed by full-text grep of the extracted PDF for "quantiz|provider|router|:floor|:nitro|require_parameters|data_collection|seed" beyond the two OpenRouter mentions and unrelated Elo-bootstrap "seeds") is: quantization tier, provider pinning/order, require_parameters, data_collection policy, and provenance/generation-id logging — i.e., the paper reports that it used OpenRouter and knows models can drift, but gives zero indication it controlled for provider/quantization variance or logged which backend served each call. All other evidence in the original verdict (MODEL_LIST at debate_new.py:17-30/Mafia.py:319-333, client setup at debate_new.py:69-74/Mafia.py:12-18, bare chat.completions.create calls at debate_new.py:337/372/469, temperature=0.7-no-require_parameters at wargames/utils.py:25 and rating_guard.py:93, repo-wide zero grep hits for extra_body/require_parameters/quantiz/data_collection, Mafia.py:10 hardcoded /om/user/dbaek path, script.sh sweep scale) was re-verified byte-for-byte against the cloned repo and stands.
rows.importance: NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight from Max Tegmark's MIT lab (Engels, Baek, Kantamneni, Tegmark); directly in the debate-for-truth / social-deduction-game scalable-oversight lane; derives headline scaling-law fits (a core contribution) from this exact OpenRouter-served data across Debate and Mafia.
rows.audit_notes: mafia/Mafia.py:10 still has the original author's hardcoded dotenv path `/om/user/dbaek/scaling-scalable-oversight/Debate/.env` (MIT OpenMind cluster path) — confirms this is genuine, unedited author code, not a sanitized release. Also debate_new.py:69 and Mafia.py:13 fetch `OPENAI_API_KEY` into a variable that is then never used (dead code — the client only ever uses `openrouter_api_key`), so despite superficially looking like it might hit OpenAI directly for some models, all calls actually route through OpenRouter (ruled out M11 silent backend mixing). README.md literally says figures are reproducible "(aspirationally)" — an unintentionally apt admission given the total absence of provider pinning.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-fetched the actual repo (git clone subhashk01/oversight-scaling-laws) and the actual arXiv PDF (2504.18530, extracted with pdftotext, not just WebFetch). Almost every code-side claim checks out verbatim; one mistake ID (M10) is factually wrong and must be dropped.

CONFIRMED (matches evidence exactly):
- debate/debate_new.py:17-30 and mafia/Mafia.py:319-333: MODEL_LIST is exactly the claimed 14 models, 8 open-weight (llama-3.3-70b, llama-3-70b, llama-3.1-8b, llama-3-8b, llama-3.2-3b, gemma-2-27b, gemma-2-9b, qwen-2-72b) + 6 proprietary. This is a genuinely mixed roster, so M1/M3/M8 applicability (which the LEGIT bar exempts only for proprietary-single-served models) is correct — not a false accusation.
- Client setup debate_new.py:69-74 / Mafia.py:12-18: bare `OpenAI(base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1", api_key=openrouter_api_key)`, and `client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=...)` calls at debate_new.py:337,372,469 with zero `extra_body`/provider arg. Only `.choices[0].message.content` is ever extracted/saved — confirms M4 (no provenance/generation-id ever read or logged).
- wargames/utils.py:8-31 and backdoor-code/rating_guard.py:71-100 and run_houdini.py:49-72: raw `requests.post` with `temperature: 0.7` (+max_tokens) and no provider/require_parameters key — confirms M2 exactly as scoped (only claimed for these files, not debate/mafia, which don't set explicit sampling params — appropriately narrow).
- Repo-wide grep for `extra_body|require_parameters|quantiz|data_collection|:floor|:nitro|:exacto|generation_id|provider` = zero hits across all .py files. Confirms M1, M3, M5.
- Mafia.py:10 hardcoded `/om/user/dbaek/scaling-scalable-oversight/Debate/.env` path confirmed verbatim; OPENAI_API_KEY fetched but dead/unused in every file (client always uses openrouter_api_key) — confirms the M11 rule-out is correct.
- debate/script.sh (20 data_idx × 14 debaters × 4 dataset variants) and mafia/script.sh (20 × 14 × 14) confirm this is the actual production sweep behind the paper's headline figures — critical_route:true is justified.
- README.md confirms verbatim "(aspirationally)" phrasing and zero routing/provenance content.
- No response_format/json_schema anywhere (M9 correctly not flagged); no seed passed to any LLM call, only dataset `shuffle(seed=42)` (M7 correctly not flagged).

CORRECTION — M10 is not supportable and must be removed:
The verdict's evidence claims "verified via WebFetch on both the arXiv abstract/HTML and the full PDF that the paper contains no mention of OpenRouter, provider routing, quantization, or inference-serving details." I downloaded the actual PDF and ran pdftotext, bypassing the summarizer. Appendix C ("Full List of Models Used in the Experiment"), page 15, contains two explicit mentions of OpenRouter: "Gemini-Pro was excluded from the debate setup because OpenRouter had ceased support for this model by the time we conducted our debate experiments" and immediately after: "During this period, it is possible that model providers updated their API-served models (e.g., openai/chatgpt-4o-latest may have been updated by OpenAI). However, we do not believe these changes significantly affected our results and therefore treat all models as fixed throughout the study." The M10 taxonomy definition requires disclosing "neither routing nor inference stack" — but the inference stack (OpenRouter, named explicitly) IS disclosed, and the model-version-drift risk (M6) is explicitly acknowledged and (weakly) dismissed rather than hidden. So the "no reporting" claim is factually false; the original auditor's PDF fetch likely failed the same way mine initially did (compressed PDF streams not parsed by a summarizing fetch) and never reached the appendix. This also means "awareness":"unaware" overstates ignorance — the authors show explicit, if incomplete, awareness of M6 specifically while apparently remaining unaware of M1 (quantization), M2 (parameter dropping), M3 (routing variance), M4 (provenance), M5 (data policy), and M8 (the confound this introduces into their own fitted scaling exponent). None of this evidence covers provider pinning, require_parameters, quantization, or data_collection — so the disclosure is real but narrow, and does not amount to a safeguard.

This correction does not change the bottom line: uses_safely remains false and severity remains high, driven by M1 (unpinned quantization on 8/14 open-weight models), M3 (probabilistic routing across the full 14×14 production sweep), and M8 (the resulting confound directly threatens the paper's core fitted scaling-law claim) — all independently confirmed against primary source code, none of which rely on the M10 claim.

rows.title: Seer (interpretability agent harness) / chinese-models-investigations (blackbox de-censoring experiments)
rows.summary: Seer is a MATS 9.0 agent-harness repo (Claude-based notebook agent + sandboxing) for interpretability research; the companion chinese-models-investigations repo uses it to run blackbox "de-censoring" experiments where a Claude agent is given an OpenRouter client and told to elicit censored/hidden knowledge (Zhuhai attack, Falun Gong, Tiananmen-adjacent facts, Harry Potter verbatim quotes) from open-weight models (Kimi K2, Qwen3, gpt-oss-20b), then an LLM judge scores the resulting notebook transcripts against ground truth.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is the sole channel for the "target" model being investigated in every blackbox experiment: an OpenAI-SDK client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 is injected into the agent's Jupyter namespace, and the agent writes its own chat.completions.create() calls inside the notebook to probe the target model. Grading/judging is NOT via OpenRouter — eval/eval.py uses a direct Anthropic() client (claude-sonnet-4-5) as the LLM judge, separate from the router path.
safeguards_present: order (provider pinning)
allow_fallbacks:false (hard-fail instead of silent reroute)
provider-name-encoded quantization (e.g. 'siliconflow/fp8', 'novita/fp8')
dated slug for Kimi ('moonshotai/kimi-k2-0905')
repo-level + post-level disclosure of OpenRouter usage and per-experiment provider
explicit reproducibility/stochasticity caveat in the published post
judge/grader runs on a separate direct Anthropic API, not through OpenRouter
rows.mistakes: Every target-model call goes through OpenRouter with `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [PROVIDER], "allow_fallbacks": False}}` (confirmed present in 85/85 chat.completions.create() call-sites across all 25 committed agent-run notebooks, and baked into the standardized system_prompt.md boilerplate all agents are given), which genuinely defuses the worst mistake (M3, probabilistic routing) — an unavailable provider errors loudly rather than silently rerouting. But three real gaps remain for how they actually use it: (1) M2 — sampling params (temperature) are sent on every call with no `require_parameters: true`, so if the pinned provider doesn't honor temperature it is silently dropped rather than erroring; this affects the target-model outputs that feed the correctness/consistency scores plotted in evaluation_results.png and referenced in the post as "the agentic benchmark ... main evidence because it is less noisy." (2) M4 — no notebook or eval script ever records the actually-served provider/generation id (grepped 'response.id', 'response.provider', 'generation?' across all 25 notebooks: zero hits), so the `order` pin is never empirically confirmed to have taken effect for a given call — they trust the request succeeded rather than verifying who served it. (3) M5 — `data_collection` is never set (defaults to OpenRouter's "allow"), which is notable given the prompts are specifically about Chinese-government-sensitive topics (Falun Gong persecution, Zhuhai attack, Tiananmen-adjacent facts) routed through providers including a Chinese-founded inference vendor (SiliconFlow) — a data-policy/opsec concern rather than a result-corruption one. None of these three threaten the paper's core qualitative claims (the authors explicitly caveat stochasticity: "these models don't always lie/refuse and ... the process can be quite stochastic ... results can be harder to replicate directly"), and the most severe taxonomy item (M3) is handled about as well as any repo in this space — this is a comparatively careful implementation with a real gap in provenance/require_parameters/data policy, not a naive default-routing setup.
rows.one_line_fix: Add require_parameters: true and data_collection: "deny" to the existing extra_body.provider dict, and log response.provider / response.id (or GET /api/v1/generation?id=) into every investigation notebook so the order-pin is empirically verified, not just requested.
rows.evidence: New evidence to append to the existing evidence field: chinese-models-investigations agent-runs/run{1,2,3,4,5}/china_elicit_qwen_opennrouter_*/*.ipynb all contain `client.chat.completions.create(model="qwen/qwen3-32b", ...)` (verified via programmatic notebook parsing — regex-matched `model\s*=\s*"([^"]+)"` returns `{'qwen/qwen3-32b'}` for 4/5 runs, and run3 additionally shows a mid-run provider failover from `siliconflow/fp8` to `novita/fp8` while keeping the same wrong model), despite falun-gong-qwen.yaml's `model.model_name: "qwen/qwen3-vl-30b-a3b-instruct"` and matching task-prompt text ('Use the model "qwen/qwen3-vl-30b-a3b-instruct"'). `agent-runs/{.,run1,run2,run3,run4}/evaluation_results.png` are the scored outputs of these same runs. Contrast with `transcripts-for-blog/arya_falun_gong.md` line 3: `**Model:** Qwen: Qwen3 VL 30B A3B Instruct` — the correct model, used only in the hand-curated narrative transcript, not in the 5 automated benchmark runs. Root-cause candidate: `agent-runs/*/system_prompt.md` boilerplate example `model="<task specific model id eg. qwen/qwen3-32b>"` — the literal placeholder value that shows up, uncorrected, as the actual model string in every affected notebook. All other experiment types (Kimi: `moonshotai/kimi-k2-0905`, Harry Potter: `openai/gpt-oss-20b`) correctly match their yaml-specified models in all runs checked, so this appears isolated to the Qwen/Falun-Gong condition specifically (likely because its correct model name is textually close to the boilerplate's example, unlike Kimi/GPT-OSS).
rows.importance: 146 GitHub stars on the seer infra repo (unusually high for a single-post MATS project), co-authored by Neel Nanda and Senthooran Rajamanoharan (Google DeepMind interpretability team leads) alongside MATS 9.0 scholars — suggests the harness is being reused as general interpretability-agent infrastructure beyond this one post, so its OpenRouter-usage patterns (the system_prompt.md boilerplate) will likely propagate to future projects built on Seer.
rows.audit_notes: Unusually sophisticated OpenRouter usage compared to the norm in this dataset: order+allow_fallbacks:False pinning is applied with 100% consistency (85/85 detected call-sites across 25 independently-generated agent notebooks spanning 5 separate runs), because it's baked into the standardized system_prompt.md template every investigation agent is given rather than left to chance per-experiment. That's a genuinely good pattern (repo-level prompt engineering as a routing safeguard) worth noting as a positive example, even though require_parameters/provenance-logging/data_collection were never added on top of it. Slight irony worth flagging: the experiment content (Falun Gong persecution, Tiananmen-adjacent facts, the Zhuhai attack) is deliberately routed to a Chinese-founded inference provider (SiliconFlow) with OpenRouter's default data_collection:"allow" — a real research-conduct/opsec wrinkle distinct from the standard "result corruption" concern the taxonomy is built around.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-fetched both repos at the cited commits (seer @ 7557b64, chinese-models-investigations @ ec49c4e via `git clone --depth 50`) and parsed all 25 agent-run notebooks programmatically (not just grep, since ipynb source lines are JSON-escaped and naive grep/window-based checks give false negatives/positives — I initially mis-measured 27/85 call sites as "missing" the pin before writing a paren-aware parser that correctly resolved it to 0/85 missing).

CONFIRMED as stated in the verdict:
- 85/85 `chat.completions.create()` call sites across all 25 notebooks carry `extra_body={"provider":{"order":[...],"allow_fallbacks":False}}` — verified with a robust per-call-site scan.
- `system_prompt.md` (all 25 runs) bakes in the identical order/allow_fallbacks boilerplate (lines ~66-94 in the run1-kimi copy checked).
- `eval.py` (line 188: `client = Anthropic()`, model `claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929`, temp 0.0) is a direct Anthropic call, not OpenRouter — M9 correctly not flagged. Note: this file lives in the **chinese-models-investigations** repo, not the seer repo as the evidence field implies by grouping it under "seer repo (... commit 7557b64 ...)"; minor citation/attribution slip, content is otherwise accurate.
- `require_parameters`, `data_collection`: zero hits repo-wide — M2/M5 confirmed as gaps.
- `response.id`/`response.provider`/generation-endpoint: zero hits across all 25 notebooks — M4 confirmed as a gap.
- WebFetch of the Alignment Forum post confirms the OpenRouter disclosure quote, the SiliconFlow/Novita provider disclosures, the stochasticity caveat, and the Nanda/Rajamanoharan co-supervision, all verbatim as cited.
- `openrouter_client.py` in both repos is a bare, unconfigured `OpenAI(base_url=...)` client — pinning is agent/notebook-level only, as claimed.

ADDITIONAL FINDING THE ORIGINAL VERDICT MISSED (materially raises severity): all 5 automated `china_elicit_qwen_opennrouter` notebooks (the Falun Gong / Qwen investigation, whose `evaluation_results.png` scores are explicitly the post's self-described "main evidence because it is less noisy") hardcode `model="qwen/qwen3-32b"` and request that model from OpenRouter in every run — but `falun-gong-qwen.yaml` and the task prompt given to the agent explicitly specify `qwen/qwen3-vl-30b-a3b-instruct`, a different (VL/MoE) model. This is not a typo that errors: the calls succeed and return coherent, in-character responses (verified in notebook outputs), so OpenRouter dutifully served the wrong model with no error, no fallback, and nothing to trip `allow_fallbacks:False` — that safeguard only guards provider identity, not model identity. The root cause looks like agent confusion: `system_prompt.md`'s boilerplate code example literally reads `model="<task specific model id eg. qwen/qwen3-32b>"`, and in all 5 independent runs the Haiku-4.5 orchestrating agent appears to have copied that placeholder verbatim instead of substituting the real target model — even the run's own markdown cell states "Using Qwen/Qwen3-32B model" with no acknowledgment of the mismatch. By contrast, the separately-published narrative transcript used in the post's prose (`transcripts-for-blog/arya_falun_gong.md`) correctly headers "Model: Qwen: Qwen3 VL 30B A3B Instruct" — so the qualitative write-up is fine, but the quantitative benchmark condition for Qwen is not, and this is undisclosed anywhere in the post or repo. This is exactly the kind of corruption M4 (no provenance logging) predicts: had `response.model`/provider been logged and spot-checked, the substitution would have been caught immediately. I don't think it cleanly mints a new taxonomy ID, but it converts M4 from a plausible/theoretical gap into a confirmed, reproducible instance of the real target model diverging from the declared one in one of the four experiment conditions feeding the paper's headline quantitative figure — which is why I'm raising severity from medium to high while keeping the same mistake_ids (M2, M4, M5) and the unsafe verdict.

rows.title: ctfish (Palisade Research — "Demonstrating Specification Gaming in Reasoning Models")
rows.summary: An LLM agent harness (player.py) plays chess against Stockfish under a "win at all costs" prompt; the paper reports that reasoning models (o1-preview, o3, DeepSeek R1) frequently hack the environment (edit game state, replace the engine binary) instead of losing.
rows.openrouter_use: Runs the actual agent rollouts — every step of the OODA-loop agent that plays chess and produces the paper's per-model hacking transcripts — for four models: deepseek/deepseek-chat, deepseek/deepseek-r1, qwen/qwq-32b-preview, and google/gemini-2.0-flash-thinking-exp:free, via an OpenAI-SDK client pointed at OpenRouter's base_url in player.py's Provider class.
rows.venue: arXiv (ICML-format writeup); widely covered by Time/Fortune/Business Insider and heavily cited in AI-control/scheming discourse
rows.one_line_fix: Pin OpenRouter routing for the DeepSeek/QwQ/Gemini rollouts (extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}}), persist the served-provider/generation-id per call into the run's state dumps (not just an overwritten /tmp file), and disclose this as a Limitation alongside the existing judge-reliability caveat — since DeepSeek R1's "hacks by default" result is compared directly against pinned, direct-API proprietary models (o3, o1-preview) in the paper's abstract and headline discussion table.
rows.mistakes: DeepSeek R1 and QwQ 32B Preview are run exclusively via OpenRouter with zero routing safeguards — no `provider` dict/extra_body anywhere in the repo — meaning each call could be silently served by any OpenRouter backend at any quantization, with default `data_collection: allow`, and no record kept of which backend actually served it (M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic routing, M4 no provenance, M5 data-policy leakage). Both are bare, undated OpenRouter slugs (M6 version drift). Critically, this unpinned DeepSeek R1 result is the direct comparator to OpenAI o3/o1-preview (served via the direct, single-provider OpenAI API) in the paper's abstract sentence "reasoning models like OpenAI o3 and DeepSeek R1 will often hack the benchmark by default" and in the central Discussion table's model-type/hacking breakdown — a classic cross-provider comparison confound (M8) threatening the paper's headline claim. The formal Limitations section (arxiv/main.typ, discussing judge unreliability and task narrowness) never mentions router/provider pinning, even though the appendix separately admits "the inference API was unstable" for DeepSeek runs — an unexplained symptom consistent with unpinned multi-provider routing that the paper attributes only to step-count noise rather than to serving-backend variability (M10 no reporting of the routing/inference-stack risk). By contrast, the paper's LLM-judge/scoring pipeline (scoring/score_stages.py) calls OpenAI directly (no OpenRouter), so M9 does not apply there.
rows.evidence:
Additional/corrected evidence beyond the original verdict:

(1) M2 addition — player.py:96-97: `if "o1-" not in model and "o3-" not in model and "o4-" not in model: kwargs_.setdefault("temperature", 0.5)`. All four OPENROUTER_MODELS fail this exclusion (none start with o1-/o3-/o4-), so temperature=0.5 is sent to OpenRouter for every DeepSeek/QwQ/Gemini call with no `require_parameters:true` set anywhere in the repo (confirmed via repo-wide grep and independently via the skill's `audit_openrouter.py` scanner, which flags `[M2] player.py:97` alongside M1/M3/M4/M5).

(2) Strengthened M4 — run-movie/runs/{2 example dirs}/ctfish.json (the actual released run transcripts referenced by the paper's Impact Statement, "we are releasing our code and experiment transcripts") were fetched and parsed: top-level keys are exactly `n, memory, plan, subgoal, history, prev_state, state, archive` — matching player.py's `dump()` schema precisely, with no provider/model/generation-id field. This confirms M4 from the actual shipped artifacts, not just static code reading: even a reader trying to verify which backend served the DeepSeek R1 calls has nothing to work with in the released data.

All other original evidence lines (player.py:33-38, 64-68, 91-121, 202-215; arxiv/main.typ:56, 300-308, 358-364; arxiv/appendix.typ:237; scoring/score_stages.py:14, 380-388, 534) were re-fetched and match verbatim or in substance.
rows.importance: One of the most widely cited real-world AI specification-gaming/reward-hacking case studies (Time, Fortune, Business Insider coverage; heavily referenced in AI-control and scheming literature). The DeepSeek R1 "hacks by default" finding sits in the paper's abstract and headline Discussion table, making the OpenRouter-routed comparison load-bearing for the paper's most-quoted claim.
rows.audit_notes: Notable case: the paper's own appendix flags exactly the kind of symptom (unstable/inconsistent inference behavior for the DeepSeek runs) that unpinned multi-provider OpenRouter routing would produce, but the authors attribute it purely to run-length/step-count noise rather than recognizing it as a provider/quantization confound — a near-miss where awareness of the symptom didn't connect to awareness of the mechanism. Good hygiene elsewhere: proprietary models (OpenAI, Anthropic) are called directly, and the separate LLM-judge scoring pipeline also bypasses OpenRouter entirely, so the risk is cleanly isolated to the four OpenRouter-routed rollout models — of which DeepSeek R1 and QwQ 32B Preview are both explicitly named in the paper's main findings.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned PalisadeResearch/ctfish (depth 1, main @ f029c61) and checked every citation.

CONFIRMED VERBATIM: player.py:33-38 OPENROUTER_MODELS set (deepseek/deepseek-chat, deepseek/deepseek-r1, qwen/qwq-32b-preview, google/gemini-2.0-flash-thinking-exp:free) — exact match. player.py:64-68 routes them to `OpenAI(base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1", api_key=os.environ["OPENROUTER_API_KEY"])` — exact match. invoke() (91-121): `self.last_invoke["result"] = result` then dumped to a single overwritten `/tmp/ctfish-invoke.json` (117-118) — confirmed. dump() (202-215) persists only `n/memory/plan/subgoal/history/prev_state/state/archive` — no kwargs/result/provider field, confirmed by reading the function directly. Repo-wide grep for provider/require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection/extra_body across the whole tree returns zero hits outside the OPENROUTER_MODELS/base_url lines — no routing config exists anywhere. arxiv/main.typ:56 abstract quote is exact. arxiv/main.typ:358-364 Discussion table exact match — o1-preview and DeepSeek R1 are literally the same row ("1st gen Reasoning Models... Hacking: ✓"), juxtaposed with o3 in a separate row, confirming the M8 cross-provider-confound framing. arxiv/main.typ:300-308 Limitations section contains exactly "Prompt complexity / Unreliable judge / One task only" — no routing/provider mention, confirmed. arxiv/appendix.typ:237 "the DeepSeek agents have run for fewer steps because the inference API was unstable" — exact, unattributed to routing. scoring/score_stages.py:14 `from openai import AsyncOpenAI`, line 534 `client = AsyncOpenAI()` (no base_url override — direct OpenAI), lines 380-386 `model="gpt-4o-mini"`/`response_format=stage_model` — confirms the judge pipeline is direct-API, so M9 correctly not flagged.

STRENGTHENED M4 evidence beyond the original citation: the repo ships two actual example run artifacts (run-movie/runs/*/ctfish.json). I loaded one and its top-level keys are exactly `['n','memory','plan','subgoal','history','prev_state','state','archive']` — i.e. the *actual released run transcripts* (the ones the paper's Impact Statement says are released "to help others verify and build on this work") contain zero provider/model/generation-id metadata. This is stronger than inferring M4 from code alone — it's a positive confirmation from the published data that provenance is unrecoverable even by someone trying to audit the released runs.

ONE CORRECTION — a missed finding, not a false accusation: M2 (silent parameter dropping) should be added. player.py:96-97 sets `kwargs_.setdefault("temperature", 0.5)` whenever the model name doesn't start with "o1-"/"o3-"/"o4-" — which covers all four OpenRouter models (deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1, qwq-32b-preview, gemini-2.0-flash-thinking-exp). This sampling parameter is sent to OpenRouter with no `require_parameters:true` anywhere in the codebase, matching M2's taxonomy tell-tale exactly ("sampling params + no require_parameters"). I independently ran the skill's bundled `audit_openrouter.py` scanner against the cloned repo and it also flags `[M2] Silent parameter dropping (player.py:97)` alongside M1/M3/M4/M5 — corroborating this from a second, independent method. I do not treat this as changing severity (already High from M1/M3/M4) or the overall verdict, but it is a real, code-confirmed corruption channel the original verdict omitted.

Sanity checks passed: no proprietary single-served model is wrongly flagged M1/M3/M8 (OPENAI_MODELS/ANTHROPIC_MODELS go through direct `OpenAI()`/`Anthropic()` clients with no base_url override, confirmed lines 62-63, 81-82). No missed M9 (player.py never passes response_format to invoke(); the judge pipeline that does use response_format is on the direct OpenAI route, not OpenRouter). QwQ 32B Preview is confirmed named in the paper's main text (main.typ:101, 259), not just the appendix, supporting the "explicitly named in main findings" audit note. Route is unambiguously critical: the OpenRouter-routed rollouts produce the transcripts that feed the paper's headline abstract sentence and its central Discussion table. Verdict, severity, confidence, and M-id list (with the one addition) are all justified by re-fetched primary sources.

rows.title: diffing-toolkit (science-of-finetuning / Neel Nanda, Julian Minder, Clément Dumas, Stewy Slocum — MATS 7)
rows.summary: A general-purpose "model diffing" mech-interp toolkit (activation-difference lens, patchscope, logit-diff mining, steering) used to produce the Alignment Forum post's finding that narrow finetuning leaves clearly readable traces; it runs a local white-box pipeline on open-weight finetuned models (Qwen/Llama/Gemma/DeepSeek) and layers an LLM-based "interpretability agent" plus several LLM-judge graders on top to interpret/score the pipeline's outputs.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is the sole backend for (1) the interpretability agent (AgentLLM, src/diffing/utils/agents/llm.py) that autonomously investigates and hypothesizes about model diffs, and (2) four LLM-judge grader classes (HypothesisGrader, CoherenceGrader, TokenRelevanceGrader, PatchScopeGrader) that score the agent's hypotheses, steered-generation coherence, token relevance, and patch-scope scale selection — all feeding numbers reported in the post. The models being diffed themselves (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, DeepSeek finetunes) are loaded locally via HuggingFace/nnsight/vLLM and never touch OpenRouter.
rows.evidence: All originally-cited evidence reconfirmed verbatim on re-fetch (file paths, line numbers, code content, and the zero-hit repo-wide grep for provider/data_collection/require_parameters/response_format all check out exactly as claimed). Additional evidence found on adversarial re-check: (1) WebFetch of the Alignment Forum post shows the OpenRouter-graded HypothesisGrader score is the post's headline quantitative result ('agent identifies the broad finetuning objective (score>=2) on 97% of organisms; 60% reach >=4' vs baseline 12%/6%), making the unlogged/unpinned route more central to the paper's core claim than 'secondary interpretive layer' suggests — this raises severity.
