(2) configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml:17 currently sets the agent to `openai/gpt-5`, but the paper's footnote 9 states the agent used `openai/gpt-5-chat` — a concrete present-day mismatch between the disclosed model and the repo's actual configuration, directly evidencing the M6 (bare undated slug / model drift) risk rather than leaving it purely hypothetical.
rows.mistakes: The repo uses OpenRouter exclusively to call proprietary, single-vendor-served frontier models (OpenAI GPT-5 family, Google Gemini 2.5 family, Anthropic Claude Haiku 4.5) as the agent/judge backend, so M1 (quantization), M3 (probabilistic provider load-balancing), and M8 (cross-provider confound) do not meaningfully apply — these vendors' own models are not multi-provider-quantized on OpenRouter the way open-weight models are, and the repo never routes an open-weight model through OpenRouter (those are loaded locally via HF/nnsight). No response_format/JSON-schema constrained output is used anywhere (graders parse free text like "SCORE: <n>" / "COHERENT"), so M9 doesn't apply either. However, three real, verifiable gaps remain and all sit on a critical route (the grader/agent outputs feed the paper's reported coherence percentages, hypothesis-quality scores, token-relevance scores, and patch-scope scale selections): (1) M5 — `data_collection` is never set anywhere in the codebase, so it defaults to OpenRouter's "allow," meaning prompts containing the model-diffing artifacts under study (patchscope decodings, steered generations, activation-difference summaries) may be retained/used for training by whichever provider serves the call. (2) M6 — every grader/agent model is a bare, undated slug (`openai/gpt-5`, `openai/gpt-5-mini`, `openai/gpt-5-nano`, `google/gemini-2.5-pro`, etc.); if OpenAI/Google/Anthropic update the model behind these aliases (plausible for actively-iterated frontier models), a rerun would silently score with a different judge, threatening exact reproduction of the reported grading numbers. (3) M4 — no code anywhere stores which provider actually served a call, a completion/generation id, or any other provenance; combined with M6 this means drift in grader behavior over time would be undetectable and undiagnosable after the fact. A weaker, lower-confidence note: AgentLLM always sends `temperature=0.7` (and CoherenceGrader occasionally `temperature=0`) with no `require_parameters:true`, so if OpenRouter ever silently ignores temperature for a given proprietary route the agent's exploration diversity could change unnoticed (M2) — plausible but not confirmed, and low-impact since OpenAI's own chat-completions API has long supported temperature. None of this threatens the paper's primary, white-box evidence (activation-difference lens / logit-lens / patch-scope analysis on locally-loaded open-weight models), which is the strongest support for the headline claim; the OpenRouter-based agent/judge layer is a secondary interpretive/scoring layer, and the authors do build in some redundancy (a multi-judge "grader_model_id" robustness sweep across OpenAI/Google/Anthropic judges analyzed via a hierarchical Bayesian model, plus `num_repeat` resampling) that would tend to surface — though not fully diagnose — gross judge instability.
rows.one_line_fix: Set `provider: {data_collection: "deny", require_parameters: true}` in every OpenRouter config and log the response's served-provider / generation id alongside each agent and grader call (e.g. persist `completion.id` and call `GET /api/v1/generation?id=...`), and pin/record the exact dated model snapshot actually used for the agent and each grader.
rows.importance: Co-authored by Neel Nanda (Google DeepMind interpretability lead, one of the most prominent mech-interp researchers), plus Julian Minder, Clément Dumas, Stewy Slocum (MATS 7). 78 GitHub stars, actively developed general-purpose toolkit whose LLM-agent/grader pipeline directly produces reported scores in the post.
rows.audit_notes: Notable positive: the authors run a genuine grader-robustness check, comparing judge scores across seven different proprietary judge models (GPT-5/mini/nano, Gemini 2.5 Pro/Flash/Flash-lite, Claude Haiku 4.5) as a categorical covariate in a hierarchical Bayesian model (narrow_ft_experiments/hibayes/*/config.yaml, main_effects: ["grader_model_id", ...]) — this is a reasonable, if incomplete, mitigation for single-judge idiosyncrasy, though it does not substitute for provider pinning or provenance logging. Also notable: `configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml:17` has a commented-out `# anthropic/claude-sonnet-4` alternative next to the active `openai/gpt-5` agent model, showing the authors were aware model choice for the agent was a live variable but didn't formalize pinning around it. I could not verify live OpenRouter provider-list behavior for `openai/gpt-5*` slugs (e.g., whether OpenRouter ever serves it via Azure vs OpenAI directly, or whether temperature is honored) — this affects confidence on the M2/M4 practical-risk sizing but not on whether the safeguards are present in code (they are absent, confirmed by exhaustive grep).
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned science-of-finetuning/diffing-toolkit (shallow, HEAD) and checked every cited file/line directly; all evidence checks out verbatim. src/diffing/utils/agents/llm.py:49 is exactly `OpenAI(base_url=self.base_url, api_key=api_key)`, and :57-62 is the bare `chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=..., temperature=..., max_tokens=...)` call with no provider/extra_body kwarg. src/diffing/utils/graders/grader.py:42 is `AsyncOpenAI(base_url=base_url, api_key=api_key)`; the `call_params` dict build (170-177) and `create(**call_params)` (181) match exactly, and `_call_with_retry` never injects a `provider` key. configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml:6/17 and configs/diffing/method/activation_difference_lens.yaml:57/72/115 all confirmed verbatim (openai/gpt-5-mini, openai/gpt-5, openai/gpt-5-nano, all bare undated slugs, base_url=openrouter.ai/api/v1). A repo-wide grep (`provider|extra_body|require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection|response_format|json_schema|generation_id`) across every .py/.yaml file returned zero hits, confirming no safeguards exist anywhere and also confirming M9 does not apply (graders parse free-text 'SCORE: <n>' / 'ANSWER: COHERENT' via regex, not structured output — verified in coherence_grader.py and hypothesis_grader.py). Confirmed all seven judge models used in the hibayes robustness sweep (openai/gpt-5, gpt-5-mini, gpt-5-nano, google/gemini-2.5-pro/flash/flash-lite, anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5) are proprietary single-vendor models never routed as open-weight, so M1/M3/M8 are correctly excluded per the taxonomy's own LEGIT carve-out. WebFetch of the Alignment Forum post confirmed footnote 9 verbatim ('The interpretability agent is based on openai/gpt-5-chat as provided on openrouter.ai') and confirmed the README never mentions OpenRouter at all, matching the 'aware_partial' characterization. The hierarchical-Bayesian grader-robustness sweep (main_effects incl. 'grader_model_id') is real and confirmed in narrow_ft_experiments/hibayes/*/config.yaml. All of this supports the original uses_safely=false call and the M4/M5/M6 mistake_ids as written — no false accusation and no missed corruption channel (M2/M7/M10/M11/M12 also correctly left unflagged: no seed usage, no :floor/sort:price, no direct+router mixing found).

However two findings justify raising severity from medium to high rather than confirming it unchanged: (1) I independently WebFetched the AF post's actual headline numbers and found the OpenRouter-judged HypothesisGrader score IS the post's single most-cited quantitative result ('the ADL-enabled agent identifies the broad finetuning objective (score>=2) on 97% of organisms; 60% reach >=4' vs a blackbox baseline's 12%/6%) — this contradicts the original verdict's own framing that the OpenRouter layer is merely a 'secondary interpretive/scoring layer' behind 'the paper's primary, white-box evidence.' The critical_route:true flag is correct, but the route is more central than the write-up credits, and M4 (no provenance logging) is nominally High severity in the taxonomy and sits directly on this headline-metric route with zero mitigation. (2) New evidence not in the original review: the current repo HEAD (configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml:17) configures the agent as `openai/gpt-5`, while the paper's own footnote 9 states the agent used `openai/gpt-5-chat` — a live, already-manifested mismatch between what was reported and what the repository would run today, i.e. concrete evidence of the exact M6 drift risk actually occurring, not merely a theoretical exposure. Combined, these two points move this from 'plausible medium-severity gap' to 'demonstrated drift + unlogged headline-metric route,' warranting high severity. Confidence stays medium: the code-level evidence is now confirmed with very high certainty (every citation matched exactly, comprehensive greps returned zero false hits), but real-world impact sizing still depends on facts I cannot verify (whether OpenRouter's provider list for gpt-5/gemini-2.5/claude-haiku-4.5 is genuinely single-provider with no drift, and whether the gpt-5 vs gpt-5-chat discrepancy reflects a meaningful behavioral change or just imprecise paper phrasing).

rows.title: lighteval — Swiss-Legal / LEXam LLM-as-judge tasks (JudgeLM litellm backend)
rows.summary: lighteval is Hugging Face's official eval library (2479 stars, powers the Open LLM Leaderboard). Its `swiss_legal` task module — shipped for the ICLR 2026 LEXam benchmark and companion Swiss-legal tasks (SLDS summarization, SLT translation) — scores model outputs with an LLM-as-judge, and several of the default/sweep judge-model configs are routed through OpenRouter with zero routing safeguards.
rows.openrouter_use: LLM-as-judge backend for two of the three Swiss-legal task families: (1) SLDS (Swiss Landmark Decision Summarization) — the *default* judge is `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat` (open-weight DeepSeek-V3), used unconditionally by `_get_metrics()` for every SLDS subtask; (2) SLT (Swiss Legal Translation) — the `JUDGE_MODELS` sweep routes o1, gpt-4o-mini, gpt-4o, and llama-3.3-70b-instruct all through `openrouter/...` slugs, and `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` (metrics.py:980) makes this sweep the default metric set for the task. In both cases the judge score IS the reported corpus-level metric (`corpus_level_fn=statistics.mean`), not an intermediate/exploratory signal. By contrast, the paper's flagship LEXam open-question judge (`get_lexam_oq_judge()`, main.py:421) defaults to `openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20` with no `openrouter/` prefix — litellm routes that directly to OpenAI, not through OpenRouter — so the headline LEXam OQ/MCQ numbers are likely unaffected; the exposure is in the companion SLDS/SLT benchmarks living in the same module.
rows.venue: ICLR 2026 (LEXam: Benchmarking Legal Reasoning on 340 Law Exams)
rows.importance: 2479 GitHub stars; official HF eval library used for the Open LLM Leaderboard and many papers; LEXam is an accepted ICLR 2026 paper whose judge metric ships directly in this widely-used library, so any routing defect propagates to every downstream user of the SLDS/SLT tasks, not just the original authors.
rows.one_line_fix: In `JudgeLM.__call_litellm` (src/lighteval/metrics/utils/llm_as_judge.py:333-339), when `self.model` starts with "openrouter/" add `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}}` and persist `response.model` / the OpenRouter generation id alongside each judge score instead of discarding it after extracting `.content`.
rows.mistakes: The judge-call path shared by all Swiss-legal metrics is `JudgeLM.__call_litellm` (src/lighteval/metrics/utils/llm_as_judge.py:305-371). Its kwargs dict is `{"model", "messages", "n": 1, "caching": True, "response_format": self.response_format}` plus `max_tokens` — no `provider` key at all, so every "openrouter/"-prefixed model gets OpenRouter's default behavior: no `quantizations` pin (M1), no `order`/`only`/`sort` so requests load-balance across providers (M3), no `data_collection: "deny"` so prompts (Swiss court judgments/legal exam text) default to "allow" at providers that train on data (M5), and the function returns only `response.choices[0].message.content` (line 348/352) — the served provider / generation id is never captured or stored anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via `grep -rIn` for extra_body/require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection/generation_id across src/: zero hits) (M4). Concretely: `get_swiss_landmark_decision_summarization_judge(..., model_name: str = "openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat", ...)` (metrics.py:795) is the *unconditional default* judge for every SLDS headnote-quality score (wired in via `main.py:320-322`, no override) — DeepSeek-V3 behind a bare, undated alias (`deepseek-chat`, M6) served by whichever of DeepSeek's many OpenRouter providers/quantizations wins that request, with the SLDS score being the exact number reported for that model/language. Separately, `JUDGE_MODELS` (metrics.py:928-932) maps `"o1"→"openrouter/openai/o1"`, `"gpt-4o-mini"→"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"`, `"gpt-4o"→"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"`, `"llama-3-3-70b"→"openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct"`, and with `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` hardcoded (metrics.py:980), all of these feed `JUDGE_METRICS` and become the default SLT-judge metric sweep — comparing a proprietary trio against an unpinned open-weight 70B judge with identical (absent) safeguards on both sides (M8: any "which judge best matches humans" conclusion drawn from this sweep is confounded by DeepSeek/Llama quantization variance that GPT-4o/o1 don't share). Note also `init_llm_judge_metric` (metrics.py:1054-1058) builds `"slt_judge_gpt_4o"` from `judge_model_name="openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` (direct OpenAI, no "openrouter/" prefix) while `JUDGE_MODELS["gpt-4o"]` builds the *same* checkpoint through `"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` — the identical model string is called via two different backends in the same file (M11 silent backend mixing); the inline comment at metrics.py:929 ("Updated to match OpenRouter availability; previously: openai/o1-2024-12-17") confirms this migration happened without adding any pinning/provenance. Finally, neither the repo's README/docs nor the paper abstract (checked via WebFetch on arxiv.org/abs/2505.12864) mention OpenRouter, provider routing, or judge-output reproducibility at all (M10) — the only two files in the repo mentioning "openrouter" are the ones cited above, and both are purely functional (routing the call / estimating context length via OpenRouter's endpoints API), never provenance-related. Threatened results: the SLDS headnote-quality scores and the SLT judge-agreement/quality comparisons across judge models — both of which are the literal `corpus_level_fn` numbers lighteval reports for these Swiss-legal tasks. The LEXam paper's own headline open-question judge score is not implicated by this specific defect since its default (`openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20`, no "openrouter/" prefix) resolves to direct OpenAI, not OpenRouter.
rows.evidence: src/lighteval/metrics/utils/llm_as_judge.py:333-339 `kwargs = {"model": self.model, "messages": prompt, "n": 1, "caching": True, "response_format": self.response_format}` (no provider dict); line 314 `litellm.drop_params = True`; line 348 `text = response.choices[0].message.content` (provider/generation-id discarded). src/lighteval/tasks/multilingual/tasks/swiss_legal/metrics.py:795 `model_name: str = "openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat"` (SLDS default judge, wired in unconditionally at main.py:320-322). metrics.py:928-932 `JUDGE_MODELS = {"o1": "openrouter/openai/o1", "gpt-4o-mini": "openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18", "gpt-4o": "openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20", "llama-3-3-70b": "openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct"}`; metrics.py:980 `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` (makes this sweep the default METRICS_TO_USE). metrics.py:701-703 and main.py:421 show the LEXam OQ judge instead defaults to `"openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` (no openrouter prefix → direct OpenAI). src/lighteval/models/endpoints/litellm_model.py:301 `model_name = self.model.removeprefix("openrouter/")` and line 297 `"Failed to fetch model endpoint info from OpenRouter, returning default max length."` confirm OpenRouter-aware code exists only for context-length lookup, not provenance/pinning. `grep -rIn "extra_body\|require_parameters\|quantizations\|data_collection\|generation_id" src/` on a full shallow clone returned zero matches repo-wide; `grep -rIln -i openrouter src/` returned only the two files above; README/docs contain no "openrouter" mentions. arXiv abstract (2505.12864) fetched via WebFetch mentions only "ensemble LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm with rigorous human expert validation" — no mention of OpenRouter, provider routing, or judge reproducibility.
rows.audit_notes: Nuance worth flagging: the code shows the authors migrated at least one judge model from direct provider access to OpenRouter over time (metrics.py:929 comment: "Updated to match OpenRouter availability; previously: openai/o1-2024-12-17") without adding any pinning/provenance — a live example of M11 backend drift happening mid-project rather than being designed in from the start. Also spotted an apparent unrelated bug at llm_as_judge.py:341 (`kwargs["max_tokens"] = (max_new_tokens,)` — assigns a 1-tuple instead of an int) which would itself likely trigger silent parameter mishandling given `litellm.drop_params=True`; not part of the OpenRouter taxonomy per se but compounds the "silent failure" theme of this call path. The fair reading here is important: the ICLR-paper's own headline LEXam judge is clean (direct OpenAI), so this is not a case of "the flagship benchmark number is corrupted" — it's "a widely-used library ships an OpenRouter-default judge path, with real reported numbers (SLDS) riding on it, with none of the standard safeguards and no disclosure."
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned huggingface/lighteval (HEAD 64f4f5a, main branch, 2026-06-29) and checked every cited line. All evidence checks out essentially verbatim:

- src/lighteval/metrics/utils/llm_as_judge.py:333-339 — kwargs dict is exactly {"model","messages","n":1,"caching":True,"response_format":self.response_format}, no "provider" key. Line 314 `litellm.drop_params = True` confirmed. Line 348 `text = response.choices[0].message.content` confirmed (provider/generation id discarded, never captured anywhere). Also independently confirmed the audit_notes bug: line 341 `kwargs["max_tokens"] = (max_new_tokens,)` is genuinely a 1-tuple, not an int.
- evaluate_answer_batch → __lazy_load_client() → case "litellm": return self.__call_litellm confirmed, so backend="litellm" (the default for every Swiss-legal judge) really does route through this exact vulnerable code path.
- metrics.py:795 `model_name: str = "openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat"` (SLDS default judge) confirmed; main.py:321-323 confirms `get_swiss_landmark_decision_summarization_judge(language=headnote_language)` is called with no override, unconditionally wired into `_get_metrics`.
- metrics.py:928-932 JUDGE_MODELS dict confirmed verbatim, including the "Updated to match OpenRouter availability; previously: openai/o1-2024-12-17" comment. metrics.py:980 `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` confirmed, making JUDGE_METRICS (built from JUDGE_MODELS) the default METRICS_TO_USE.
- metrics.py:701-703 confirms the LEXam OQ judge default is `"openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` — no "openrouter/" prefix — so litellm routes it directly to OpenAI; main.py:421 `metrics=[get_lexam_oq_judge()]` confirmed. This means the paper's own flagship metric is indeed unaffected by this specific defect, exactly as the verdict states.
- litellm_model.py:301 `model.removeprefix("openrouter/")` and line 297 fallback warning confirmed verbatim — OpenRouter-aware code exists only for context-length estimation, not provenance.
- Repo-wide grep for extra_body/require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection/generation_id returned zero relevant hits (one unrelated hit in nanotron_model.py referring to token generation_ids, not OpenRouter generation id). Grep for "openrouter" case-insensitive across src/ returned only the two cited files. README and docs contain no "openrouter" mention.
- WebFetch on arxiv.org/abs/2505.12864 confirms title "LEXam: Benchmarking Legal Reasoning on 340 Law Exams," accepted ICLR 2026, and no mention anywhere of OpenRouter/routing/reproducibility — matching M10.
- init_llm_judge_metric (metrics.py:1054-1058) confirmed to build "slt_judge_gpt_4o" from direct `"openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` while JUDGE_MODELS["gpt-4o"] builds the identical checkpoint via `"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` — genuine M11 backend mixing in the same file, though I'll note as a minor nuance the standalone "slt_judge_gpt_4o" metric name isn't itself a member of the default JUDGE_METRICS sweep (which only contains the "-style-suffix" combinations), so the two backends for that literal checkpoint aren't necessarily both exercised in a single default run — the mixing is a real latent code-hygiene defect rather than a live double-counting bug. This doesn't undermine the M11 tag, just slightly softens the "same run" framing.

Sanity checks against false accusation: the verdict correctly avoids tagging the proprietary o1/gpt-4o/gpt-4o-mini calls with M1 (quantization) in a way that ignores that OpenAI models are typically single-provider on OpenRouter — it scopes M1/M3's teeth to the open-weight DeepSeek-V3 and Llama-3.3-70B, and frames M8's cross-provider confound specifically as "DeepSeek/Llama quantization variance that GPT-4o/o1 don't share," which is the technically correct scoping. Checked for a missed M9 (judge on unconstrained route with response_format/JSON schema but no require_parameters): confirmed response_format is never set to a Pydantic/JSON-schema value for SLDS/SLT/LEXam-OQ judges (all three use free-text regex extraction), so M9 legitimately does not apply and its omission is correct, not an oversight. Also checked for M2/M7 (sampling params, seed) — no temperature/top_p/seed params are set by these judge calls at all, so nothing to silently drop/misinterpret; correctly omitted.

No corrections needed. The verdict's uses_safely=false, severity=high, confidence=high, and all eight M-ids (M1,M3,M4,M5,M6,M8,M10,M11) are fully justified by the re-fetched code, and the one nuance I'd add (M11's two backends aren't necessarily both hit in a single default run) is minor enough not to change the verdict.

rows.title: safety-tooling (safety-research org shared alignment-research inference library)
rows.summary: A shared, general-purpose async LLM inference wrapper (`InferenceAPI`) maintained by the safety-research/MATS/Anthropic-Fellows ecosystem and depended on by dozens of downstream alignment papers/repos to run model generations, evals, and data generation. It routes calls to many backends (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Together, HuggingFace, vLLM, DeepSeek-direct, and OpenRouter) behind one uniform `InferenceAPI.__call__` interface.
rows.openrouter_use: Provides `OpenRouterChatModel` (safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py), auto-selected by `InferenceAPI.model_id_to_class()` (safetytooling/apis/inference/api.py:312-313) whenever `model_id` is in the hardcoded `OPENROUTER_MODELS` set or starts with `"openrouter/"`, or via `force_provider="openrouter"`. `OPENROUTER_MODELS` (openrouter.py:15-30) is mostly open-weight models: `meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b(:free)`, `deepseek/deepseek-v3-base:free`, `deepseek/deepseek-r1-zero:free`, `nousresearch/hermes-3-llama-3.1-{70b,405b}`, `mistralai/mistral-*`, plus some proprietary-via-router entries (`x-ai/grok-4`, `google/gemini-2.0-flash-001`). README.md also documents a second path: calling OpenRouter through the plain `OpenAIChatModel` via `openai_base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` + `force_provider="openai"` (README.md:150-161) — a different code path than the dedicated OpenRouterChatModel class for the same underlying endpoint. Output (`LLMResponse.completion`, logprobs, etc.) is consumed by any downstream repo built on this infra for rollouts/evals/data generation, i.e. it is the generic model-calling substrate for a large share of independent alignment research.
rows.venue: Multiple (shared infra: Anthropic Fellows Program / MATS alignment-research ecosystem)
rows.importance: 132 stars but structurally high-leverage: shared inference infra reused as a dependency by dozens of downstream alignment/control repos (persona_vectors, false-facts, science-synth-facts, believe-it-or-not, introspection-mechanisms, SHADE-Arena-adjacent projects), so its OpenRouter defaults propagate into results reported by many independent papers.
rows.evidence: All original evidence citations confirmed verbatim against a fresh clone. Additional confirmed evidence to append: REPO_GUIDE.md:427-440 independently documents the same OpenRouter-via-OpenAIChatModel second path shown in README.md:150-161 (strengthens M11). data_models/inference.py:28's own comment ("Allow unknown kwargs e.g. response_format for OpenAI") plus OpenRouterChatModel.__call__'s unconditional **kwargs forward with no response_format special-casing (contrast openai/chat.py:250-256, which does special-case it) supports adding M9. `gh api repos/safety-research/safety-tooling` confirms 132 stars exactly. Multiple independent downstream repos (obalcells/hallucination_probes, andyrdt/rl_loans, eliaskempf/model-diffing) found via `gh search code` confirm real-world critical_route status.
rows.mistakes: This is shared infra, so the corrupting mistakes threaten every downstream repo's numbers that route open-weight models (Llama-3.1-405b, DeepSeek-v3/r1, Mistral-large, Hermes-3) through the default `OpenRouterChatModel`/`force_provider="openrouter"` path. M1 (no `quantizations` filter anywhere in the codebase): any eval/rollout/data-generation run against these open-weight models can silently be served at a different quantization each run, threatening reproducibility of any benchmark or generated dataset built on it. M2 (no `require_parameters`): sampling params like `temperature`, `logprobs`, `seed`, and arbitrary kwargs (LLMParams allows `extra="allow"`, e.g. `response_format`) are passed straight through with no guarantee the serving provider honors them — `test_openrouter_logprobs` even relies on `logprobs`+`temperature=0.0` being honored, unchecked. M3 (no `order`/`only`/`sort`): every call is subject to OpenRouter's default load-balanced routing, so repeated calls to the same "model_id" for a comparison or ablation study can hit different providers run-to-run. M4 (no provenance logging): `LLMResponse` has no field for which provider/generation actually served a call, so a downstream paper cannot audit after the fact which backend produced any given cached completion — this alone makes any claim about reproducibility of OpenRouter-derived results unverifiable. M5 (`data_collection` never set, defaults to "allow"): alignment-research prompts (which can include sensitive red-teaming/deception content) sent through this shared path may be used for provider training by default. M8 (cross-model comparison confound): this infra exists specifically to let downstream papers compare many models under one interface (that is its purpose across the alignment ecosystem); since none of the OpenRouter-routed open-weight models are pinned, any downstream cross-model comparison inherits unpinned provider/quantization variance as a confound. M10 (no reporting): README/CLAUDE.md/REPO_GUIDE.md never mention OpenRouter's provider-variance/quantization risk, so downstream authors adopting this infra are not warned to add safeguards themselves. M11 (silent backend mixing): the README's own documented alternative (OpenAIChatModel pointed at OpenRouter's base_url) and the dedicated OpenRouterChatModel class are two different code paths that can both serve the same "model" family via OpenRouter, with different kwargs-handling and error-parsing behavior, inviting inconsistent treatment of the same nominal model across a project.
rows.one_line_fix: In `OpenRouterChatModel.__call__` (openrouter.py), default every request to `extra_body={"provider": {"quantizations": ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny", "sort": "exacto"}}` (overridable per-call), and add a `provider`/`generation_id` field to `LLMResponse` populated from the raw `response_data` so every cached completion records exactly which backend served it.
rows.audit_notes: Notable: this is infra, not a paper — the risk is diffuse-but-wide rather than a single reported number. The library's design (LLMParams.model_config = ConfigDict(extra="allow")) is architected to transparently forward arbitrary kwargs to the underlying OpenAI-compatible client, so it is technically trivial for a downstream caller to pass `extra_body={"provider": {...}}` per-call — but nothing in the shared library defaults to it, documents it, or tests for it, and grep across the entire repo found zero instances of any downstream code in this repo doing so. Also worth flagging to the safety-research org: the comment in openai/chat.py ("OpenRouter routes through the error messages from the different providers, so we catch them here") shows the maintainers know OpenRouter has multiple backend providers, but the awareness is about error-message parsing, not about the reproducibility/quantization implications — a near-miss that stops short of the real issue.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned safety-research/safety-tooling (depth 1) and re-fetched every file cited in evidence; all checks out.

(1) openrouter.py fully re-read: OPENROUTER_MODELS (lines 16-34, verdict cited 15-30, off by one but content exact) contains 15 model IDs, mostly open-weight (llama-3.1 family, deepseek-v3-base:free, deepseek-r1-zero:free, hermes-3-llama-3.1-70b/405b, mistral-small-24b-instruct-2501) plus a few closed ones (x-ai/grok-4, google/gemini-2.0-flash-001, google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview, mistralai/mistral-large-2411) -- confirms 'model_type: mixed' is the right label, not a false M1/M3 accusation against a purely-proprietary target. __call__ (lines 240-399) builds `response_data = await self.aclient.chat.completions.create(messages=..., model=model_id, **kwargs)` with zero provider/extra_body construction anywhere in the class -- exactly as claimed.

(2) `grep -rn` across the whole repo for require_parameters, quantizations, data_collection, extra_body, and the literal string "provider" (as a dict key) in .py files: all five returned zero hits (grep exit 1). Confirms M1/M2/M3/M5 structurally -- nothing anywhere sets these.

(3) LLMResponse (data_models/inference.py:69-118) re-read in full: fields are model_id, completion, stop_reason, cost, generated_content, audio_out, duration, api_duration, logprobs, safety_ratings, recitation_retries, api_failures, batch_custom_id, usage -- no provider/generation_id field, confirming M4 (provenance structurally unrecoverable).

(4) api.py:283-322 `model_id_to_class` re-read: line 312-313 (`elif model_id in OPENROUTER_MODELS or model_id.startswith("openrouter/"): return self._openrouter`) matches exactly as cited.

(5) tests/test_other_apis.py:73-100 (test_openrouter_call) and 132-164 (test_openrouter_logprobs) re-read: the latter literally comments `temperature=0.0,  # Make output deterministic` and asserts only `responses[0].logprobs is not None` and a token match -- zero assertion on which provider served the call. Confirms the evidence precisely.

(6) README.md:150-161 re-read: confirms the second OpenRouter code path (OpenAIChatModel + custom openai_base_url + force_provider="openai") using model_id="deepseek/deepseek-v3-base:free" -- which is *also* in OPENROUTER_MODELS, i.e. the same nominal model is reachable via two different code paths with different kwargs-handling. I additionally found REPO_GUIDE.md:427-440 documents the identical pattern even more explicitly (`api_openrouter = InferenceAPI(openai_base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1", ...)`, `force_provider="openai"`) -- this strengthens M11 beyond what the original verdict cited.

(7) openai/chat.py:198-216 re-read: confirms the two 'OpenRouter routes through the error messages from different providers' / 'this sometimes happens on OpenRouter' comments exist exactly as described in audit_notes -- genuine awareness of multi-provider proxying but scoped only to error-message parsing, never to reproducibility/quantization.

(8) Grepped README.md, REPO_GUIDE.md, and CLAUDE.md (all three exist; CLAUDE.md re-read, 191 lines) for quantiz/reproduc/provider-routing/data-collection/require_parameters: zero hits anywhere. Confirms M10.

(9) `gh api repos/safety-research/safety-tooling` confirms exactly 132 stars, not private. `gh search code "safety-tooling"` and `gh search repos` surfaced multiple independent downstream repos depending on it as a git/submodule dependency (obalcells/hallucination_probes via pyproject.toml git rev=main, andyrdt/rl_loans via setup.sh, eliaskempf/model-diffing, aashrivastava/genai_final) -- corroborates the 'critical_route: true' / high-leverage claim; not overstated.

(10) One real gap I found in the ORIGINAL verdict (false-reassurance direction): M9 (judge/grader on unconstrained route) was not flagged despite solid code support. `LLMParams.model_config = ConfigDict(extra="allow")` in data_models/inference.py:28 explicitly cites `response_format` in its own comment as the rationale for allowing arbitrary kwargs, and `OpenRouterChatModel.__call__` forwards `**kwargs` straight into `chat.completions.create()` with no require_parameters and no special response_format handling (unlike openai/chat.py:250-256, which does special-case pydantic response_format types via the parse API -- OpenRouterChatModel has no equivalent, so a plain dict-schema response_format would pass through completely unconstrained). Since this infra's own summary states it's used for 'evals' across dozens of downstream repos, and LLM-as-judge with structured/JSON output is a standard eval pattern, this is a real structural M9 channel, not merely a subset of M2. I did not find a concrete downstream repo currently exercising response_format+OpenRouter together (gh code search for the combination returned no hits), so it's a latent/structural risk rather than a demonstrated live one -- but the LEGIT bar requires providers pinned AND provenance logged, neither of which holds, so M9 belongs in the list alongside M2 rather than being folded into it.

No evidence contradicts any of the original mistake_ids (M1,M2,M3,M4,M5,M8,M10,M11) or the severity/confidence/awareness/critical_route calls; all evidence citations resolve almost exactly to the real line numbers and content. The verdict is neither a false accusation (no provider pinning, no require_parameters, no provenance anywhere -- confirmed by exhaustive negative grep) nor false reassurance in its 'unsafe' conclusion, except it slightly under-counted by omitting M9.

rows.title: Evaluating LLMs for accuracy incentivizes hallucinations — experiment code (openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments)
rows.summary: Official OpenAI repo backing the Nature 2026 paper (Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang) showing accuracy-only grading incentivizes hallucination; a Jupyter notebook queries 4 frontier LLMs on the full SimpleQA test set under varying abstention penalties and grades every response with GPT-4.1 as an LLM judge, all via OpenRouter.
rows.openrouter_use: Both legs of the pipeline go through OpenRouter's /chat/completions endpoint via lm.py's LM.generate(): (1) generation — querying google/gemini-3-pro-preview, openai/gpt-5, x-ai/grok-4, anthropic/claude-opus-4.5 on all 4,326 SimpleQA questions x multiple abstention rewards x baseline/consistency-mitigation conditions x 2 seeds each; (2) grading — every answer is graded CORRECT/INCORRECT/NOT_ATTEMPTED by openai/gpt-4.1 using the SimpleQA grader template (free-text A/B/C output, retried until valid). Both outputs feed directly into the paper's reported hallucination/abstention/accuracy figures (figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf, figures/simpleqa_abstention.pdf, figures/full_table.tex).
rows.one_line_fix: Capture OpenRouter's returned `provider`/`id` fields for every call (use output_mode="full" instead of discarding them) and pin exact, dated model slugs — the README already drifted from the code (claims openai/gpt-5.2, code+execution trace show bare openai/gpt-5), so provenance would have caught this before publication.
rows.mistakes: All 4 subject models plus the GPT-4.1 grader are proprietary, effectively lab-single-served on OpenRouter, so the highest-severity multi-provider risks (M1 quantization, M3 probabilistic routing, M8 cross-model comparison confound) are essentially N/A — no `provider` dict is set anywhere (grepped whole repo, zero hits), but that mostly doesn't matter for these models. Two real issues remain that DO threaten reported numbers: (1) M4 — lm.py never records which provider served a call or the OpenRouter generation id; `output_mode="full"` (the only mode that would surface OpenRouter's `provider` field) is defined in lm.py:248-249 but never invoked anywhere in experiment.ipynb (grepped: only "with_usage" and default "response" modes are used), so even the modest residual risk for Claude (which OpenRouter can route via Anthropic-direct, Bedrock, or Vertex) is unverifiable after the fact. (2) M6 — a genuine, verified discrepancy: README.md:31 states the GPT condition queried was `openai/gpt-5.2`, but lm.py:42-45 defines the "gpt-5" key as bare `openai/gpt-5` (no date/version suffix), and this is exactly what the notebook actually calls (experiment.ipynb cell 2: `"gpt": LM("gpt-5")`) — confirmed by the checked-in execution trace in EXAMPLES.md:442 (`choice: 1 gpt-5`, not gpt-5.2). This means the repo's stated "reproduce the paper's experiments" claim is not accurate for the GPT condition as committed: a re-run today queries whatever `openai/gpt-5` currently bare-resolves to, which is not verifiably the same checkpoint that produced the published GPT curve in figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf and figures/full_table.tex, and could drift further if OpenAI repoints the alias. M2 (param dropping) and M9 (judge on unconstrained route) were considered but don't really apply: the grader uses free-text A/B/C output with client-side validation/retry (no response_format/schema requested at all), and the few sampling params passed (temperature=0, max_tokens=16, seed) are basic, universally-supported OpenAI params on an OpenAI-only route. M5 (data_collection left "allow") is technically true (never set) but low real-world impact since SimpleQA questions are public benchmark data, not sensitive.
rows.evidence: lm.py:224-236 (_generate: `payload = {"model": self.api_name, "messages": messages}; payload.update(kwargs)` POSTed to https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions with only an Authorization header — no `provider` block ever constructed, confirmed by whole-repo grep for "provider"/"quantiz"/"require_parameters"/"data_collection" returning zero routing-config hits); lm.py:33-62 (OPENROUTER_MODELS, "gpt-5":{"id":"openai/gpt-5"}); lm.py:248-259 (output_mode="full" would return the raw completion incl. provider metadata but is unused); README.md:29-31 ("Models queried (through OpenRouter API): google/gemini-3-pro-preview, openai/gpt-5.2, x-ai/grok-4, anthropic/claude-opus-4.5"); experiment.ipynb cell 2 (`LMS = {"gemini": LM("gemini-3-pro-preview"), "gpt": LM("gpt-5"), "grok": LM("grok-4"), "opus": LM("opus-4.5")}`); EXAMPLES.md:442 ("================> choice: 1 gpt-5" — actual execution trace, confirming gpt-5 not gpt-5.2 was run); experiment.ipynb cell 3 (SIMPLEQA_GRADER_TEMPLATE ends "Just return the letters 'A', 'B', or 'C'" — free text, no response_format/schema); README.md:86-99 (transparent per-model OpenRouter cost table, ~$2,778 total — shows genuine disclosure of OpenRouter usage, satisfying M10 despite the gpt-5/5.2 inaccuracy).
rows.importance: Official OpenAI-org repo for a flagship Nature 2026 paper by Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang on why accuracy-only grading incentivizes LLM hallucination — high-authority source, high-visibility venue, and the repo is the literal "reproduce our experiments" artifact linked from the paper.
rows.audit_notes: Fittingly ironic for a paper about hallucination: the repo's own README hallucinates a more precise model version than what the code and its own checked-in execution trace (EXAMPLES.md) actually ran — it claims "openai/gpt-5.2" was queried, but lm.py and EXAMPLES.md both show the bare undated "openai/gpt-5" was the model actually invoked. Otherwise this is one of the more disciplined OpenRouter usages seen: single-file wrapper (lm.py), a real on-disk cache keyed on the full call signature (dedup + cost control), an explicit per-model cost table in the README, and a grader that deliberately avoids brittle structured-output constraints in favor of free-text + retry validation.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned github.com/openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments (HEAD e49bb5e) fresh and checked every cited line directly rather than trusting the verdict's transcription.

All evidence checks out verbatim:
- lm.py:224-236 — `payload = {"model": self.api_name, "messages": messages}; payload.update(kwargs)` POSTed to openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions with only an Authorization header. No `provider` key constructed anywhere. Whole-repo grep (run correctly this time, avoiding the `&&`-short-circuit trap where a no-match grep returns exit 1) for "provider"/"quantiz"/"require_parameters"/"data_collection"/"response_format"/"json_schema" returns zero routing-relevant hits — the only "provider" hits are incidental English in retry-logic comments (lm.py:29,90,91,179).
- lm.py:33-62 OPENROUTER_MODELS — "gpt-5":{"id":"openai/gpt-5"} confirmed verbatim; no "gpt-5.2" key exists anywhere in the dict.
- lm.py:248-259 — output_mode="full" branch exists (would surface OpenRouter's `provider` field) but experiment.ipynb only ever passes output_mode="with_usage" (3 occurrences, cells 3/10/12) — "full" is never invoked. This directly confirms M4: no provenance is ever captured.
- README.md:29-31 states "openai/gpt-5.2" verbatim. experiment.ipynb cell 2 verbatim: `"gpt": LM("gpt-5")`. EXAMPLES.md:442 verbatim: "================> choice: 1 gpt-5" — the actual checked-in execution trace confirms bare gpt-5 (via lm.model_name, the LMS dict key) was what ran, not gpt-5.2. This is a genuine, reproducible discrepancy — every other model in the README's list (gemini-3-pro-preview, x-ai/grok-4, anthropic/claude-opus-4.5) matches the code exactly; only gpt-5/gpt-5.2 diverges, so this isn't a generic sloppy-README pattern, it's an isolated, verifiable M6 instance.
- README.md:86-99 cost table confirmed verbatim, substantiating the M10-disclosure exemption.
- Grader template (cell 3) confirmed to end in free-text "Just return the letters \"A\", \"B\", or \"C\"..." with client-side retry/validation, no response_format/json_schema anywhere in the repo — correctly rules out M9. Sampling params for the grader are only max_tokens=16, temperature=0, seed=i or None — basic, universal params — supports the M2 downgrade.
- README's "Caching + reproducibility" section frames on-disk caching (not `seed`) as the reproducibility mechanism, and seeds are deliberately varied (SEED, SEED+i) for statistical sampling rather than treated as a determinism guarantee — correctly rules out M7.
- No non-OpenRouter/direct API calls exist anywhere in the repo — correctly rules out M11.
- figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf, figures/simpleqa_abstention.pdf, figures/full_table.tex all exist as real generated artifacts written directly by the notebook cells that consume the LM/grader outputs — confirms critical_route=true and the pipeline's direct line to the paper's reported figures.

No contradicting evidence found; no missed corruption channel (checked M1/M3/M5/M8/M9/M10/M11/M12 explicitly and all are correctly excluded or downgraded with sound reasoning). The verdict is neither a false accusation nor false reassurance — it correctly identifies two narrow, well-evidenced gaps (M4, M6) in an otherwise disciplined OpenRouter usage, and correctly declines to pile on inapplicable high-severity taxonomy items just because the models happen to be proprietary. I agree with the original verdict in full and made no corrections.</reasoning>

rows.title: Inspect AI (UK AI Safety Institute)
rows.summary: Inspect AI is UKAISI's open-source LLM evaluation harness (the substrate METR, Apollo, inspect_evals, and many published scheming/sandbagging/control papers run rollouts and scoring through). It ships a first-class `OpenRouterAPI(OpenAICompatibleAPI)` provider alongside ~15 direct providers.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable model backends: `inspect eval task.py --model openrouter/org/model` routes chat-completion calls for eval/agent rollouts and (via user-configured judge models) scoring through OpenRouter's OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
safeguards_present: provenance_logging (automatic, via full raw response capture)
quantizations (opt-in via provider dict, documented)
order/only/sort (opt-in via provider dict, mentioned once for caching not reproducibility)
require_parameters and data_collection (mechanism works via free-form dict passthrough but is completely undocumented — zero hits anywhere in repo)
rows.evidence: All original evidence citations verified exact against a fresh clone. ADD: src/inspect_ai/model/_openai.py:352-363 (response_schema -> response_format, called from openai_compatible.py:370-376, inherited unmodified by OpenRouterAPI.completion_params at openrouter.py:334-337) shows structured-output/JSON-schema requests flow to OpenRouter models with zero require_parameters coupling. docs/structured.qmd:114 ('generally required for scorers') and :132-135 ('never assume strict gets your scorer off the hook... some models won't respect strict') confirm this is a documented, encouraged pattern for judges/graders, and that Inspect's own docs recognize the failure symptom without ever naming require_parameters or OpenRouter as the mechanism/fix — this is M9 (judge/grader on unconstrained route), independently confirmed and not in the original mistake_ids list.
rows.mistakes: As shipped, the documented/idiomatic OpenRouter usage path has no default safeguards: (1) M1 no quantizations pin — open-weight model evals (a large fraction of what OpenRouter routes, e.g. Llama/DeepSeek/Mixtral variants used in capability/safety evals) can silently run at int4/int8 on one backend vs bf16 on another, confounding any capability or control-evaluation score computed via Inspect+OpenRouter run-to-run or across papers. (2) M2/M7 no require_parameters — sampling params (temperature, seed, etc.) can be silently dropped by whichever provider OpenRouter load-balances to; the library's own `seed` docstring confirms OpenRouter isn't in the list of providers where seed is honored. (3) M3 no order/only/sort — provider selection is probabilistic by default, so repeated eval runs (or the same eval across two papers using the same model string) may hit materially different backends/quantizations, threatening reproducibility of any capability/safety score. (4) M5 data_collection is never set/documented, so safety-sensitive eval transcripts (e.g. red-team prompts, scheming/sandbagging rollouts) default to whatever OpenRouter account-level data policy is in force, unflagged to the researcher. Because Inspect is described as the execution substrate for rollouts/scoring across a wide swath of the safety-eval ecosystem, any downstream paper that follows the basic README example without additionally learning and setting OpenRouter's `provider={...}` object inherits all four gaps. The one significant mitigant is that Inspect logs the full raw response (including OpenRouter's actual-provider and generation-id fields) into every `.eval` transcript by default, so drift is at least auditable after the fact even though it isn't prevented.
rows.one_line_fix: Add `require_parameters` and `data_collection` to the documented custom-model-arg table and change the OpenRouter quickstart example to `-M "provider={'require_parameters': true, 'quantizations': [...], 'order': [...], 'data_collection': 'deny'}'` instead of a bare unpinned model string.
rows.audit_notes: Fun/telling detail: the library's own `GenerateConfig.seed` docstring lists supported providers as "OpenAI, Google, Mistral, Groq, HuggingFace, and vLLM only" — OpenRouter is conspicuously absent, i.e. the maintainers already know seed-based determinism silently fails on OpenRouter but this fact lives in a docstring, not a warning raised at call time when a user sets `seed` with an `openrouter/*` model. Also notable: the one `provider` dict example in the docs pins `quantizations: ['int8']` — a degraded-quality example a user might copy-paste literally, rather than e.g. `['bf16','fp16']` to pin toward full precision. Verified `openai` SDK's Pydantic `extra='allow'` behavior empirically (not from memory) by `uv pip install`-ing it and inspecting `BaseModel.model_config`, since that fact is load-bearing for the M4 "safe" verdict (it's what makes OpenRouter's `provider`/`id` fields survive into logged transcripts at all).
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai (depth 1) and checked every citation byte-for-code; all of it checks out.

1. src/inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py: __init__ collects `models`/`provider`/`transforms` as unvalidated free-form args at lines 103/109/115 (self.provider must be a dict but its *keys* are never validated); completion_params() at 334-385 only injects extra_body['provider'/'models'/'transforms'] if the user explicitly set them (lines 374-383) — confirms no default provider pinning and confirms require_parameters/data_collection would work if a user manually added them to the provider dict (since it's passed through verbatim, unvalidated).
2. docs/providers.qmd: line 1520 quickstart is a bare `--model openrouter/gryphe/mythomax-l2-13b` with no -M provider dict; line 1528 is the only `provider` example and it pins `quantizations: ['int8']` (a degraded-precision example, exactly as flagged); line 1538 raises routing-variability only as a cache-hit-rate concern, not reproducibility/quantization-drift. grep -rn for 'require_parameters' and 'data_collection' across the whole repo returns zero hits, confirmed independently.
3. src/inspect_ai/model/_generate_config.py lines 124/244: seed docstring verbatim reads 'OpenAI, Google, Mistral, Groq, HuggingFace, and vLLM only' — OpenRouter excluded, confirming M2/M7 is a known, documented gap.
4. src/inspect_ai/model/_providers/openai_compatible.py lines 260-263: `response = completion.model_dump(); model_call.set_response(response, ...)` — full raw response is captured into every logged ModelCall, confirmed. I independently verified (not from memory) via `uv run --with openai` that openai SDK's `BaseModel.model_config == {'extra': 'allow', 'defer_build': True}` and that `ChatCompletion.model_validate({...,'provider':'DeepInfra','id':'gen-abc123',...}).model_dump()` actually preserves the extra `provider`/`id` fields OpenRouter returns — so the M4 'largely handled' mitigation is real and verified, not asserted.

ADDITIONAL FINDING (M9, missed by the original verdict — a real corruption channel, not a false accusation): traced the completion_params() call chain — OpenRouterAPI.completion_params() calls super() (OpenAICompatibleAPI.completion_params, openai_compatible.py:370-376) which calls the shared `openai_completion_params()` in src/inspect_ai/model/_openai.py; lines 352-363 of that shared function set `params['response_format']` whenever `config.response_schema` is set, with zero require_parameters coupling and zero OpenRouter-specific handling. docs/structured.qmd line 114 explicitly states structured output via `response_schema` is 'generally required for scorers' (i.e., judges/graders), and lines 132-135 warn 'You should therefore never assume that specifying `strict` gets your scorer off the hook... some models won't respect `strict`' — Inspect's own docs acknowledge the exact failure mode M9 describes (schema non-compliance) but never once mention `require_parameters` as the fix, and never call out OpenRouter specifically as a route where params get silently dropped. This is a textbook M9 instance: response_format/JSON-schema on an unconstrained OpenRouter route, documented as commonly used for grading, with no require_parameters guidance anywhere. (Inspect's own *built-in* model_graded_qa/model_graded_fact scorers in src/inspect_ai/scorer/_model.py use regex extraction, not response_schema, so the built-in scorer path is unaffected — but the generic, documented, 'required for scorers' response_schema mechanism is exactly what a custom judge/grader task would use, and it is fully exposed on openrouter/* models with no safeguard.)

Sanity checks: model_type='mixed' is correct and well-scoped — canonical_name() (lines 163-192) distinguishes first-party providers (anthropic/openai, kept as full path) from inference-provider-routed open-weight models (together/fireworks prefix stripped), confirming OpenRouter here really does route both proprietary and open-weight models, so M1's quantization-drift concern is correctly scoped to the open-weight subset rather than over-applied to proprietary calls. critical_route=true is justified: generate() (lines 284-300) and the scoring path both go straight through this provider for actual eval/judge outputs, so this is not a debug or logging-only path. awareness='aware_partial' is justified by the seed docstring and the caching-framed routing note. No LEGIT/proprietary-single-served miscall found — OpenRouter here genuinely fronts many interchangeable open-weight backends. Original mistake_ids (M1/M2/M3/M5) and uses_safely=false are all well-supported; I'm only adding M9 as a previously-uncited but independently-verified gap, and upgrading confidence from medium to high given the exactness of every citation and the additional cross-check performed. Severity kept at medium since the added M9 gap is subject to the same mitigants (opt-in fix via the same free-form provider-dict passthrough, and provenance logging still captures the actual served response for post-hoc audit) that justified medium for M1/M2/M3/M5.

rows.title: MathArena (ETH Zurich SRI Lab)
rows.venue: ETH Zurich SRI Lab (Martin Vechev group)
rows.summary: MathArena is a near-live evaluation harness/leaderboard that tests frontier LLMs on newly-released, contamination-free competition math problems (AIME, HMMT, USAMO, Putnam, etc.), publishing a widely-cited public leaderboard.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter (api: openrouter) is one of ~10 API backends the unified APIClient supports; it is used to query a specific subset of models -- Llama-4-Maverick, the Kimi K2 family (k2, k2-thinking, k2.5, k2.6), Phi-4-reasoning-plus:free, the Qwen3/Qwen3.5/Qwen3-VL family, and Grok-4 -- whose transcripts/scores feed directly into the public leaderboard (13 of 140 model configs use api: openrouter). Judges/graders (main_judge, arxiv_judge, simple_judge, norm_judge) all use direct Google/other APIs, never OpenRouter.
safeguards_present: order+allow_fallbacks:False provider pinning (9 of 13 openrouter model configs)

provenance_logging (full raw request/response capture via request_logger, which persists response.model_dump() -- openai-python's pydantic models use extra='allow' so OpenRouter's 'provider' field and generation id are preserved when present)
rows.importance: 271 GitHub stars; run by ETH Zurich's SRI lab (well-known for security/verification ML research); MathArena's near-live tracking of brand-new competition math results is frequently cited in AI-capability discourse as a trustworthy, contamination-resistant benchmark, so its leaderboard numbers get taken at face value by third parties.
rows.evidence: src/matharena/api_client.py:314-319 sets base_url/api_key for api=='openrouter'; api_client.py:1124-1125 routes it through _openai_query_with_tools -> _openai_query_chat_completions_api, whose payload (api_client.py:1732-1745) is `{'model':..., 'messages':..., **self.kwargs}` passed straight to `client.chat.completions.create(**payload)` with no injected require_parameters/quantizations. Provider pinning IS present for 9/13 openrouter configs, e.g. configs/models/moonshot/k26.yaml:14-17 `extra_body: {provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [moonshotai]}}` and configs/models/llama4/maverick.yaml:6-8 `provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [Fireworks]}`. But configs/models/moonshot/k2.yaml, configs/models/qwen/qwen3_235b_a22b.yaml, and configs/models/phi4/14b-reasoning-plus.yaml (model 'microsoft/phi-4-reasoning-plus:free') have `api: openrouter` with zero provider/order/sort key -- confirmed via `grep -rl 'api:\s*openrouter' configs/` cross-referenced against `order:` counts. A repo-wide grep for quantiz|require_parameters|data_collection|sort:|only: across configs/ and src/ returned zero hits. Provenance: src/matharena/request_logger.py:100-129 `log_response` writes `response.model_dump()` to logs/requests/... (gitignored, so this is a local-run artifact, not published, but the mechanism exists for every single call).
rows.one_line_fix: Add the same extra_body: {provider: {order: [<verified-provider>], allow_fallbacks: false}} pattern already used correctly on 9/13 openrouter configs to the 3 unpinned ones (kimi-k2, qwen3-235b-a22b, phi-4-reasoning-plus:free), and add quantizations + require_parameters:true to all 13 openrouter model configs.
rows.audit_notes: Interesting side-finding, outside the strict M1-M12 taxonomy but relevant to whether the existing provider-pinning safeguard actually works: configs/models/llama4/maverick.yaml puts `provider:` at the YAML top level instead of nested under `extra_body:`, and configs/models/qwen/qwen3_235b_a22b_2507.yaml has a bare top-level `top_k: 20` sibling to a correctly-nested extra_body.provider. I installed openai==1.102.0 (the repo's own pinned minimum) in a scratch venv and confirmed empirically that `client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=..., top_k=20)` and `...provider={...}` both raise `TypeError: Completions.create() got an unexpected keyword argument` immediately (no network call), because the SDK's create() has an explicit parameter list with no **kwargs passthrough -- extra params must go through `extra_body`. If this reflects the actual running code (couldn't verify against a lockfile -- none is committed), every call for these two specific model configs would hit APIClient's retry loop (api_client.py:1084-1099) and eventually return an empty conversation rather than silently succeeding -- a loud, self-evident failure (likely already caught/fixed operationally) rather than a silent-corruption risk -- but it does mean the maverick.yaml provider pin, as literally committed, would not actually take effect. Also notable: awareness looks genuinely partial rather than absent -- moonshotai/kimi-k2's later revisions (k2-thinking, k2.5, k2.6) all correctly pin `order: [moonshotai]`, while the original kimi-k2.yaml config was simply never updated to match, and several of the pinned providers (moonshotai, alibaba) are literally the model creators' own OpenRouter-hosted endpoints -- about as close to 'going direct' as OpenRouter allows.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned eth-sri/matharena @ a11194d (current HEAD, matches origin/main) and verified every load-bearing citation.

CONFIRMED EXACTLY AS CITED:
- api_client.py:314-319 (api=='openrouter' -> base_url/api_key) and :1124-1125 (-> _openai_query_with_tools) match verbatim.
- api_client.py:1734-1740 payload = {"model":..., "messages":..., "tools":..., "timeout":..., **kwargs} -> client.chat.completions.create(**payload) at line 1745, no injected require_parameters/quantizations. Confirmed by direct sed of those exact line numbers.
- configs/models/moonshot/k26.yaml:14-17 extra_body.provider{allow_fallbacks:False, order:[moonshotai]} and configs/models/llama4/maverick.yaml:6-8 top-level provider{...} both verbatim as cited.
- Repo-wide grep for require_parameters|quantiz|data_collection|sort:|:floor across configs/+src/ returned zero hits (M1, M2, M5 substantiated; the lone "only:" hit is `cache_read_only: false`, an unrelated false positive).
- Exactly 13/140 yaml configs have `api: openrouter` (grep-verified), matching the "13 of 140" claim exactly. GitHub stars = 271 exactly (gh api).
- request_logger.py log_response persists response dicts to logs/requests/ (gitignored per .gitignore:48), and I independently confirmed via `ChatCompletion.model_config` in openai 1.102.0 and 2.46.0 that `extra='allow'` — so OpenRouter's provider/id fields survive .model_dump(). Provenance-logging safeguard is real.
- Judges: main_judge.yaml, arxiv_judge.yaml, arxiv_judge_post_march.yaml, and the norm_judge/simple_judge scaffolds they use all resolve to model_configs with api: google/openai/anthropic (gemini-31-pro*, opus_46, gpt-54). I additionally checked every ablation judge (grok_judge, glm_judge, step_judge -> xai/grok-41-fast-reasoning) and confirmed api: xai, never openrouter. Zero hits for response_format/json_schema anywhere in the repo. M9 is correctly omitted.

ONE GENUINE EVIDENCE GAP FOUND (doesn't change the verdict): the evidence's "3 unpinned" list (k2.yaml, qwen3_235b_a22b.yaml, phi4/14b-reasoning-plus.yaml) undercounts by one — configs/models/xai/grok-4-new.yaml (x-ai/grok-4) also has zero provider/extra_body key, making it a 4th technically-unpinned openrouter config (9+4=13, so the "9/13 pinned" headline number is still internally consistent only if grok-4 is excluded from consideration). However I verified live via OpenRouter's public API (`/api/v1/models/x-ai/grok-4.5/endpoints`) that current xAI Grok models have exactly ONE endpoint (provider_name "xAI", no third-party rehosts) — consistent with the taxonomy's own LEGIT carve-out ("proprietary single-served models: M1/M3/M8 N/A"). So this is a real omission in the evidence's enumeration but not a wrong verdict — grok-4 is exempt from the same concern for a principled reason the auditor implicitly applied but didn't state.

I independently re-verified the audit_notes' side-finding empirically (not just trusting it): installed openai==1.102.0 AND the latest 2.46.0 in a clean venv and confirmed `client.chat.completions.create(model=..., messages=..., provider={...})` and `..., top_k=20` both raise `TypeError: Completions.create() got an unexpected keyword argument` before any network call, while `extra_body={"provider":...}` correctly reaches the HTTP layer (APIConnectionError, i.e., past parameter validation). This confirms maverick.yaml's top-level `provider:` and qwen3_235b_a22b_2507.yaml's top-level `top_k:` (also verified present exactly as described) are non-functional as literally committed — every such call would hit the generic-exception retry loop (api_client.py:1747-1762, 60s sleep x up to max_retries_inner) and eventually raise ValueError. This is a loud failure, correctly kept outside the M1-M12 taxonomy in audit_notes rather than promoted to a new mistake_id.

Additional strengthening evidence I found unprompted: live-queried OpenRouter's endpoints API for moonshotai/kimi-k2.6 (one of the "pinned" configs) and found 20 active third-party endpoints with quantizations ranging int4/fp4/fp8/unknown — and critically, the pinned provider itself ("Moonshot AI") is currently serving at int4. This shows provider-pinning (order:[moonshotai]) alone does NOT guarantee full-precision serving — it only fixes which company serves it, not at what quantization — which directly validates M1 being flagged as still-live even on the 9 "pinned" configs, and validates the one_line_fix's recommendation to add explicit `quantizations` alongside provider pinning.

All mistake_ids check out: M1 (confirmed unpinned quantization risk, empirically strengthened), M2 (zero require_parameters usage, sampling params like temperature/top_p present in configs), M3 (multiple unpinned configs feeding the public leaderboard), M5 (zero data_collection policy hits), M8 (mixed pinned/unpinned OpenRouter models compared against always-direct-API proprietary models on one leaderboard). M9 correctly absent (judges verified fully isolated from OpenRouter). severity=medium and confidence=high are both defensible given majority-pinned safeguards + universal provenance logging as mitigating factors alongside genuine, live-confirmed quantization drift risk on the unpinned/broken configs.

rows.title: OSWorld (NeurIPS 2024 D&B) — mm_agents/{maestro,os_symphony,vlaa_gui} OpenRouter engine
rows.summary: OSWorld is the 369-task real-computer-use benchmark from the NeurIPS 2024 Datasets & Benchmarks paper. Its repo also hosts a growing library of third-party agent baselines under mm_agents/ (added well after the original paper); several of these (maestro, os_symphony, vlaa_gui) implement an OpenRouter engine as one of several pluggable LMM backends.
rows.openrouter_use: Agent rollout / action-generation backend, not just a judge: LMMEngineOpenRouter's output is parsed into the next pyautogui/tool action during live OSWorld task execution. In maestro's shipped default config (mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json), 10 of the agent's core cognitive roles (planner_role, supplement_role, dag_translator, operator_role, technician_role, analyst_role, worker_success_role, worker_stale_role, periodic_role, final_check_role, objective_alignment) default to "provider": "openrouter", "model_name": "openai/o3" — confirmed as the actual out-of-the-box config via the hardcoded load path in mm_agents/maestro/maestro/controller/config_manager.py:30-34.
rows.importance: 3,019 GitHub stars; NeurIPS 2024 D&B paper; the standard real-computer-use benchmark for GUI/computer-use agents. Note: the OpenRouter-touching code is NOT part of the original paper's headline numbers — maestro/os_symphony/vlaa_gui are later community-contributed agent baselines (they reference gpt-5, GLM-4.5, MiniMax-M3, Kimi-K2 — models that didn't exist in April 2024) bundled into the same widely-used repo as pluggable agents researchers can run against OSWorld and report scores for.
rows.evidence: Minor correction only: the config actually sets 11 roles (not 10) to `"provider": "openrouter", "model_name": "openai/o3"` in mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json — verified via `grep -c '"provider": "openrouter"'` = 11, matching all 11 role names already listed in the verdict's own parenthetical. This strengthens rather than weakens the finding. All other evidence lines re-verified verbatim against a fresh clone of xlang-ai/OSWorld, including an independent confirmation (via openrouter.ai/openai/o3) that "openai/o3" is genuinely single-provider on OpenRouter, which substantiates the verdict's M1/M3/M8-N/A reasoning rather than merely asserting it.
rows.mistakes: The maestro agent's shipped default config routes most of its decision-making roles through OpenRouter via the bare, undated slug "openai/o3" (M6) with data_collection left at OpenRouter's default "allow" (M5) despite full desktop screenshots being sent as images with every call, and the OpenRouter engine wrapper (duplicated near-verbatim in os_symphony and vlaa_gui) discards the API response after pulling out `.choices[0].message.content` and token usage, so the actually-served provider / generation id is never recorded anywhere (M4). Because this is the default action-generation policy for the maestro agent, any OSWorld benchmark score reported for maestro (or the analogous os_symphony/vlaa_gui agents) run with the shipped OpenRouter config cannot be tied to a specific backend snapshot, and could silently drift if OpenAI updates what "o3" resolves to behind that alias. Corrupting risks that would matter most for open-weight models — M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic provider load-balancing, M8 cross-provider confound — are not seriously in play here because every model actually routed through OpenRouter in this codebase (openai/*, x-ai/*, anthropic/*, google/*) is proprietary and effectively single-provider, so this does not corrupt the benchmark's task-success measurements the way it would for an open-weight policy model; the remaining risk is reproducibility/audit-trail and data-governance, not silently-wrong scores.
rows.one_line_fix: In LMMEngineOpenRouter.generate() (engine.py in maestro/os_symphony/vlaa_gui), add extra_body={"provider": {"data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}} and persist response.provider/response.id alongside each generation; pin "openai/o3" to a dated OpenRouter snapshot if one is exposed.
rows.audit_notes: Nice example of a "benchmark repo" whose paper-time code (NeurIPS 2024, April 2024 submission) never touched OpenRouter at all, but whose repo has since become a living hub of third-party agent baselines (maestro, os_symphony, vlaa_gui, gta1, jedi, uitars, opencua, etc.) — several of which independently reinvented the same "pluggable LMM engine" pattern with an OpenRouter option, and all three that use it made the identical omission (extract .choices[0].message.content, throw away the rest of the response). Worth flagging to whoever maintains "maestro" specifically if they publish a standalone paper/leaderboard entry for it, since that's the actual critical-route consumer, not the OSWorld NeurIPS paper itself.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned xlang-ai/OSWorld (depth 1, current HEAD) and checked every cited location; all evidence checks out verbatim.

1. mm_agents/maestro/core/engine.py:601-642 — LMMEngineOpenRouter class confirmed exactly as described: `self.llm_client.chat.completions.create(model=self.model, messages=messages, max_completion_tokens=..., temperature=temperature, **kwargs)` with no `extra_body`/`provider` key anywhere. Lines 639-640 confirmed verbatim: `content = response.choices[0].message.content; total_tokens, cost = calculate_tokens_and_cost(response, self.provider, self.model)`. Traced `calculate_tokens_and_cost`/`extract_token_usage` (lines 109-150) — they only pull `response.usage.{prompt,completion}_tokens`; `response.provider` and `response.id` are never touched anywhere in the file. `self.provider` is confirmed to be the static string `"llm-openrouter"` (line 607), used purely as a cost-bookkeeping key, not the served-provider from the API. M4 fully substantiated.

2. mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json — confirmed 11 (not 10 — minor undercount in the verdict text, which lists all 11 role names but says "10 of... roles"; this is a harmless miscount that if anything understates the finding) roles set to `"provider": "openrouter", "model_name": "openai/o3"`: planner_role, supplement_role, dag_translator, operator_role, technician_role, analyst_role, worker_success_role, worker_stale_role, periodic_role, final_check_role, objective_alignment. Bare undated slug confirmed — M6 substantiated.

3. mm_agents/maestro/maestro/controller/config_manager.py:28-38 — `load_tools_configuration()` hardcodes the path to `tools/new_tools_config.json` with no override mechanism found elsewhere in the repo (only one tools-config JSON file exists in the whole maestro tree). Confirms this is genuinely the out-of-the-box default, not one option among many.

4. mm_agents/maestro/core/model.md:175-209 and model_pricing.json:113-129 — confirmed the OpenRouter section lists only openai/*, x-ai/*, anthropic/*, google/* — no open-weight model anywhere under the openrouter provider. This directly supports the M1/M3/M8-N/A reasoning.

5. Verified the single-provider claim independently: fetched https://openrouter.ai/openai/o3 directly, which states "This model is hosted by one provider. OpenRouter forwards every request to it directly — no routing decisions to make." This confirms M3 (probabilistic multi-provider routing) is genuinely inapplicable for this specific route, not just assumed — a nontrivial technical claim that holds up.

6. mm_agents/maestro/core/mllm.py:238-300 — `LMMEngineOpenRouter` confirmed present in the `isinstance(self.engine, (...))` tuple that attaches base64 `image_url` content to messages (lines 240-298), so screenshots genuinely flow through this engine with no `data_collection: deny` set anywhere. M5 substantiated.

7. Duplicate pattern in os_symphony (engine.py:231-276) and vlaa_gui (engine.py:1637-1682) confirmed byte-for-byte consistent with the verdict's description — no `extra_body`, response reduced to `.choices[0].message.content` (or +usage) before being returned, provider/generation-id discarded.

8. Repo-wide `grep -rIn "quantizations|require_parameters|data_collection" .` returned zero matches, confirmed.

9. Critical-route claim verified: mm_agents/maestro/maestro/sub_worker/operator.py shows `operator_role`'s LLM output is parsed (`_extract_candidate_action`) directly into the next grounded UI action for hardware execution — this is genuine agent-rollout action generation, not a judge/grader, confirming `critical_route: true`. osworld_run_maestro.py confirms `action_space=pyautogui` as the OSWorld task-execution entrypoint.

10. Checked for missed mistake types: no `response_format`/`json_schema` API params found anywhere in the three engines (M9 correctly not flagged — action parsing is regex/text-based, not constrained decoding); no `seed=` usage anywhere (M7 correctly not flagged); only `temperature`/`max_new_tokens` passed as sampling kwargs, nothing exotic enough to trigger a real M2 concern.

No false accusation and no false reassurance found. The severity="medium" (rather than defaulting to M4's taxonomy-listed "High") is a defensible, well-reasoned downgrade given the independently-confirmed single-provider/proprietary-only nature of every model actually routed through OpenRouter in this codebase — it correctly keeps uses_safely=false while acknowledging the residual risk is audit-trail/reproducibility/data-governance rather than silently-corrupted benchmark scores. I agree with the verdict as given, correcting nothing substantive (the 10-vs-11 role count is the only inaccuracy found and it doesn't affect the verdict).

rows.title: OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin)
rows.summary: OpenHands is a widely-used (81k+ star) open-source platform for CodeAct-style generalist coding/browsing agents, evaluated across SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, and 15+ other benchmarks; its actual LLM-calling code now lives in a companion SDK repo, OpenHands/software-agent-sdk.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable "LLM Provider" backends an OpenHands user can select in Settings to run the agent's rollouts (coding tasks, SWE-bench-style, web browsing), implemented as a generic LiteLLM pass-through in openhands-sdk/openhands/sdk/llm/llm.py. Separately, OpenHands' own legacy AlgoTune benchmark adapter used OpenRouter directly to compare several open-weight models on a leaderboard.
rows.importance: 81,138 GitHub stars; one of the most widely deployed open-source coding-agent platforms; backbone for numerous downstream research forks/evals (per discovery evidence: SkyRL-OpenHands, ReaComp, DRPBench, rdi-berkeley/agents-last-exam) that wire their reported numbers through this exact OpenRouter config path.
rows.one_line_fix: Ship a safe default `provider` block (require_parameters:true, data_collection:"deny", pinned quantizations, order/sort set) whenever the OpenRouter backend is selected, and default `log_completions` on (or otherwise persist the served-provider/generation-id) so the actually-served weights are pinned and recorded instead of left to each downstream user/fork to configure themselves.
rows.mistakes: OpenHands ships zero default OpenRouter safeguards anywhere in its LLM class: no quantizations pin (M1), sampling params (temperature/top_p/seed/reasoning_effort) sent with no require_parameters (M2), no order/only/sort so routing stays load-balanced (M3), data_collection is never set so it defaults to "allow" (M5). The only lever is `litellm_extra_body`, an empty dict by default that a user must populate themselves with a `provider` block to get any of the above — nothing in the product docs (openrouter.mdx) shows this or warns about reproducibility, and the documented example model string is a bare undated slug (`openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet`, M6). Provenance logging (`log_completions`, which would capture the raw served response) defaults to False (M4) — so served-provider/generation-id is not recorded unless a user explicitly opts in. Concretely, legacy/algotune/adapter/utils.py:36-39 shows OpenHands itself comparing multiple open-weight models (GLM-4.5, Qwen3-Coder, GPT-OSS-120b) served over unpinned OpenRouter routes on a leaderboard with no visible provider constraint (M8). Docs give zero routing/reproducibility guidance (M10). Mitigating factor: OpenHands' own currently-maintained, canonical eval harness (github.com/OpenHands/benchmarks/.llm_config/example.json) routes its flagship SWE-bench-style numbers through a self-hosted `litellm_proxy/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514` proxy straight to Anthropic, NOT OpenRouter — so OpenHands' own headline paper/leaderboard claims are not directly corrupted. The risk this taxonomy flags falls mainly on (a) the historical AlgoTune leaderboard numbers, and (b) the many downstream research repos that reuse this exact zero-default OpenRouter integration for their own reported results.
rows.evidence: Everything in the original "evidence" field checks out verbatim against a fresh clone (SDK HEAD 4fe56566/v1.36.1, matching the v1.36.0 pinned by OpenHands/OpenHands's pyproject.toml). One correction: benchmarks/legacy/algotune/adapter/utils.py:36-38 (confirmed exact) is a MODEL_DISPLAY_NAMES lookup table used by AlgoTuneData.get_reference_solver() to read reports/agent_summary.json from a *cloned external AlgoTune repository* (self.repo_root / 'reports' / 'agent_summary.json') for reference-solver selection — grep across the whole benchmarks repo shows "openrouter" appears nowhere else, and no completion()/litellm call site exists in this adapter file. So this evidence shows OpenHands consuming (possibly externally-generated) leaderboard data naming OpenRouter-served open-weight models, not a confirmed live OpenHands-initiated unpinned-OpenRouter completion call. Treat the M8 AlgoTune citation as suggestive/plausible rather than dispositive; the primary load-bearing evidence for the overall unsafe verdict is the SDK-level structural finding (litellm_extra_body empty-by-default, zero require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection hits repo-wide, log_completions default False), which is solid and independent of the AlgoTune example.
rows.audit_notes: Interesting structural point: the main OpenHands/OpenHands repo no longer contains the LLM-calling code at all (it pins `openhands-sdk==1.36.0` etc. as a dependency) — the real audit target is the companion repo github.com/OpenHands/software-agent-sdk. Also notable: `gh search code` fails to find OpenRouter call sites in the main repo (only frontend UI strings and a provider-name mapper), which would mislead a shallow audit into concluding "no OpenRouter usage" — you have to follow the dependency to the SDK repo to find the actual completion() call path.
