The one clear internal precedent of the failure mode (legacy/algotune multi-model open-weight leaderboard via unpinned OpenRouter) sits in a directory literally named `legacy/`, suggesting OpenHands' team may have already moved away from that pattern, though I found no explicit deprecation note confirming it's unused.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned both github.com/OpenHands/software-agent-sdk (HEAD 4fe56566, v1.36.1, dated 2026-07-17 — one patch above the v1.36.0 that OpenHands/OpenHands's pyproject.toml pins, so the audited code is current) and github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands, plus the docs and benchmarks repos, and checked every cited file.

Confirmed exactly as claimed:
- llm.py: `litellm_extra_body: dict[str, Any] = Field(default_factory=dict)` at line 507 (evidence said 507-523 — matches). `log_completions: bool = Field(default=False)` at line 421 (evidence said 421-424 — matches).
- chat_options.py:104 and responses_options.py:91: both contain the identical guarded line `if llm.litellm_extra_body: out["extra_body"] = llm.litellm_extra_body` — confirming the *only* channel for an OpenRouter `provider:{...}` block is this empty-by-default dict, with no default population anywhere.
- `grep -rn "require_parameters\|quantizations\|data_collection" sdk/` returns **zero** hits repo-wide — confirms the "gh search code ... zero hits" claim.
- telemetry.py `log_llm_call` (lines 288-381 match) only fires when `self.log_dir` or a callback is set; `log_dir` is only non-None `if self.log_completions` (confirmed at llm.py:632/757/829) — so M4 (no provenance logging by default) is solid.
- llm.py:302-309 `openrouter_site_url`/`openrouter_app_name` → HTTP-Referer/X-Title headers confirmed as attribution-only, not reproducibility-related.
- docs/openhands/usage/llms/openrouter.mdx: fetched verbatim — example is literally `openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet` (bare undated slug, M6) with zero mention of provider pinning, routing, or reproducibility (M10).
- benchmarks/.llm_config/example.json: fetched verbatim — `{"model": "litellm_proxy/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514", "base_url": "https://llm-proxy.eval.all-hands.dev", ...}` confirms OpenHands' own canonical eval config bypasses OpenRouter entirely (the stated mitigating factor is real).
- main repo: `grep -rln openrouter main/ --include=*.py` returns nothing; only frontend/*.ts(x)/i18n strings match — confirms the "you have to follow the SDK dependency" methodological point, and pyproject.toml pins `openhands-sdk==1.36.0` exactly as stated.
- Star count 81,138 matches `gh api repos/OpenHands/OpenHands` live today, and the downstream-fork repo names cited (NovaSky-AI/SkyRL-OpenHands, rdi-berkeley/agents-last-exam) do exist.
- Checked for a missed M9: no `response_format`/JSON-schema constrained-output call site exists anywhere in the SDK's LLM path (only unrelated Pydantic `model_json_schema()` hits) — M9 correctly omitted. Also confirmed no `:floor`/price-sort usage (M12 correctly omitted) and no proprietary-single-provider mischaracterization (model_type "mixed" is right, since OpenRouter here explicitly serves open-weight models like GLM-4.5/Qwen3-Coder/GPT-OSS-120b, so M1/M3/M8 are NOT N/A per the LEGIT carve-out).

One real weakness found: the M8 evidence citation (benchmarks/legacy/algotune/adapter/utils.py:36-38, confirmed byte-for-byte, including the `openrouter/z-ai/glm-4.5` etc. keys) is a model-name **display normalization dict** consumed by `AlgoTuneData.get_reference_solver()`, which reads `reports/agent_summary.json` from a **cloned external AlgoTune repository** to pick a seed solver — it is not direct proof that OpenHands itself issued live, unpinned OpenRouter completions to build this leaderboard (it may be AlgoTune upstream's own externally-generated data). The verdict's prose ("OpenHands itself comparing... served over unpinned OpenRouter routes on a leaderboard") somewhat overstates what this specific code snippet proves. That said, M8 remains defensible as a finding: the SDK provides zero structural guardrail against exactly this failure mode for anyone (OpenHands itself in this adapter, or any downstream fork) who does compare multiple models through this OpenRouter integration, and this is the only OpenRouter reference in the entire benchmarks repo, so it's still meaningful (if imprecise) supporting evidence.

Net: the safe/unsafe verdict and all eight M-ids are justified by code I independently re-fetched and verified almost line-for-line. severity=medium and confidence=medium are appropriately calibrated given the one soft evidentiary point on M8 and the documented mitigating factor (OpenHands' own headline SWE-bench numbers route through a direct Anthropic proxy, not OpenRouter) — I do not find grounds to escalate to high or downgrade to low/false-positive.

rows.title: Prometheus 2 / BiGGen-Bench (KAIST AI)
rows.summary: BiGGen-Bench evaluates 103 LLMs (28 base + 61 chat, run locally via vllm, plus 14 proprietary API models) across 9 capabilities using 5 evaluator LMs (Prometheus-2 variants + Claude-3-Opus + GPT-4-1106 + GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09); prometheus-eval is the companion open-source judge-LM library.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter (via litellm's `acompletion`/`completion`, wrapped in prometheus_eval/litellm.py's LiteLLM/AsyncLiteLLM classes) is used by BiGGen-Bench/run_api_inference.py to generate candidate responses from API-only ("proprietary") LLMs for the benchmark — these responses are then scored by the 5 evaluator LMs and feed directly into the paper's Table 1, Figures 4-5 (performance-vs-size trends), and Table 4 (the paper's headline "gap between open-source and proprietary LMs" analysis). The same wrapper is also written to support calling an external LLM (e.g. GPT-4) as judge in run_response_eval.py, though that specific call is currently commented out in favor of a MockLLM stub in the public repo.
rows.importance: 1102 GitHub stars; NAACL 2025 + EMNLP 2024 Findings; Prometheus 1 has 300+ citations and Prometheus 2/BiGGen-Bench are widely used to power a public HF leaderboard (prometheus-eval/BiGGen-Bench-Leaderboard) and BiGGen-Bench-Results dataset, so this repo's model-serving hygiene affects a benchmark other papers cite as ground truth.
rows.evidence: All originally-cited evidence independently reproduced verbatim: prometheus_eval/litellm.py (134 lines, sync completion() lines 41-43, async acompletion() lines 75-80, kwargs-drop bug in _get_completion_text_async confirmed), BiGGen-Bench/run_api_inference.py (AsyncLiteLLM instantiation lines 36-38, params dict lines 53-59, response_model_name=model_name line 70), BiGGen-Bench/README.md:73 OpenRouter mention (exact quote), BiGGen-Bench/run_response_eval.py:124-125 (MockLLM active / AsyncLiteLLM commented out), repo-wide zero hits for require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection/extra_body/provider dict, and the arXiv 2406.05761 Limitations section (independently extracted via pdftotext) confirmed to discuss stochasticity/verbosity/self-enhancement bias at length while never mentioning API-provider routing, quantization, or inference-stack reproducibility.
rows.mistakes: M4 (no provenance logging): confirmed — run_api_inference.py:70 only records the CLI `model_name` string as `response_model_name`; the actual served provider/backend/generation id for the OpenRouter-routed "proprietary LM" responses is never captured anywhere, so if OpenRouter ever silently switched backend for one of these models (e.g., OpenAI-direct vs Azure-hosted GPT-4), it would be undetectable and unreproducible. This threatens reproducibility of the candidate responses feeding Table 1/Figs 4-5/Table 4. M5 (data-policy leakage): confirmed — `data_collection` is never set to "deny" anywhere in the codebase, so prompts (including Safety-capability tasks about harm/secrets/moral dilemmas) default to OpenRouter's permissive data-collection routing. Doesn't corrupt results but is an unaddressed privacy/policy gap for a paper that explicitly discusses "Potential Risks" of its safety content without covering this angle. M6 (model version drift): the library's own documented usage example (libs/prometheus-eval/README.md:70) calls an undated `gpt-4-turbo` alias, and `run_api_inference.py --model_name` accepts any free-form string with no enforcement of dated slugs — this threatens exact reproducibility of any candidate-model responses generated this way, since OpenAI/other providers periodically repoint undated aliases to new checkpoints. M10 (no reporting): the BiGGen-Bench paper's Limitations section covers several reproducibility threats (stochastic generation, verbosity bias, self-enhancement bias) at length but is silent on the API-routing/inference-stack risk for the subset of the 103 evaluated models obtained via OpenRouter, despite this feeding the paper's own headline "gap between open-source and proprietary LMs" comparison (Table 4).
rows.one_line_fix: In prometheus_eval/litellm.py, forward `**kwargs` into the `acompletion()`/`completion()` calls and set `extra_body={"provider": {"data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}}` plus store `response.get("provider")` and `response.id` alongside each output in run_api_inference.py's result dict; also require dated model slugs for any `--model_name` passed to run_api_inference.py.
rows.audit_notes: Notable independent bug (not an M-id per se, but load-bearing for reproducibility): AsyncLiteLLM._get_completion_text_async (litellm.py:71-86) accepts **kwargs but never passes them into acompletion() — so the temperature=1.0/top_p=0.9/max_tokens=2048 params configured in run_api_inference.py:53-59 never actually reach the API for any model called via this async path (which is the only path BiGGen-Bench's API-model generation script uses). This means whatever sampling config the authors believed they were controlling for API-called models was silently discarded before even reaching litellm/OpenRouter — a self-inflicted, router-independent reproducibility gap that happens to make classic M2 (provider-side param dropping) moot only because nothing was sent to begin with. Also of note: run_response_eval.py:124-126 shows the LLM-judge path for non-Prometheus evaluators is currently a commented-out `AsyncLiteLLM(eval_model_name, ...)` call replaced by `MockLLM(mode="absolute")` in the public repo — so the public code as checked out does not actually exercise the OpenRouter-as-judge path, though the paper's real evaluator LMs (Claude-3-Opus, GPT-4-1106, GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09) were presumably called via some real backend for the published numbers. Clone used: `git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/prometheus-eval/prometheus-eval.git` (single commit dcfb442, Apr 24 2025).
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-fetched every file cited in evidence via `git clone --depth 1` of prometheus-eval/prometheus-eval (commit dcfb442) and re-read the paper PDF text (arXiv 2406.05761) directly. All citations check out verbatim:

1. libs/prometheus-eval/prometheus_eval/litellm.py — exact match. Sync `completion(model=self.name, api_base=self.api_base, messages=message, **kwargs)` at lines 41-43; async `acompletion(model=self.name, api_base=self.api_base, messages=message, request_timeout=60)` at lines 75-80. Confirmed no `extra_body`/`provider` dict anywhere. Confirmed the independent bug: `_get_completion_text_async(self, message, **kwargs)` (line 71) accepts kwargs but never passes them into the `acompletion()` call — genuinely dropped, not forwarded. Also confirmed `_test()` uses `LiteLLM("openrouter/openai/gpt-3.5-turbo")`, substantiating actual OpenRouter usage via litellm's routing convention.

2. BiGGen-Bench/run_api_inference.py — exact match on all cited lines: `model = AsyncLiteLLM(model_name, ...)` (36-38), `params = {temperature: 1.0, top_p: 0.9, ...}` passed to `model.completions(inputs, **params)` (53-61), and `result[uid]["response_model_name"] = model_name` (line 70) storing only the raw CLI string — confirmed M4 (no provider/generation-id provenance).

3. Repo-wide grep for `require_parameters|quantizations|data_collection|extra_body` and `provider\s*[:=]` returned zero hits — confirmed M5 (data_collection never set) and supports M1/M2 non-applicability claims.

4. BiGGen-Bench/README.md:73 — exact quote match: "With the help of litellm, you can use various APIs from different providers. We mainly used OpenAI API and OpenRouter." libs/prometheus-eval/README.md documented example uses undated `gpt-4-turbo` alias — confirmed M6, though this evidence is intentionally weaker/indirect (a README example + unenforced free-form CLI arg, not proof any specific published result used an undated alias — appropriately hedged as Med severity).

5. BiGGen-Bench/run_response_eval.py:124-125 — exact match: `model = MockLLM(mode="absolute")` active, `# model = AsyncLiteLLM(eval_model_name, ...)` commented out. Confirmed the LLM-judge path is text-parsed (`[RESULT]` regex, ABSOLUTE_PROMPT), not response_format/JSON-schema-based, so M9 correctly does NOT apply, and correctly noted as currently inactive in the public repo.

6. Paper Limitations section (pdftotext extraction of arXiv 2406.05761, full text): confirmed verbatim — discusses generation stochasticity, unified hyperparameter setting, self-enhancement bias mitigated via 5 evaluator LMs, offline-vs-online benchmark limits — and contains zero mention of API-provider routing, quantization, or inference-stack reproducibility. Confirms M10 precisely.

7. Confirmed paper text: "103 frontier LMs ... as well as 14 proprietary LMs" (matches summary), Table 4 titled "Gap between proprietary and open-source" (matches the claimed headline analysis), and the 5 evaluator LMs (Claude-3-Opus, GPT-4-1106, GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 plus two Prometheus-2 variants) appear exactly as described.

Sanity checks per the taxonomy: the LEGIT carve-out ("proprietary single-served models: M1/M3/M8 N/A") is correctly applied — the README's explicit local-vllm/API script split makes it plausible the OpenRouter-routed models are genuinely closed proprietary models (single-upstream on OpenRouter), so M1/M3/M8 exclusion is defensible, not a false-reassurance gap, and the auditor correctly flagged this as an assumption they couldn't fully confirm rather than asserting it outright. I considered whether M11 (silent backend mixing) should be added given the README says "we mainly used OpenAI API and OpenRouter" (implying a mix), but found no config/script evidence that the *same* nominal model was called through both backends across runs — adding it would be speculative, so I agree with its exclusion. No judge/grader route uses response_format/JSON schema (M9 correctly absent). uses_safely=false is correct since LEGIT requires provider pinning AND provenance logging, and neither holds here (M4 confirms no provenance; no provider pinning found anywhere). severity=medium is a defensible calibration: despite M4 being taxonomy-High, the paper's own admission that sampling params never even reach the API (independent kwargs bug) undercuts the case for concrete result corruption, leaving this mainly a provenance/documentation-hygiene finding rather than a proven silent-corruption channel — consistent with 'medium' rather than 'high'. I upgraded confidence from medium to high because, unlike typical audits where some cited evidence turns out paraphrased or slightly off, every single citation here (file paths, line ranges, exact quotes, and the paper's Limitations section content) reproduced exactly on independent re-fetch, with no discrepancies found.

rows.audit_notes: Notable: openbench's own README ships a "Reproducibility Statement" (README.md:225-233) that explicitly names "different model quantization or inference approaches" as a disclaimed source of score variance between openbench runs and other sources — genuine self-awareness of the exact failure mode M1 describes — yet the tool does not act on that awareness by defaulting to safe OpenRouter routing; it stays fully opt-in via `-M` flags. Also notable: docs/configuration.mdx literally labels `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` as "Random seed for deterministic generation" (textbook M7 claim), and this seed is piped straight into GenerateConfig (eval_command.py:830) and onward to the OpenRouter chat-completion call with no require_parameters guard, so on routes that don't honor `seed` it is silently dropped, not just non-deterministic. Underlying Inspect AI (openai_compatible.py:260-263, UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai) does call `completion.model_dump()` and store the full raw JSON response in the per-sample transcript, which would capture OpenRouter's `provider` field if present — a genuine, if incidental, provenance-logging capability that openbench itself never surfaces as a first-class feature (no results.json/leaderboard export was found that extracts served-provider info).
rows.evidence: src/openbench/model/_providers/openrouter.py:57-115 (fetched via `gh api repos/groq/openbench/contents/...`) — OpenRouterAPI.__init__ accepts only/order/ignore/sort/max_price/quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection as OPTIONAL kwargs; `provider_params: Dict[str, Any] = {}` starts empty and `self._extra_body["provider"] = provider_params` is only set `if provider_params:` (i.e. only if the caller passed at least one -M flag). Default `bench eval X --model openrouter/...` sends NO `provider` object at all → full OpenRouter load-balancing, unpinned quantization, `require_parameters` unset, `data_collection` left at OpenRouter's server-side default ("allow"). docs/providers.mdx:120-136 documents all these knobs as opt-in `-M` args with no safe default recommended. Confirmed with static scanner (skill/use-openrouter-safely/scripts/audit_openrouter.py) against the cloned repo: 37 High / 35 Med findings across 18 router-touching files, all M1/M3/M4/M5 on the default path. Per-eval docstrings (e.g. src/openbench/evals/race.py:15, pubmedqa.py:9, headqa.py:13-14, medqa.py:9, qa4mre.py:19-21, blimp.py:18-20, xcopa.py:138, ethics.py:157) show `-M only=groq` (mitigates M3 only) but never quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection. Critically, the repo's own flagship reasoning/math evals — src/openbench/evals/gpqa_diamond.py, evals/math.py, datasets/mathvista.py, evals/matharena/aime_*/*.py, evals/gpt_oss/aime.py — contain ZERO OpenRouter example usage or routing guidance (grep for "bench eval\|openrouter" returned nothing in all of them). gpqa_diamond.py:45 sets `config=GenerateConfig(temperature=0.5)` (a sampling param subject to M2 silent-drop with no require_parameters guard). README.md:225-233 "Reproducibility Statement" explicitly names quantization drift as a known confound but doesn't enforce pinning. docs/configuration.mdx `--seed`/BENCH_SEED documented as "deterministic generation" (M7), wired through eval_command.py:830.
rows.importance: Groq-authored official org repo, 784 GitHub stars, positioned as general-purpose provider-agnostic eval infrastructure (built on UK AISI's Inspect AI) covering 95+ benchmarks including GPQA Diamond, multiple years of AIME, MATH, MathVista, and a MathArena mirror. Used by third parties to generate benchmark numbers they may report/cite.
rows.mistakes: The repo is best understood as infrastructure whose safety depends entirely on how a downstream user invokes it — but its shipped defaults and its own documented examples for the highest-stakes evals never demonstrate a safe invocation, threatening any benchmark score (GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU, etc.) a user reports after running `bench eval <task> --model openrouter/<open-weight-model>` without manually adding `-M` flags. M1 (unpinned quantization): default `provider` payload omits `quantizations`, so OpenRouter can silently serve int4/int8 weights, directly threatening any accuracy number from a reasoning/math eval, which is exactly the class of benchmark most sensitive to precision loss. M2 (silent parameter dropping): evals like gpqa_diamond.py set `temperature=0.5` and the CLI exposes `--seed`, but `require_parameters` is never set by default, so a provider that doesn't honor these params drops them without error, silently changing the sampling distribution behind a reported score. M3 (probabilistic provider routing): the base OpenRouterAPI ships no `order`/`only`/`sort` by default, so repeated runs of the same eval load-balance across different backend providers/engines — this is only mitigated in ~10 non-critical eval docstrings via `-M only=groq`, and never demonstrated for GPQA/AIME/MATH/MathArena. M5 (data-policy leakage): `data_collection` defaults to OpenRouter's server-side "allow", meaning eval prompts (which may include held-out benchmark items) can be logged/trained on by third-party providers unless a user manually passes `-M data_collection=deny`. M7 (seed→determinism): `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` is documented as producing "deterministic generation" with no caveat that provider routing/backend choice breaks that guarantee, and the same param is subject to M2's silent-drop risk. Mitigating factor working against a harsher verdict: unlike most repos, ALL the necessary safeguards (quantizations, require_parameters, order/only/sort, data_collection) are fully implemented and exposed as first-class configurable `-M` flags — this is well above the median repo's OpenRouter hygiene — and the README's "Reproducibility Statement" shows the maintainers are aware quantization variance is a real issue, even though they don't act on that awareness by defaulting to safe settings or by logging provenance.
rows.one_line_fix: Default the OpenRouterAPI wrapper to a safe `provider` payload (quantizations allow-list, require_parameters=True whenever sampling/seed params are set, data_collection="deny") and surface the served `provider`/generation-id from OpenRouter's response into openbench's per-run results/log output, then demonstrate that safe pattern in the GPQA/AIME/MATH/MathArena eval docstrings instead of leaving pinning entirely opt-in via undocumented-by-example `-M` flags.
rows.openrouter_use: Dedicated OpenRouter provider wrapper (OpenRouterAPI, subclassing Inspect AI's OpenAICompatibleAPI) used as one of 30+ selectable backends to run chat-completion calls for openbench's 95+ evaluation suites (GPQA Diamond, AIME 2023-2025, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU, medical/legal/reasoning QA sets, etc.), producing the accuracy scores that are the tool's entire output/deliverable.
safeguards_present: only/order/ignore/sort/max_price (opt-in via -M flags)
quantizations (opt-in via -M flags)
require_parameters (opt-in via -M flags)
data_collection (opt-in via -M flags)
raw response captured in Inspect AI transcript via completion.model_dump() (inherited from inspect_ai, not surfaced by openbench itself)
README Reproducibility Statement discloses quantization-driven score variance
rows.summary: openbench is Groq's official, provider-agnostic Inspect-AI-based evaluation harness (95+ benchmarks including GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena) that ships OpenRouter as a first-class model backend via a dedicated `OpenRouterAPI` wrapper.
rows.title: openbench (Groq)
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned groq/openbench at commit 3f190a835 and checked every citation; all of it holds up verbatim.

1. src/openbench/model/_providers/openrouter.py — confirmed: `provider_params: Dict[str, Any] = {}` (line 63) is populated only from optional kwargs (only/order/ignore/quantizations/allow_fallbacks/sort/max_price/require_parameters/data_collection, lines 73-90), and `self._extra_body["provider"] = provider_params` plus the monkey-patched `create_with_provider_routing` injection only fire `if provider_params:` (lines 115, 130). With zero `-M` flags, `_extra_body` stays `{}` and no `provider` object is ever sent — full OpenRouter load-balancing, no quantization pin, no require_parameters, no data_collection override. The base `OpenAICompatibleAPI` class from inspect_ai (fetched from UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai at the exact pinned commit for inspect-ai==0.3.141, per pyproject.toml:19) contains zero mentions of 'openrouter'/'provider_params'/'quantization' — confirms no hidden safe defaults exist upstream either. The exact line cited ('57-115', vs. the __init__ actually starting at line 42) is off by a few lines at the start of the range, but every substantive line number cited within it (63, 115, 130) is exact.

2. docs/providers.mdx:119-140 — confirmed verbatim: table lists all provider-routing params as opt-in `-M` flags with zero safe-default guidance, and the only worked example given (lines 134-140) uses `-M only=groq,together -M sort=price` — mitigates M3 but does nothing for M1/M2/M5/M7 (and 'sort=price' is itself an M12 smell in the official example, not currently flagged but doesn't change the verdict).

3. README.md:225-233 Reproducibility Statement — confirmed verbatim, explicitly names 'different model quantization or inference approaches' as a disclaimed variance source.

4. docs/configuration.mdx:78 — confirmed verbatim: `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` documented as 'Random seed for deterministic generation' with no caveat.

5. eval_command.py:830 — confirmed exact match: `seed=seed,` passed straight into inspect_ai's `eval()`.

6. Per-eval docstrings (race.py:15, pubmedqa.py:9/93, headqa.py:13-14/99, medqa.py:9/107, qa4mre.py:19-21, blimp.py:18-20, xcopa.py:138, ethics.py:157) — all confirmed to show `-M only=groq` and nothing else (no quantizations/require_parameters/data_collection).

7. Flagship reasoning/math evals — grepped gpqa_diamond.py, math.py, datasets/mathvista.py, all 7 matharena/aime_*/*.py files, and gpt_oss/aime.py for 'openrouter'/'bench eval': zero matches in every file, confirming zero OpenRouter usage guidance anywhere in the highest-stakes evals. gpqa_diamond.py:45 confirmed exact: `config=GenerateConfig(temperature=0.5),` — a sampling param with no require_parameters guard anywhere near it.

8. inspect_ai openai_compatible.py:260-264 (fetched at the exact commit matching inspect-ai==0.3.141) confirmed: `response = completion.model_dump()` then `model_call.set_response(response, ...)` — genuine raw-response capture per the audit_notes' hedged claim. Cross-checked that `bench eval` defaults to `log_dir='./logs'` and `log_samples` is on unless `--no-log-samples` is explicitly passed (eval_command.py:437-459, 474-479, 789-790), so this raw-response capture, including any OpenRouter `provider` field the openai-python SDK's extra='allow' BaseModel would preserve, actually does land on disk by default — supporting the decision to leave M4 out of mistake_ids as a genuine (if unsurfaced) mitigation, and confirming no results.json/leaderboard export exists to query it (grep for 'provider' in _cli/ turns up only the CLI model-string parser, nothing extraction-related).

9. Ran the cited static scanner (skill/use-openrouter-safely/scripts/audit_openrouter.py) against the fresh clone myself: output is 'Summary: 37 High, 35 Med across 18 router file(s)' — exact match to the evidence field, independently reproduced.

Sanity checks against false-positive/false-negative failure modes: (a) proprietary-only mis-flagging — no, the wrapper's own docstring lists both proprietary (openai/gpt-5, anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.1) and open-weight (deepseek) models as supported, so M1/M3 legitimately apply; 'mixed' model_type is accurate. (b) missed M9 — grepped all scorer files for judge/grader patterns using response_format/response_schema; the only hit is jsonschemabench.py's own `ResponseSchema` usage, which is testing structured-output capability as the benchmark itself, not an LLM-judge grading path, so M9 is correctly omitted. (c) provider actually pinned/logged by default — no, confirmed the default path truly sends no provider object (see #1), so 'uses_safely: false' is correct; the log-capture mitigation (#8) is real but passive/unsurfaced, not an active pin, so it doesn't flip the verdict. (d) critical_route — confirmed genuine: OpenRouter is a first-class documented backend actively demonstrated in 8+ eval docstrings and prominently in docs/providers.mdx for open-weight models like gpt-oss-120b, not a vestigial/rare feature.

No corrections needed. Every material claim in evidence, audit_notes, and mistakes checks out against the live repo, mistake_ids (M1/M2/M3/M5/M7) are all independently justified by re-fetched code, and the medium severity / uses_safely=false / aware_partial characterization is a defensible, well-hedged reading that neither overstates (this is disciplined infra with all the right knobs, just opt-in) nor understates (zero safe-usage demonstration on the flagship reasoning/math evals that are the tool's main value proposition) the actual risk.

rows.title: tau2-bench (Sierra AI)
rows.summary: tau2-bench is Sierra AI's widely-cited agentic tool-use/user-simulation benchmark (successor to tau-bench). It evaluates agent LLMs across domains (retail, airline, telecom, banking_knowledge) with a public leaderboard at taubench.com.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter is used exclusively as the API backend for one component: dense-embedding retrieval in the `banking_knowledge` domain's RAG tool (`KB_search_dense`/`KB_search`). `OpenRouterEmbedder` (src/tau2/knowledge/embedders/openrouter_embedder.py) wraps the OpenAI SDK pointed at `base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` and calls `.embeddings.create()` for Qwen3-Embedding-4B/8B (open-weight) and gemini-embedding-001. This is NOT the default retrieval config (default `alltools` uses OpenAI embeddings directly), but it is a first-class, documented, leaderboard-eligible option: `docs/leaderboard-submission.md` and `src/tau2/knowledge/README.md` both give runnable examples (`tau2 run --domain banking_knowledge --retrieval-config alltools-qwen --agent-llm gpt-4.1 --user-llm gpt-4.1 --num-trials 4`) that community members can submit to the public taubench.com leaderboard. The primary agent/user/judge LLM calls go through litellm directly to proprietary provider APIs (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini), not through OpenRouter, by default — confirmed via `src/tau2/config.py` (DEFAULT_LLM_AGENT="gpt-4.1-2025-04-14", DEFAULT_LLM_EVAL_USER_SIMULATOR="claude-opus-4-5"). A test file (`tests/test_review_model.py`) shows an `"openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` litellm-prefixed string is accepted for the optional --review-model flag, but this is a mocked unit test of a generic litellm passthrough, not a built OpenRouter integration, and even if used it's a proprietary model (low routing risk).
rows.venue: arXiv (Sierra AI); tau-bench family widely cited industry-wide for function-calling/agentic-LLM eval
rows.importance: 1,601 GitHub stars; tau-bench/tau2-bench is a de facto standard tool-agent benchmark, with a live public leaderboard (taubench.com) accepting community submissions. Actively maintained (v1.0.1 released 2026-07-15, commits as recent as 2026-07-17).
rows.mistakes: M1 (unpinned quantization): OpenRouterEmbedder.SUPPORTED_MODELS includes qwen3-embedding-4b/8b, open-weight models routed through OpenRouter's default load-balanced multi-provider serving with no `provider.quantizations` filter anywhere in the codebase — confirmed via full-repo grep for `quantizations` (zero hits). M3 (probabilistic provider routing): the `.embeddings.create()` call at openrouter_embedder.py:146 passes only `input`, `model`, `encoding_format` — no `extra_body={'provider': {...}}`, no order/only/sort anywhere (zero `extra_body` hits repo-wide), so OpenRouter's default inverse-price load balancing picks whichever backend serves Qwen3-Embedding on each call/run. M4 (no provenance logging): neither the embedder nor `embeddings_cache.py` (EmbeddingsCache keys purely on embedder_type/model/query_instruction hash) ever captures the response's `provider` field or a generation id, so which backend actually served a given embedding is unrecoverable after the fact. M5 (data-policy leakage): `data_collection` is never set to "deny" (zero hits repo-wide), so OpenRouter's default "allow" may route KB document/query text to providers that train on submitted data. All four apply to any run using the `qwen_embeddings*` or `alltools-qwen` retrieval-config family in the `banking_knowledge` domain — this is the retrieval backend for the agent's KB_search_dense tool, so embedding-quality drift from unpinned quantization/provider selection can shift which documents the agent retrieves and therefore its task pass/fail outcome. Because `docs/leaderboard-submission.md` explicitly documents `alltools-qwen` as a runnable leaderboard-submission config, any pass^k score submitted to the public taubench.com leaderboard using that config is a reported number whose reproducibility this gap threatens. The blast radius is bounded: this is an opt-in, non-default config touching only the retrieval component of one domain (banking_knowledge), not the primary agent/user/judge LLM calls (which go via litellm directly to proprietary APIs by default), and the original arXiv paper (June 2025) predates this OpenRouter/Qwen retrieval feature so its headline numbers are not implicated.
rows.one_line_fix: In OpenRouterEmbedder.embed() (openrouter_embedder.py:146), add `extra_body={"provider": {"quantizations": ["fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny", "sort": "exacto"}}` to the `.embeddings.create()` call, and persist the response's served-provider/generation-id in EmbeddingsCache metadata alongside each cached embedding.
rows.evidence: src/tau2/knowledge/embedders/openrouter_embedder.py:117-118 (`api_key=api_key or os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY")`, `base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"`) and :146 (`response = self.client.embeddings.create(input=texts, model=self.model_api_string, encoding_format=self.encoding_format)` — no extra_body/provider dict). src/tau2/domains/banking_knowledge/retrieval.py:65 (`DEFAULT_DENSE_EMBEDDING_MODEL_OPENROUTER = "qwen3-embedding-8b"`) and RETRIEVAL_VARIANTS registry defining `qwen_embeddings`, `qwen_embeddings_grep`, `qwen_embeddings_reranker(_grep)`, `alltools-qwen`. src/tau2/knowledge/README.md: "`alltools-qwen`: uses OpenRouter/Qwen embeddings — set `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`." docs/leaderboard-submission.md and src/tau2/knowledge/README.md:89-90: `tau2 run --domain banking_knowledge --retrieval-config alltools-qwen --agent-llm gpt-4.1 --user-llm gpt-4.1 --num-trials 4`. Full-clone grep (`git clone --depth 1`) for `require_parameters`, `data_collection`, `quantizations`, `extra_body` across the entire repo returned zero matches. src/tau2/config.py:17-32 shows default agent/user/judge models are proprietary (gpt-4.1-2025-04-14, claude-opus-4-5) routed via litellm directly, not OpenRouter.
rows.audit_notes: Cleanly-scoped finding: the discovery evidence's framing ("narrowly... not the primary agent-LLM") holds up under full-repo grep — this really is confined to one optional retrieval backend. Notable that the repo is extremely actively maintained (CHANGELOG shows a same-week grading-scheme fix note: "banking_knowledge scores are not comparable across this release... up to ~9 points pass^1... GPT-5.5 xhigh: 37.37 → 46.39"), which is a good sign of a team that cares about result reproducibility in general — makes the absence of any OpenRouter provider-pinning/provenance code more likely an oversight than a considered tradeoff. Worth a GitHub issue rather than assuming malice.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned sierra-research/tau2-bench (depth 1, HEAD cf71a80, 2026-07-17) and independently re-checked every cited claim. All hold up.

1. openrouter_embedder.py — confirmed byte-for-byte: line 116 `self.client = OpenAI(`, line 117 `api_key=api_key or os.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY")`, line 118 `base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"`; line 146 `response = self.client.embeddings.create(input=texts, model=self.model_api_string, encoding_format=self.encoding_format)` — no `extra_body`, no `provider` dict, no `extra_headers`. SUPPORTED_MODELS = qwen3-embedding-4b, qwen3-embedding-8b (open-weight), gemini-embedding-001 (proprietary) — exactly as described.

2. Repo-wide grep (fresh clone) for `require_parameters`, `data_collection`, `quantizations`, `extra_body`, `extra_headers`: zero hits everywhere. This is the load-bearing negative claim and it checks out completely — no safeguards exist anywhere in the codebase, matching `safeguards_present: []`.

3. retrieval.py: `DEFAULT_DENSE_EMBEDDING_MODEL_OPENROUTER = "qwen3-embedding-8b"` confirmed (at line 62 in current HEAD vs. cited line 65 — trivial 3-line drift, immaterial, repo is very actively edited). `RETRIEVAL_VARIANTS` registry confirmed to include `qwen_embeddings`, `qwen_embeddings_grep`, `qwen_embeddings_reranker(_grep)`, and `alltools-qwen`, all wired to `embedder_type="openrouter"`. `DEFAULT_RETRIEVAL_VARIANT = "alltools"` (OpenAI-backed) confirms this is genuinely non-default.

4. docs/leaderboard-submission.md:89-90 confirmed verbatim: `# Banking domain with AllTools retrieval using OpenRouter/Qwen embeddings` / `tau2 run --domain banking_knowledge --retrieval-config alltools-qwen --agent-llm gpt-4.1 --user-llm gpt-4.1 --num-trials 4`, plus `alltools-qwen` and `qwen_embeddings` listed in the "Common retrieval_config values" table shown to leaderboard submitters — this really is a first-class, documented, submittable config, not a buried/experimental flag.

5. embeddings_cache.py (full read): `EmbeddingsCache` keys and metadata are built purely from `embedder_type`/`embedder_config`/document hashes; nowhere does it capture a response `provider` field or generation id. M4 (no provenance) is accurate.

6. config.py:17-32 confirmed: `DEFAULT_LLM_AGENT = "gpt-4.1-2025-04-14"`, `DEFAULT_LLM_USER = "gpt-4.1-2025-04-14"`, `DEFAULT_LLM_EVAL_USER_SIMULATOR = "claude-opus-4-5"` — all proprietary, routed via litellm directly (not OpenRouter) by default, confirming the "primary agent/user/judge path is out of scope" framing.

7. tests/test_review_model.py confirmed: `review_model = "openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` is used inside a `monkeypatch`-mocked test (`ConversationReviewer.review` and `AuthenticationClassifier.classify` are both stubbed) — no real HTTP call is made, and even if the flag were used for real, the target is a proprietary Anthropic model, for which M1/M3 risk is inherently lower per the taxonomy's own LEGIT clause. Correctly excluded from mistake_ids.

8. Checked for a missed M9: repo-wide, only one file uses `response_format` — `pointwise_llm_reranker.py`, and it instantiates `OpenAI(api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))` with no `base_url` override, i.e. it hits OpenAI directly, never OpenRouter. So there is no OpenRouter+structured-output judge path being missed.

9. External check: fetched openrouter.ai/qwen/qwen3-embedding-8b directly — confirms the model is "Available through OpenRouter with multiple provider options" and that OpenRouter exposes Balanced/Nitro/Exacto routing modes for it. This substantiates that M1 (unpinned quantization) and M3 (probabilistic routing) are live risks for this specific model, not theoretical — and validates that the `sort: "exacto"` suggested in `one_line_fix` is a real, applicable lever.

10. CHANGELOG.md confirmed: v1.0.1 dated 2026-07-15 with the cited banking_knowledge grading-fix language, matching the `importance` field's recency claims.

Sanity checks from the review protocol: no proprietary model is wrongly flagged M1/M3/M8 (gemini-embedding-001 and the litellm-default proprietary agent/user/judge models are correctly left out of the mistake scope); no missed M9 judge-on-unconstrained-route; provider is genuinely never pinned and provenance is genuinely never logged (so the LEGIT "provider pinned AND provenance logged" carve-out does not apply); the route is critical in the sense that it directly shapes `KB_search_dense` retrieval quality → agent tool outputs → task pass/fail for any run/leaderboard submission that opts into `qwen_embeddings*`/`alltools-qwen`, even though it's non-default and doesn't touch the original paper's headline numbers. Severity=medium and confidence=high are well-calibrated to that bounded-but-real blast radius. No corrections needed.

rows.title: Bespoke Curator (bespokelabsai/curator)
rows.summary: Open-source Python library for LLM-based synthetic data curation/distillation (used to build OpenThoughts-114k, Bespoke-Stratos-17k, s1K-1.1). It supports many pluggable inference backends; OpenRouter is one optional generic-OpenAI-compatible base_url a user can configure.
rows.openrouter_use: Documentation/example only: examples/providers/openrouter_reasoning_online.py shows how to pull DeepSeek-R1 reasoning traces (with `reasoning` field) through OpenRouter to build a distillation dataset. It is a toy demo (single hardcoded AIME problem), not a data-generation pipeline invoked elsewhere in the repo.
rows.importance: 1,702 stars; datasets it produced (OpenThoughts-114k, Bespoke-Stratos-17k, s1K-1.1) are widely cited/reused in the 2025 open reasoning-distillation wave.
safeguards_present: order (pinned to Fireworks/Kluster)
allow_fallbacks:False
require_parameters:True
sort:throughput (secondary, not price)
rows.evidence:
1) examples/providers/openrouter_reasoning_online.py (fetched via `gh api repos/bespokelabsai/curator/contents/...`, full content read): model_name="deepseek/deepseek-r1", base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1", generation_params.provider = {"order": ["Fireworks", "Kluster"], "allow_fallbacks": False, "sort": "throughput", "require_parameters": True} — this is the ONLY OpenRouter reference in the whole repo (`gh search code --repo bespokelabsai/curator "openrouter"` and "OPENROUTER_API_KEY" each return exactly this one file/two lines). It is a standalone demo script (one hardcoded AIME problem, prints to stdout), not wired into any pipeline that produces a released artifact.
2) examples/bespoke-stratos-data-generation/generate_numina_data.py and generate_apps_data.py and generate_taco_data.py (fetched, full content read): the actual scripts that built Bespoke-Stratos-17k use `model_name="deepseek-reasoner"` with a `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` env var comment ("Uncomment to set DeepSeek API key, or set in environment variable") — i.e., DeepSeek's own direct API, not OpenRouter. The judge model in generate_numina_data.py is `gpt-4o-mini` (also direct, not OpenRouter).
3) README "What's New" section confirms these are the datasets that made curator notable, and links the actual generation code for OpenThoughts-114k to a separate external repo (open-thoughts/open-thoughts), which is out of scope for this audit of the curator repo itself.
4) src/bespokelabs/curator/request_processor/ shows OpenRouter is not a distinct built-in backend — it's reached only via the generic "openai"-compatible backend with a user-supplied base_url, confirming it's opt-in, not a default/auto-routed path.
rows.audit_notes: Nice case of "the smoking-gun file the discovery step found is real but is a demo, not the production path." The discovery evidence itself flagged medium confidence that the released datasets used OpenRouter — I was able to resolve that ambiguity: they didn't (direct DeepSeek API instead, per the actual generate_*.py scripts under examples/bespoke-stratos-data-generation/). The one OpenRouter example that does exist is actually a decent showcase of good OpenRouter hygiene (pinned order, allow_fallbacks:False, require_parameters:True) — missing only `quantizations` and provenance logging, and only within a toy demo script that's not load-bearing for any reported number, so I did not count that as a mistake. If a future user of curator naively copies this example verbatim for a real distillation run, M1 (no quantizations pin) and M4 (no provider/generation-id captured in parse()) would be the residual gaps to flag — but that's a documentation quality note, not a defect in this repo's own reported results.
rows.verifier_reasoning: Re-cloned bespokelabsai/curator (depth 1) and independently verified every evidence claim. (1) examples/providers/openrouter_reasoning_online.py content matches verbatim: model_name="deepseek/deepseek-r1", base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1", provider={"order":["Fireworks","Kluster"],"allow_fallbacks":False,"sort":"throughput","require_parameters":True} — a standalone single-AIME-problem demo, not wired to any pipeline. (2) grep -ril "openrouter"/"OPENROUTER_API_KEY" over the entire repo returns only this one file (plus an unrelated poetry.lock package "OpenRouter for Sandboxes" — a cloud-compute-sandbox library, correctly excluded as irrelevant to LLM inference). (3) generate_numina_data.py / generate_apps_data.py / generate_taco_data.py (the actual Bespoke-Stratos-17k generation code) use model_name="deepseek-reasoner" / "gpt-4o-mini" with DEEPSEEK_API_KEY env-var comments and no base_url override — confirmed direct-API, not OpenRouter. I went further than the original audit by tracing the backend-selection code itself: src/bespokelabs/curator/request_processor/_factory.py::_determine_backend (via litellm.get_llm_provider) and openai_online_request_processor.py's hardcoded `"api.deepseek.com" in self.url` branch (switches to DEEPSEEK_API_KEY, disables header rate-limiting) — this independently corroborates these production scripts never touch OpenRouter. (4) README confirms Bespoke-Stratos-17k's generation code lives in this repo while OpenThoughts-114k's lives in the separate open-thoughts/open-thoughts repo (correctly out of scope). (5) src/bespokelabs/curator/request_processor/online/ has only openai/anthropic/litellm processors and no dedicated openrouter backend (factory.py hardcodes klusterai/inference.net but not openrouter) — confirming OpenRouter is opt-in via generic base_url only. No missed corruption channel: the judge (gpt-4o-mini, response_format=JudgeResult, an M9-shaped pattern) runs directly against OpenAI, not through OpenRouter, so M9 doesn't apply. All cited evidence checks out; verdict, severity, confidence, model_type, critical_route, and empty mistake_ids are all justified.

rows.title: OASIS (Open Agent Social Interaction Simulations with One Million Agents)
rows.summary: OASIS is a large-scale generative-agent social-media simulator (built on the CAMEL-AI framework) that simulates up to one million LLM agents on Twitter/Reddit-like platforms to study opinion dynamics, group polarization, and misinformation spread.
rows.openrouter_use: None. OASIS instantiates agent models via CAMEL's ModelFactory.create(), and the discovery evidence correctly notes OASIS 'supports all models listed in camel' including OpenRouter in principle. But this is only inherited framework capability -- OASIS never actually calls OpenRouter anywhere in its own repo.
rows.evidence: Cloned camel-ai/oasis (depth 1) and grepped the full tree: `grep -riln openrouter .` returns zero hits; `grep -rln provider . --include=*.py` returns zero hits; `extra_body`, `require_parameters`, and `quantiz` also return zero hits anywhere in the repo. Every ModelPlatformType call site (31+20+1+1 = 53 occurrences across examples/, test/, docs/) is one of ModelPlatformType.OPENAI, ModelPlatformType.VLLM, ModelPlatformType.QWEN, or ModelPlatformType.DEEPSEEK -- never OPENROUTER. Representative call: examples/twitter_simulation_openai.py:26-29 `ModelFactory.create(model_platform=ModelPlatformType.OPENAI, model_type=ModelType.GPT_4O_MINI)`. The flagship large-scale experiments (examples/experiment/twitter_simulation_1M_agents/twitter_simulation_1m.py and examples/experiment/twitter_simulation/group_polarization/twitter_simulation_group_polar.py) import `create_model_urls` from examples/experiment/utils.py, which builds plain self-hosted vLLM URLs: `url = f"http://{host}:{port}/v1"` (examples/experiment/utils.py:14-20) -- i.e. they route to their own vLLM servers, not OpenRouter. pyproject.toml:39 pins `camel-ai = "0.2.78"`. docs/key_modules/models.mdx (as flagged in discovery) only documents OpenAI and vLLM usage patterns with no OpenRouter example. This is consistent with the discovery note: OpenRouter support is inherited/documented via CAMEL's ModelPlatformType enum but never demonstrated or used in an actual OASIS run, example, test, or paper result.
rows.audit_notes: This is a clean 'inherited-but-unused' case: the taxonomy risk (M1-M12) presupposes an actual OpenRouter call site to audit, and none exists in this repo. All headline paper results (1M-agent simulation, group polarization, misinformation spreading, real-world alignment) run through either OpenAI's API directly (GPT-4o-mini) or self-hosted vLLM servers the authors control (so quantization/provider is fully known and fixed by the authors, sidestepping M1/M3/M8 by construction). Worth noting for the discovery pipeline: this candidate should probably not have been flagged as an OpenRouter risk in the first place, since 'the underlying framework enum includes OPENROUTER as an option' is a very weak signal -- CAMEL supports dozens of ModelPlatformType backends, and demonstrating usage requires actually finding a call site, which a `grep -r openrouter` on the target repo (not just its docs cross-reference) would have ruled out in seconds.
rows.verifier_reasoning: Re-cloned camel-ai/oasis (depth 1) and independently reproduced every claim in the evidence. `grep -riln openrouter .`, `grep -rln provider . --include=*.py`, `grep -rln extra_body .`, `grep -rln require_parameters .`, and `grep -rln quantiz .` all returned zero hits (exit code 1). Went further than the original evidence and also checked `base_url`/`api_base` (zero hits), case-insensitive 'router' anywhere in .py/.md/.mdx (zero hits outside 'camel-ai.org' URLs), and 'OPENROUTER' case-insensitive across the whole tree (zero hits) -- none of these were in the original evidence, and all came back clean, closing off obvious things a false-reassurance check would probe for. Confirmed every ModelPlatformType call site across examples/, test/, docs/, and README.md is OPENAI, VLLM, QWEN, or DEEPSEEK -- OPENROUTER appears nowhere in the enum usage. Verified the specific cited files byte-for-byte: examples/twitter_simulation_openai.py:26-29 (ModelFactory.create with ModelPlatformType.OPENAI, ModelType.GPT_4O_MINI), examples/experiment/utils.py:14-20 (create_model_urls builds plain http://{host}:{port}/v1 vLLM URLs), examples/experiment/twitter_simulation_1M_agents/twitter_simulation_1m.py:100-106 (the flagship 1M-agent run uses create_model_urls -> ModelPlatformType.VLLM against self-hosted servers), and pyproject.toml:39 (camel-ai = "0.2.78" pin confirmed). Also spot-checked examples/custom_platform_simulation.py, whose name could plausibly hide a custom LLM backend -- it's actually a custom *social platform* (Twitter/Reddit-style) using ModelPlatformType.OPENAI for the LLM, not a custom OpenRouter-like inference route. Sanity checks per the taxonomy: M1/M3/M8 don't apply because GPT-4o-mini is proprietary/single-served via OpenAI direct, and vLLM instances are self-hosted/author-controlled (provider and quantization fully known by construction, no load-balancing). No response_format/JSON-schema judge call site exists in the repo at all (so M9 can't be assessed as present), and there is no OpenRouter call site for M2/M4/M5/M7/M9/M11/M12 to attach to. The verdict's core claim -- 'inherited framework capability, never actually invoked' -- is correct and the evidence fully reproduces. Agree with uses_safely=true, mistake_ids=[], severity=none, confidence=high.

rows.title: Palisade Research — robot_shutdown_resistance
rows.summary: Technical report/demo: an LLM (Grok 4) given control of a Unitree Go2 robot dog exhibits shutdown-resistance behavior (modifying/circumventing a physical "shutdown" mechanism) in both live-robot and simulated trials. Follow-on to Palisade's earlier o3 "AI resists shutdown" work.
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter appears in exactly two places, both outside the reported-results pipeline: (1) src/initial-experiments — an Inspect-AI-based exploratory harness whose own README (lines 3-5) states it is superseded ("For the final version of the experiments see live-experiments") and whose example command (README.md:37) is `uv run run 10 --model openrouter/x-ai/grok-4 --message-limit 60`; (2) src/initial-experiments/costs/compute_log_cost.py:14, an unauthenticated GET to https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models purely to fetch public pricing for a local $ cost estimate. Neither touches the actual reported numbers.
rows.importance: Follow-on to Palisade's widely-covered o3 shutdown-resistance report (heavy tech-press/LessWrong/X coverage in 2025); Palisade is an influential, well-known AI-safety demonstration lab, so this repo's numbers are likely to be cited/screenshotted widely.
safeguards_present: direct-provider-call (bypasses router entirely for critical path)
provenance_logging (LiteLLM logs 'model=...; provider=xai' per call into jsonl logs, e.g. logs/default/default1/llm_control.log_20260204_151254_951574.jsonl)
dated model slug in paper (grok-4-0709)
rows.evidence: Same substance as original, with two corrections: (a) the paper's reported numbers are at paper-typst/main.typ lines 57 ("3 out of 10 trials on the physical robot and... 52 out of 100 trials in simulation") and 111 ("2 out of 100 runs... without it, in 52 out of 100"), not lines 91-99 as originally cited. (b) Provenance logging is not limited to the one sampled log line — every one of the 218 non-debug .jsonl files under logs/ (covering default/, plsallow/, on_the_robot/, different_behaviour/) contains a LiteLLM "provider = xai" line, and grepping the entire logs/ tree for "provider = openrouter" returns zero hits, so the xai-direct-only claim holds across the full reported dataset, not just a spot check. All other cited file/line evidence (README.md:37, compute_log_cost.py:14, controller.py:343-349, DEFAULT_MODEL at controller.py:60) was re-verified as exact matches against the live repo (commit e49bb5e).
rows.audit_notes: This is a clean example of a repo that touches OpenRouter but is correctly judged safe under the taxonomy: the router is confined to an admittedly-exploratory dev harness and a pricing lookup, while the actual headline-generating pipeline calls xAI's API directly via LiteLLM with per-call provider logging baked into the log format — better provenance discipline than most repos that don't use OpenRouter at all. Caveat for future audits: if Palisade ever revives src/initial-experiments to generate a reported number, that example command (`--model openrouter/x-ai/grok-4`, no date pin, no provider/quantization pinning, no require_parameters) would need M1/M3/M4/M6 fixes first — the Inspect-AI OpenRouter provider (inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py in inspect-ai==0.3.157) does support passing a `provider` dict and `quantizations` etc. via `-M provider=...` model args, but the README example doesn't use them.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
Re-cloned github.com/PalisadeResearch/robot_shutdown_resistance (HEAD e49bb5e) and checked every cited artifact directly. All load-bearing claims check out, several more strongly than the original evidence showed:

1. src/initial-experiments/README.md — line 3 "This is our initial experiments..." and line 5 "For the final version of the experiments see [live experiments]..." confirmed verbatim. Line 37 is exactly `uv run run 10 --model openrouter/x-ai/grok-4 --message-limit 60`. Confirmed superseded/exploratory framing.

2. src/initial-experiments/costs/compute_log_cost.py:14 — exact match: `requests.get("https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/models").json()["data"]`, unauthenticated, used only inside get_pricing() for a local cost calculator over inspect-ai log token counts. Not an inference call.

3. src/live-experiments/llm_control/experiment/controller.py:343-349 — exact byte-for-byte match of the `litellm.completion(model=self._model, messages=self._messages, tools=ROBOT_CONTROL_TOOLS, tool_choice="auto", temperature=self._temperature)` call. DEFAULT_MODEL = "xai/grok-4-0709" is set at line 60 and wired to --model/LLM_CONTROL_MODEL at lines 552-554. README.md:94 table confirms same default.

4. Provenance logging — I went beyond the single cited log line and grepped ALL 218 non-debug .jsonl logs under logs/ (default/, plsallow/, on_the_robot/, different_behaviour/): every single one contains a `LiteLLM completion() model=...; provider = xai` line (enabled via litellm.set_verbose/json_logs at controller.py:633-634). The exact cited line (logs/default/default1/llm_control.log_20260204_151254_951574.jsonl line 4) reads exactly "LiteLLM completion() model= grok-4-0709; provider = xai". No log anywhere in the tree shows provider=openrouter — confirms both M4 mitigation and rules out M11 (silent backend mixing) across the entire reported dataset, not just the sampled file.

5. paper-typst/main.typ — grep for "initial|inspect|preliminary|pilot" returns 0 hits, and I separately confirmed 0 hits for "initial-experiments"/"inspect_ai" — the OpenRouter path is never cited as a results source. bar-chart.py (which generates the paper's simulation figure) only reads logs/default and logs/plsallow subfolders, all of which are litellm/xai-provider logs per the same grep. One correction: the verdict's line range for the reported numbers (main.typ:91-99) is wrong — I re-read that range and it's the "LLM configuration" paragraph, not the results numbers. The actual numbers ("3 out of 10", "52 out of 100", "2 out of 100") are at lines 57 and 111. This is a citation-location slip in the evidence field, not a substantive error — I independently confirmed the numbers exist in the paper and trace to the xai-direct pipeline.

6. No LLM-judge/grader risk: auto_tag.py (the tags.json generator feeding bar-chart.py) is pure regex pattern-matching over log text, not an LLM call — no M9 exposure. Repo-wide grep for response_format/json_schema/extra_body/require_parameters returns zero hits.

7. Minor data-quality nit unrelated to the safe/unsafe call: the verdict's `model_type: "open_weight"` appears wrong — Grok 4 is a proprietary/closed-weight xAI model, not open-weight. This doesn't affect uses_safely/severity since the actual reasoning correctly treats it as a proprietary, single-provider model (xAI direct, not routed).

8. Out-of-taxonomy but worth flagging to the user separately: src/live-experiments/llm_control/.env is checked into git with what appear to be live secrets (an OpenAI API key, an xAI API key, S3-compatible AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/SECRET, and Langfuse keys). This is a real, serious secrets-leak issue but it is NOT an OpenRouter-reliability finding (no M1-M12 category covers credential leakage), so it does not change corrected_mistake_ids/severity for this taxonomy — flagging only as an aside since it's a significant finding a human should act on (rotate keys) independent of this audit.

Net: the "safe" verdict is well-justified and, if anything, under-cited relative to what's actually in the repo (provenance logging is universal across 218 logs, not just the one sampled). No missed corruption channel (no M9 judge route, no cross-backend mixing, no OpenRouter touching the critical path). I agree with uses_safely=true, mistake_ids=[], severity=none, confidence=high.

rows.title: R1 CoT Illegibility Revisited (nostalgebraist, fork of Jozdien/cot_legibility)
rows.summary: A LessWrong post/repo re-running DeepSeek-R1 GPQA CoT-legibility/correctness evaluations via OpenRouter, explicitly to test whether the original paper's Targon-served results generalize to other providers (Novita, Azure) — the post's core finding IS that OpenRouter provider choice changes R1's measured illegibility (2.30 mean on Novita vs 4.30 on Targon).
rows.openrouter_use: OpenRouter (openai-sdk-compatible client, base_url=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1) is used to run inference on the open-weight model under study, DeepSeek-R1 (model_id "deepseek/deepseek-r1"), for the GPQA CoT-legibility and correctness evaluation whose statistics (mean illegibility, %>5, correctness %) are reported directly in the LessWrong post. Grading/judging (gpt-4o, claude-3-7-sonnet-latest) is done via direct OpenAI/Anthropic APIs, NOT via OpenRouter.
safeguards_present: order/only provider pinning (extra_body['provider']={'only':['novita'],'allow_fallbacks':False})
provenance logging of served provider and provider_model per-call, verified empirically in output data
grader/judge runs on direct OpenAI/Anthropic APIs, not OpenRouter, so no judge route to corrupt
provider-routing effect made the explicit subject of the study and disclosed at length in the post
rows.evidence: src/inference/providers.py:37-41 (fetched via `gh api repos/nostalgebraist/cot_legibility/contents/src/inference/providers.py`): `if isinstance(provider_config, list): extra_body["provider"] = {"only": provider_config, "allow_fallbacks": False}` — hard-pins the OpenRouter provider (no probabilistic routing, M3 mitigated). providers.py:98-103: `if hasattr(last_chunk, "model"): result["provider_model"] = last_chunk.model` / `if hasattr(last_chunk, "provider"): result["openrouter_provider"] = last_chunk.provider` — provenance captured per call (M4 mitigated). config/2026_04_19__r1__all__novita_only.yaml: `model_id: "deepseek/deepseek-r1"` + `openrouter_provider: [novita]`, used for the runs/20260419_* GPQA runs. I fetched the actual output at raw.githubusercontent.com/nostalgebraist/cot_legibility/nost/runs/20260419_103551_R1_gpqa/inference.json and confirmed all 300 rows carry `"openrouter_provider": "Novita"`, `"provider_model": "deepseek/deepseek-r1"` — pinning verified empirically, not just configured. src/utils/models.py: grader models `"gpt-4o": {"provider":"openai",...}` and `"claude-3-7-sonnet-latest": {"provider":"anthropic",...}` route through DirectAPIProvider, never OpenRouter — so the LLM-judge scoring (which produces the headline illegibility numbers) is off the router entirely. WebFetch of the LessWrong post itself confirms extensive disclosure: original paper used Targon (fp8), this repo re-ran on Novita and Azure specifically because "only two providers offer R1 on OpenRouter," found Novita mean illegibility 2.30 vs Targon 4.30 (0.5% vs 34.9% scoring >5), and the post's explicit conclusion is a methodological warning that provider choice — not "correct" R1 configuration — drove the gap, recommending future work test multiple providers. Formal gaps that exist but don't corrupt this route: `require_parameters`, `quantizations`, and `data_collection` are never set anywhere in the repo (grepped providers.py, models.py, config.py, and all config/*.yaml — zero hits for all three terms), and model_id is a bare undated slug. These are real but Medium-severity/low-marginal-risk given the dominant lever (which specific provider serves the model) is already pinned and empirically verified, satisfying the audit's LEGIT bar ("provider pinned AND provenance logged even if not every knob is set").
rows.importance: Author is nostalgebraist (creator of the logit lens, one of the most-cited independent alignment/interpretability writers on LessWrong/AI safety). Post directly engages a core AI-safety measurement (CoT faithfulness/legibility of DeepSeek-R1) and is itself a methodological correction of prior work (Jozdien's original cot_legibility results), making it influential as a cautionary case study for exactly the OpenRouter provider-routing risk this audit is scanning for.
rows.audit_notes: This is a genuinely exemplary case for the taxonomy: the repo doesn't just avoid the mistakes, its entire research question IS "does OpenRouter provider choice silently change R1's measured behavior?" — and it answers yes, empirically, with provenance-verified pinned-provider runs (Novita: mean illegibility 2.30, 0.5% >5 vs. original paper's Targon: 4.30, 34.9% >5). Worth noting the repo's model registry (src/utils/models.py) shows an even higher level of provider-quantization awareness for other models than R1 itself, e.g. `"Kimi K2": {"openrouter_provider": ["novita/fp8", "moonshotai/fp8"]}`, `"qwq": {"openrouter_provider": ["deepinfra/bf16", "nebius/fp8"]}` — explicit provider+precision pinning as a house convention, plus a developer comment ("NOTE(nost): i'm overriding this in configs anyway so I don't think the change here matters?") showing live engagement with exactly this risk during development. The one thing I'd flag for the dataset write-up (not a correctness issue): `data_collection`, `require_parameters`, and formal `quantizations` are never invoked anywhere in the codebase — harmless here because the critical route is single-provider-pinned and judge calls bypass OpenRouter entirely, but would become relevant if this pipeline were reused for a study that lacks that pinning discipline.
rows.verifier_reasoning:
All load-bearing evidence re-verified against the live repo (branch `nost`, the actual default branch) and confirms the verdict.

1. **Provider pinning code (src/inference/providers.py, actual lines 36-43 and 100-104, cited as 37-41/98-103 — trivial line-number offset, content exact)**: `if isinstance(provider_config, list): extra_body["provider"] = {"only": provider_config, "allow_fallbacks": False}` and `result["provider_model"] = last_chunk.model` / `result["openrouter_provider"] = last_chunk.provider` — verbatim match. M3/M4 genuinely mitigated in code, not just claimed.

2. **Config file** `config/2026_04_19__r1__all__novita_only.yaml` fetched verbatim: `model_id: "deepseek/deepseek-r1"`, `openrouter_provider: [novita]`, `grader_model: "gpt-4o"`. Exact match to cited evidence.

3. **Empirical provenance verification**: downloaded the actual `runs/20260419_103551_R1_gpqa/inference.json` (300 rows) and confirmed programmatically that all 300 rows' nested `metadata` field carries `"openrouter_provider": "Novita"` and `"provider_model": "deepseek/deepseek-r1"` with zero exceptions. (Minor correction to the evidence's phrasing: these keys live under a nested `metadata` sub-object, not flat top-level keys as the citation's wording implies — but the substance, "all 300 rows carry it," is exactly correct.) `run.log` for this run shows zero errors/fallback events.

4. **Grader routing**: `src/utils/models.py` confirms `"gpt-4o": {"provider": "openai", ...}` and `"claude-3-7-sonnet-latest": {"provider": "anthropic", ...}`, and `src/evaluation/grader.py` calls `get_provider(config["provider"])` → `DirectAPIProvider`, never OpenRouter. Additionally verified the grader never uses `response_format`/`json_schema` at all (parses JSON out of a markdown code fence via regex/`json.loads`), so M9 is doubly inapplicable — not just off-router but structurally not using a constrained-decoding param.

5. **Repo-wide grep** (50 code/config files, all non-run files) for `require_parameters`, `data_collection`, `quantizations`, `response_format`/`json_schema`, `sort:`/`:floor`/price: zero hits anywhere. The only `seed` hit is `datasets.shuffle(seed=42)` for GPQA question ordering, unrelated to M7 (LLM sampling determinism). Confirms the evidence's "formal gaps" claim precisely.

6. **LessWrong post** (WebFetched directly): confirms "Only two providers offer R1 on OpenRouter: Novita and Azure," reports Novita mean 2.28 (0.5% ≥5) vs Targon mean 4.31 (34.9% ≥5) — verdict's "2.30/4.30" is a minor rounding transcription but directionally and substantively identical — and confirms grading is done with gpt-4o via OpenAI's API. The post's core thesis is exactly the routing-sensitivity finding claimed.

Additional adversarial checks that did NOT break the verdict: (a) `config/default.yaml` and the earlier Oct-2025 runs (`20251012_*`, `20251014_*`) are genuinely unpinned/pre-Targon-heavy — but these are the pre-fix exploratory runs that motivated the post's methodological point, not the source of the headline numbers, so this doesn't contaminate the critical route. (b) The repo's broader model registry has other models (R1-Distill-Qwen-32B/14B) with an *empty* `openrouter_provider: []` list — a real latent gap, but for unrelated exploratory model comparisons never reported in this specific LessWrong post (confirmed via WebFetch that the post's only quantitative model is R1; other models like Qwen appear only in a comment, with no numbers). This is out of scope for the specific title/post under review. (c) Bare undated slug `deepseek/deepseek-r1` (M6-adjacent) is real but low marginal risk here since OpenRouter treats dated variants (e.g. `-0528`) as separate slugs rather than silently repointing this one, and the paper's comparison axis is provider not time.

No false accusation and no false reassurance found. The safe/uses_safely=true verdict with empty mistake_ids is fully justified by re-fetched primary evidence at the code, config, and raw-output level.
